Existing efficiency methods using sliding window subsampling cause instability and fail to achieve full trajectory performance, motivating the need for a more efficient and stable training approach.

Method: Flash-GRPO proposes two key components: (1) iso-temporal grouping, which enforces prompt-wise temporal consistency to decouple policy performance from timestep difficulty; (2) temporal gradient rectification, which neutralizes time-dependent scaling factors causing inconsistent gradient magnitudes across timesteps.

Result: Experiments on 1.3B to 14B parameter models show Flash-GRPO achieves substantial training acceleration with consistent stability and state-of-the-art alignment quality, outperforming full trajectory training under low computational budgets.

Conclusion: Flash-GRPO effectively overcomes the computational bottleneck of GRPO for video diffusion model alignment, proving that single-step training can surpass full trajectory methods in both efficiency and alignment quality.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization has emerged as essential for aligning video diffusion models with human preferences, but faces a critical computational bottleneck: training a 14B parametered model typically demands hundreds of GPU days per experiment. Existing efficiency methods reduce costs through sliding window subsampling training timesteps, but fundamentally compromise optimization, exhibiting severe instability and failing to reach full trajectory performance. We present Flash-GRPO, a single-step training framework that outperforms full trajectory training in alignment quality under low computational budgets while substantially improving training efficiency. Flash-GRPO addresses two critical challenges: iso-temporal grouping eliminates timestep-confounded variance by enforcing prompt-wise temporal consistency, decoupling policy performance from timestep difficulty; temporal gradient rectification neutralizes the time-dependent scaling factor that causes vastly inconsistent gradient magnitudes across timesteps. Experiments on 1.3B to 14B parameter models validate Flash-GRPO's effectiveness, demonstrating substantial training acceleration with consistent stability and state-of-the-art alignment quality.

</details>


### [83] [Segmentation, Detection and Explanation: A Unified Framework for CT Appearance Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15997)
*Yuyuan Liu,Can Peng,Yingyu Yang,Qianye Yang,Cheng Ouyang,J. Alison Noble*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a unified autoregressive framework for CT image analysis that integrates language-guided visual reasoning, task-routing tokens, and a coarse-to-fine "closer-look" mechanism to jointly perform detection, segmentation, and textual reasoning, outperforming SoTA on BTCV and MosMed+.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning advances in CT segmentation are limited to image-level pattern recognition without explicit anatomical or contextual reasoning. Large vision-language models focus on single tasks, insufficient for clinical workflows requiring multiple fine-grained analyses like anatomy detection and segmentation.

Method: A unified autoregressive framework with: (1) task-routing tokens that trigger detection and segmentation heads conditioned on hidden states of a large vision-language model; (2) a "closer-look" mechanism enabling progressive coarse-to-fine region-of-interest visits under refined fields of view; (3) a curated multimodal CT dataset with pixel-wise masks, bounding boxes, spatial prompts, and structured descriptions via AI-assisted annotation.

Result: Achieves up to 1.0% Dice improvement on BTCV and 1.7% Dice on MosMed+ over SoTA, while additionally generating appearance reasoning outputs.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively unifies multiple CT analysis tasks (detection, segmentation, reasoning) in a coherent autoregressive manner, outperforming prior specialized methods and enabling richer clinical interpretation beyond pure segmentation.

Abstract: Recent progress in deep learning has significantly advanced CT image analysis, particularly for segmentation tasks. However, these advances are largely confined to image-level pattern recognition, with most methods lacking explicit anatomical or contextual reasoning. Large vision-language models introduce linguistic context into image analysis, yet most approaches typically focus on a single task, which is insufficient for clinical workflow analysis that requires multiple fine-grained types of analysis, such as anatomy detection and segmentation. In this paper, we propose a unified autoregressive framework that integrates language-guided visual reasoning into CT interpretation. Our method introduces task-routing tokens that trigger detection and segmentation heads conditioned on the hidden states of a large vision-language model, enabling coherent generation of visual outputs (e.g., masks and bounding boxes) and textual reasonings. To progressively enhance localisation accuracy and semantic clarity, we further design a "closer-look" mechanism that allows the model to perform progressive coarse-to-fine visits to regions of interest under refined fields of view. To support model training and evaluation, we curated a new multimodal CT dataset containing pixel-wise masks, bounding boxes, spatial prompts, and structured descriptions for visual objects constructed through an AI-assisted annotation process with human verification. Experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over the SoTA, achieving up to 1.0% Dice on BTCV and 1.7% Dice on MosMed+, while additionally providing appearance reasoning outputs. The code and dataset will be available.

</details>


### [84] [Echo-Forcing: A Scene Memory Framework for Interactive Long Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16003)
*Mingqiang Wu,Weilun Feng,Zhefeng Zhang,Haotong Qin,Yuqi Li,Guoxin Fan,Xiaokun Liu,Zhulin An,Libo Huang,Yongjun Xu,Chuanguang Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Echo-Forcing is a training-free memory framework for interactive long video generation that decouples historical KV states into stable anchors, compressed history, and recent windows, enabling smooth transitions, hard cuts, and long-range scene recall under bounded cache.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing training-free long-video optimization methods handle stable extension under a single prompt but fail in interactive scenarios involving prompt switching, old scene forgetting, and historical scene recall. The core bottleneck is the functional entanglement of historical KV states where stable anchors and recent dynamics share the same cache policy.

Method: Three core mechanisms: (1) Hierarchical Temporal Memory - decouples stable anchors, compressed history, and recent windows under relative RoPE; (2) Scene Recall Frames - compresses historical scenes into spatially structured KV representations; (3) Difference-aware Memory Decay - adaptively forgets conflicting tokens based on discrepancy between old and new scenes.

Result: Achieves best overall performance on VBench-Long in both long-video generation and interactive video generation settings.

Conclusion: Echo-Forcing uniformly supports smooth transitions, hard cuts, and long-range scene recall under bounded cache budget, effectively addressing interactive long video generation challenges.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion models enable open-ended generation through local attention and KV caching. However, existing training-free long-video optimization methods mainly focus on stable extension under a single prompt, making them difficult to handle interactive scenarios involving prompt switching, old scene forgetting, and historical scene recall. We identify the core bottleneck as the functional entanglement of historical KV states: stable anchors and recent dynamics are handled by the same cache policy, leading to outdated background contamination, delayed response to new prompts, and loss of long-range memory. To address this issue, we propose Echo-Forcing, a training-free scene memory framework specifically designed for interactive long video generation with three core mechanisms: (1) Hierarchical Temporal Memory, which decouples stable anchors, compressed history, and recent windows under relative RoPE; (2) Scene Recall Frames, which compresses historical scenes into spatially structured KV representations to support long-term recall; and (3) Difference-aware Memory Decay, which adaptively forgets conflicting tokens according to the discrepancy between old and new scenes. Based on these designs, Echo-Forcing uniformly supports smooth transitions, hard cuts, and long-range scene recall under a bounded cache budget. Extensive evaluations on VBench-Long further demonstrate that Echo-Forcing achieves the best overall performance in both long-video generation and interactive video generation settings. Our code is released in https://github.com/mingqiangWu/Echo-Forcing

</details>


### [85] [End-to-end plaque counting and virus titration from laboratory plate images with deep learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16008)
*Eugenia Moris,Alicia Costábile,Sebastián Rey,Irene Ferreiro,Joaquín Hurtado,Lizandra Lissette Luciano,Matías Villagrán,Aisha Espino Vázquez,Jomari Ramos,Isadora Monteiro,María Victoria de Santiago,Pilar Moreno,Gonzalo Moratorio,José Ignacio Orlando*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A computer-aided workflow combining SAM2-based well segmentation and SAM-based plaque segmentation for automated virus titration from plaque assay images, achieving strong agreement with manual annotations (Pearson r=0.92 for MAYV/CVB3, r=0.88 for VACV).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Plaque assays are the gold standard for virus infectivity measurement, but manual plaque counting is labor-intensive, time-consuming, and subject to inter-operator variability.

Method: An end-to-end pipeline integrating two models from the Segment Anything Model (SAM): a SAM2-based well-segmentation module for localizing wells across heterogeneous imaging conditions, and a SAM-based plaque-segmentation model for detecting and counting plaques within each well. The system outputs per-well PFU/mL and is deployed in a web-based platform.

Result: Tested on Mayaro virus, Coxsackievirus B3, and Vaccinia virus images across 6-well and 12-well plates. Achieved Pearson correlation of 0.92 (MAYV/CVB3) and 0.88 (VACV) against manual annotations, with high concordance compared to four independent expert annotations.

Conclusion: The proposed open-source workflow enables reproducible, scalable, and audit-ready plaque assay analysis while substantially reducing manual annotation effort.

Abstract: Plaque assays remain the gold standard readout of virus infectivity; however, plaque counting from plate images is labor-intensive and prone to inter-operator variability. We present an end-to-end, computer-aided workflow for cytopathic effect-based virus titration directly from laboratory plaque assay images. The proposed approach combines two models derived from the Segment Anything Model (SAM): a SAM2-based well-segmentation module that localizes assay wells across heterogeneous imaging conditions, and a SAM-based plaque-segmentation model that detects and enumerates plaques within each well. The method was evaluated on a mixed dataset comprising private plaque assay images of Mayaro virus and Coxsackievirus B3, together with public Vaccinia virus images from the VACVPlaque dataset. The pipeline outputs per-well plaque counts, automatically computes plaque-forming units per milliliter (PFU/mL), and is integrated into a web-based platform that allows users to review results and organize experiments. On held-out plates (17 from MAYV/CVB3 and 22 from VACV), the workflow generalized across two plate formats (6-well and 12-well) and showed strong agreement with manual annotations (Pearson correlation coefficients of 0.92 for MAYV/CVB3 and 0.88 for VACV). Automated plaque counts were further compared with annotations from four independent experts, demonstrating high concordance. The proposed system will be open sourced and publicly released upon acceptance of this manuscript to enable reproducible, scalable, and audit-ready plaque assay analysis while substantially reducing manual annotation effort.

</details>


### [86] [EndoGSim: Physics-Aware 4D Dynamic Endoscopic Scene Simulations via MLLM-Guided Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16022)
*Changjing Liu,Yiming Huang,Long Bai,Beilei Cui,Hongliang Ren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified framework using MLLM-guided 4D Gaussian Splatting for physics-aware reconstruction and simulation of deformable tissues/tools in endoscopic surgery, with automatic physical property inference via differentiable MPM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods focus on visual reconstruction but lack physics-based descriptions needed for realistic simulation in robot-assisted surgery.

Method: Integrates 4D Gaussian Splatting with segmentation/depth estimation; introduces object-wise material field initialized by MLLM and refined via differentiable MPM under joint image and optical flow supervision.

Result: Achieves superior simulation fidelity and physical accuracy over state-of-the-art methods on open-source and in-house datasets.

Conclusion: The framework shows strong potential for advancing robot-assisted surgical applications through physics-aware reconstruction and simulation.

Abstract: In robot-assisted minimally invasive surgery, high-fidelity dynamic endoscopic scene reconstruction and simulation are crucial to enhancing downstream tasks and advancing surgical outcomes. However, existing methods primarily focus on visual reconstruction, lacking physics-based descriptions of the scene required for realistic simulation. We propose a unified framework that achieves physics-aware reconstruction and physical simulation of endoscopic scenes through Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs)-guided Gaussian Splatting. Our approach utilizes 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) integrated with pre-trained segmentation and depth estimation to represent deformable tissues and tools. To achieve automatic inference of physical properties, we introduce an object-wise material field that initializes material parameters via MLLM and refines them through a differentiable Material Point Method (MPM) under joint supervision from rendered images and optical flow. Validated on both open-source and in-house datasets, our framework achieves superior simulation fidelity and physical accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods, underscoring its potential to advance robot-assisted surgical applications.

</details>


### [87] [Robust Prior-Guided Segmentation for Editable 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16065)
*Raushan Joshi,Jean-Yves Guillemaut*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A framework combining 3D Gaussian Splatting with SAM-HQ for accurate 3D segmentation and real-time object editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D-GS lacks robust segmentation for editing tasks; existing 2D-to-3D lifting methods suffer from view inconsistencies and coarse masks.

Method: Uses SAM-HQ for accurate 2D masks, then applies prior-guided label reassignment to assign labels to 3D Gaussians via multiview consistency.

Result: State-of-the-art segmentation accuracy, superior boundary preservation, enables interactive real-time editing.

Conclusion: Advances 3D scene editing with practical utility in VR and robotics.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D-GS) enables real-time 3D scene reconstruction but lacks robust segmentation for editing tasks such as object removal, extraction, and recoloring. Existing approaches that lift 2D segmentations to the 3D domain suffer from view inconsistencies and coarse masks. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that leverages the Segment Anything Model High Quality (SAM-HQ) to generate accurate 2D masks, addressing the limitations of the standard SAM in boundary fidelity and fine-structure preservation. To achieve robust 3D segmentation of any target object in a given scene, we introduce a prior-guided label reassignment method that assigns labels to 3D Gaussians by enforcing multiview consistency with learned priors. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy and enables interactive, real-time object editing while maintaining high visual fidelity. Qualitative results demonstrate superior boundary preservation and practical utility in Virtual Reality (VR) and robotics, advancing 3D scene editing.

</details>


### [88] [AgriMind: An Ensemble Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Class Plant Disease Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16076)
*Salma Hoque Talukdar Koli,Fahima Haque Talukder Jely*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AgriMind, an ensemble of ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0, and DenseNet121, achieves 99.23% accuracy on PlantVillage disease classification (15 classes) by averaging softmax outputs, outperforming individual models (96-97%) and running at 53 FPS on an NVIDIA T4 GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Plant disease detection in Bangladesh remains manual and inefficient due to millions of smallholdings, motivating the need for an automated, lightweight solution.

Method: An ensemble of ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0, and DenseNet121 trained on 20,638 PlantVillage images (15 disease classes) using transfer learning with frozen ImageNet backbones and 10 epochs of head-only training. Softmax outputs are averaged for final prediction.

Result: Individual models achieve 96-97% accuracy; the ensemble reaches 99.23% overall (two-thirds error reduction). Pepper and potato classify perfectly; tomato reaches 99.01%. Runs at 53 FPS on an NVIDIA T4 GPU.

Conclusion: Ensemble averaging significantly improves accuracy over individual models, though real-time mobile deployment requires further TensorFlow Lite optimization not yet completed.

Abstract: Plant disease detection is still largely manual in Bangladesh, where extension workers eyeball leaf samples across millions of smallholdings. We built AgriMind to automate this: an ensemble of ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0, and DenseNet121 trained on 20,638 PlantVillage images across 15 pepper, potato, and tomato disease classes. Transfer learning with frozen ImageNet backbones and 10 epochs of head-only training keeps the pipeline lightweight. Individual models hit 96--97% on the held-out test set, but averaging their softmax outputs pushes the ensemble to 99.23% -- a two-thirds cut in error rate. We tried biasing the average toward the best validation model; it backfired. Dropping any single model also hurt. Pepper and potato classify perfectly; tomato, with ten visually similar classes, still reaches 99.01%. On an NVIDIA T4 GPU the full ensemble runs at 53 FPS. Whether that translates to real-time mobile use depends on TensorFlow Lite optimization -- work we have not yet completed.

</details>


### [89] [VideoSeeker: Incentivizing Instance-level Video Understanding via Native Agentic Tool Invocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16079)
*Yiming Zhao,Yu Zeng,Wenxuan Huang,Zhen Fang,Qing Miao,Qisheng Su,Jiawei Zhao,Jiayin Cai,Lin Chen,Zehui Chen,Yukun Qi,Yao Hu,Xiaolong Jiang,Feng Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VideoSeeker is a new paradigm for instance-level video understanding that uses visual prompts instead of text, integrates agentic reasoning, and achieves +13.7% improvement over baselines, surpassing GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Pro.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LVLMs struggle with precise spatiotemporal localization at the instance level due to: (1) text prompts providing poor spatial/temporal references, and (2) models centering reasoning around language rather than visual content, limiting proactive perception of fine-grained visual evidence.

Method: VideoSeeker integrates agentic reasoning with instance-level video understanding via visual prompts. It uses a four-stage fully automated data synthesis pipeline for large-scale training data, and internalizes tool-calling and proactive perception capabilities through cold-start supervision and RL training.

Result: Average improvement of +13.7% over baselines on instance-level tasks, surpassing GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Pro, with effective transferability to general video understanding benchmarks.

Conclusion: Visual prompts and proactive agentic reasoning significantly outperform text-based approaches for instance-level video understanding. The proposed data pipeline and training strategy are effective and transferable.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have shown significant progress in video understanding, yet they face substantial challenges in tasks requiring precise spatiotemporal localization at the instance level. Existing methods primarily rely on text prompts for human-model interaction, but these prompts struggle to provide precise spatial and temporal references, resulting in poor user experience. Furthermore, current approaches typically decouple visual perception from language reasoning, centering reasoning around language rather than visual content, which limits the model's ability to proactively perceive fine-grained visual evidence. To address these challenges, we propose VideoSeeker, a novel paradigm for instance-level video understanding through visual prompts. VideoSeeker seamlessly integrates agentic reasoning with instance-level video understanding tasks, enabling the model to proactively perceive and retrieve relevant video segments on demand. We construct a four-stage fully automated data synthesis pipeline to efficiently generate large-scale, high-quality instance-level video data. We internalize tool-calling and proactive perception capabilities into the model via cold-start supervision and RL training, building a powerful video understanding model. Experiments demonstrate that our model achieves an average improvement of +13.7% over baselines on instance-level video understanding tasks, surpassing powerful closed-source models such as GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Pro, while also showing effective transferability on general video understanding benchmarks. The relevant datasets and code will be released publicly.

</details>


### [90] [ReAlign: Generalizable Image Forgery Detection via Reasoning-Aligned Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16080)
*Qing Huang,Zhipei Xu,Xuanyu Zhang,Xiangyu Yu,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReAlign is a lightweight AIGI detector that distills reasoning from GRPO-optimized LLMs into a contrastive learning framework, achieving state-of-the-art generalization and accuracy on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AIGI detectors face a trade-off: non-LLM methods are efficient but lack semantic understanding, while LLM methods offer reasoning but are computationally heavy. The true value of LLM-generated reasoning texts for forgery detection is unclear, motivating the need for an efficient yet generalizable solution.

Method: Proposes ReAlign, a framework that uses GRPO-optimized LLM to generate high-quality reasoning texts, then distills them into a lightweight detector via contrastive learning. A joint optimization strategy combining contrastive loss (image-text alignment) and classification loss (forgery discrimination) is employed.

Result: ReAlign consistently outperforms state-of-the-art detectors on AIGCDetectBenchmark, AIGI-Holmes, and the newly constructed UltraSynth-10k, especially on complex, high-fidelity forgeries from modern generative models.

Conclusion: LLM-generated reasoning texts are valuable for improving generalization and semantic-error sensitivity in AIGI detection, and can be effectively distilled into efficient lightweight models without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: The rise of AI-generated images (AIGIs) poses growing challenges for digital authenticity, prompting the need for efficient, generalizable image forgery detection systems. Existing methods, whether non-LLM-based or LLM-based, exhibit distinct advantages and limitations. While non-LLM-based models offer efficient low-level artifact detection, they often lack semantic understanding. Conversely, LLM-based methods provide strong semantic reasoning and explainability but are computationally intensive and less sensitive to subtle visual artifacts. Moreover, the true contribution of explanatory reasoning texts to forgery detection performance remains unclear. In this work, we investigate the intrinsic value and potential of LLM-generated reasoning texts, considering it a source of generalization and semantic-error sensitivity. Based on these findings, we propose ReAlign, a novel framework that distills high-quality reasoning texts generated by a GRPO-optimized LLM into a lightweight AIGI detector via contrastive learning. ReAlign effectively inherits the generalization ability and semantic sensitivity capability of reasoning textual representations, while remaining efficient and lightweight for deployment. Moreover, ReAlign adopts a tailored joint optimization strategy that integrates contrastive loss for image-text alignment and classification loss for accurate forgery discrimination. Experimental results on AIGCDetectBenchmark, AIGI-Holmes, and our newly constructed UltraSynth-10k demonstrate that ReAlign consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art detectors in both accuracy and generalization, particularly when facing complex, high-fidelity forgeries from modern generative models.

</details>


### [91] [GenShield: Unified Detection and Artifact Correction for AI-Generated Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16122)
*Zhipei Xu,Xuanyu Zhang,Youmin Xu,Qing Huang,Shen Chen,Taiping Yao,Shouhong Ding,Jian Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GenShield proposes a unified autoregressive framework that jointly performs explainable AIGI detection and controllable artifact correction via a closed-loop diagnose-then-repair process, achieving state-of-the-art performance on both detection and correction benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While AIGI detection has advanced significantly, correcting detected artifacts to restore realistic appearance is underexplored, and no existing work connects detection with artifact correction.

Method: A unified autoregressive framework with Visual Chain-of-Thought curriculum learning for multi-step "diagnose-then-repair" correction with an explicit stopping criterion, plus a large-scale "artifact-restored" paired dataset.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on correction benchmarks and mainstream AIGI detection benchmarks with strong generalization.

Conclusion: Detection and artifact correction are mutually reinforcing tasks that benefit from a closed-loop diagnosis-to-restoration framework, advancing both AIGI authenticity assessment and restoration.

Abstract: Diffusion-based image synthesis has made AI-generated images (AIGI) increasingly photorealistic, raising urgent concerns about authenticity in applications such as misinformation detection, digital forensics, and content moderation. Despite the substantial advances in AIGI detection, how to correct detected AI-generated images with visible artifacts and restore realistic appearance remains largely underexplored. Moreover, few existing work has established the connection between AIGI detection and artifact correction. To fill this gap, we propose GenShield, a unified autoregressive framework that jointly performs explainable AIGI detection and controllable artifact correction in a closed loop from diagnosis to restoration, revealing a mutually reinforcing relationship between these two tasks. We further introduce a Visual Chain-of-Thought based curriculum learning strategy that enables self-explained, multi-step ``diagnose-then-repair'' correction with an explicit stopping criterion. A high-quality dataset with large-scale ``artifact-restored'' pairs is also constructed alongside a unified evaluation pipeline. Extensive experiments on our correction benchmark and mainstream AIGI detection benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization of our method. The code is available at https://github.com/zhipeixu/GenShield.

</details>


### [92] [WeatherOcc3D: VLM-Assisted Adverse Weather Aware 3D Semantic Occupancy Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16127)
*A. Enes Doruk,Abdelaziz Hussein,Hasan F. Ates*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a VLM-assisted framework that uses CLIP embeddings to dynamically adjust camera-LiDAR fusion ratios for 3D semantic occupancy prediction based on environmental conditions, achieving state-of-the-art results on nuScenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static fusion strategies fail when specific sensors become unreliable under adverse conditions (e.g., camera low-light degradation, LiDAR backscatter noise in rain), creating a modality trust problem that limits robustness.

Method: A VLM-assisted framework leveraging pre-trained CLIP latent space to guide multi-sensor integration via linguistic cues, using a parameter-efficient adapter to align weather-specific text embeddings with sensor features, and a gating strategy that decomposes environmental uncertainty into visibility and illumination factors to dynamically modulate the fusion ratio.

Result: On nuScenes, implementing the framework on OccMamba and M-CONet achieves mIoU scores of 26.3 and 21.1, significantly outperforming traditional baselines.

Conclusion: Dynamic, language-guided fusion strategies effectively resolve modality trust issues in adverse environments, improving multi-modal 3D occupancy prediction robustness.

Abstract: While multi-modal 3D semantic occupancy prediction typically enhances robustness by fusing camera and LiDAR inputs, its effectiveness is fundamentally constrained by environmental variability. Specifically, camera sensors suffer from severe low-light degradation, while LiDAR sensors encounter significant backscatter noise during heavy precipitation. These adverse conditions create a modality trust problem, as static fusion strategies fail to adaptively re-weight inputs when a specific sensor becomes unreliable. To address this, we propose a VLM-assisted framework leveraging the pre-trained CLIP latent space to guide multi-sensor integration via linguistic environmental cues. We utilize a parameter-efficient adapter to align weather-specific text embeddings with sensor features, coupled with a gating strategy that decomposes environmental uncertainty into two factors: visibility and illumination. This enables the model to dynamically modulate the fusion ratio - prioritizing semantic camera features in clear daylight and shifting to geometric LiDAR priors during rainy nights. Evaluations on the nuScenes dataset demonstrate the versatility of our approach, as implementing our proposed framework on the OccMamba and M-CONet architectures achieves mIoU scores of 26.3 and 21.1, respectively, significantly outperforming their traditional baselines.

</details>


### [93] [Registers Matter for Pixel-Space Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16147)
*Nikita Starodubcev,Ilia Sudakov,Ilya Drobyshevskiy,Artem Babenko,Dmitry Baranchuk*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether register tokens, previously used to mitigate outlier issues in Vision Transformers (ViTs), are beneficial for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs). The authors find that unlike ViTs, DiTs don't have patch-token outliers, but register tokens still improve convergence and generation quality in pixel-space DiTs by producing cleaner feature maps at high noise levels. They also propose a parameter-efficient dual-stream architecture for register tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Register tokens have been shown to effectively mitigate high-norm patch-token outliers in Vision Transformers (ViTs), improving feature map quality. As diffusion models increasingly adopt transformer architectures and shift toward pixel-space training, they become more similar to ViTs, raising the question of whether register tokens are also useful for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs).

Method: The authors analyze intermediate representations of DiTs to study the behavior of register tokens, compare ViTs and DiTs in terms of patch-token outliers, and propose a parameter-efficient dual-stream architecture that specializes processing for register tokens.

Result: DiTs do not exhibit patch-token outliers unlike ViTs. Register tokens significantly improve convergence and generation quality of pixel-space DiTs, producing cleaner feature maps at high noise levels. Recent pixel-space DiT architectures implicitly incorporate register-like mechanisms. The proposed dual-stream architecture improves pixel-space generation quality with negligible runtime overhead.

Conclusion: Register tokens are beneficial for DiTs despite the absence of patch-token outliers, and their effectiveness stems from cleaner feature maps at high noise levels. The findings also suggest that implicit register-like mechanisms in modern DiT architectures contribute to their strong performance.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) are known to exhibit high-norm patch-token outliers that degrade feature map quality, a problem effectively mitigated by \textit{register tokens}. As diffusion models increasingly adopt transformer architectures and move toward pixel-space training, they become closer in form to ViTs, raising the question of whether register tokens are also useful for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs). In this work, we show that DiTs differ from ViTs in a key respect: they do not exhibit patch-token outliers. Interestingly, register tokens significantly improve convergence and generation quality of pixel-space DiTs. By analyzing intermediate representations, we find that register tokens produce cleaner feature maps at high noise levels, which may contribute to their effectiveness in pixel-space generation. We further observe that recent pixel-space DiT architectures implicitly incorporate register-like mechanisms, which may partially account for their strong empirical performance. Motivated by these insights, we investigate a parameter-efficient dual-stream architecture that specializes processing for register tokens and improves pixel-space generation quality with negligible runtime overhead.

</details>


### [94] [Second-Order Multi-Level Variance Correction for Modality Competition in Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16165)
*Yishun Lu,Wes Armour*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ML-FOP-SOAP is a second-order optimizer with multi-level variance correction that resolves modality competition in multimodal autoregressive training, achieving up to 1.4× sample efficiency and 1.5× training speedup over AdamW at batch size 8192.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive next-token training for multimodal models (image generation + text understanding) suffers from strong modality competition that destabilizes optimization and limits large-batch scaling. First-order optimizers like AdamW are vulnerable to cross-modality gradient heterogeneity.

Method: The authors propose ML-FOP-SOAP, a second-order optimization framework with Multi-Level Variance Correction. It uses Fisher-Orthogonal Projection to suppress variance-induced modality conflicts and a hierarchical folding strategy to capture fine-grained variance with low micro-step overhead for practical large gradient accumulation.

Result: On Janus and Emu3 models, ML-FOP-SOAP achieves consistent gains across both visual generation and text understanding modalities, enables stable training at batch size 8192, improves sample efficiency by up to 1.4×, and accelerates wall-clock training by up to 1.5× compared to AdamW.

Conclusion: Second-order preconditioning (especially SOAP) provides more stable multimodal alignment than first-order optimizers. ML-FOP-SOAP offers a robust optimizer for scaling multimodal foundation models by effectively mitigating modality competition.

Abstract: Autoregressive next-token training offers a unified formulation for image generation and text understanding, but it also creates strong modality competition that destabilizes optimization and limits large-batch scaling. We show that first-order optimizers such as AdamW are vulnerable to cross-modality gradient heterogeneity, while second-order preconditioning, particularly SOAP, provides a more stable basis for multimodal alignment. Building on this insight, we propose \emph{ML-FOP-SOAP}, a second-order optimization framework with Multi-Level Variance Correction. Our Fisher-Orthogonal Projection suppresses variance-induced modality conflicts, reducing the trade-off between visual generation and textual understanding. To make this practical under large gradient accumulation, we introduce a hierarchical folding strategy that captures fine-grained variance with low micro-step overhead. Experiments on Janus and Emu3 show consistent gains across both modalities and stable training at batch size 8192. Compared with AdamW, our method improves sample efficiency by up to $1.4\times$ and accelerates wall-clock training by up to $1.5\times$, offering a robust optimizer for scaling multimodal foundation models.

</details>


### [95] [Res$^2$CLIP: Few-Shot Generalist Anomaly Detection with Residual-to-Residual Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16171)
*Xinyue Liu,Jianyuan Wang,Biao Leng,Shuo Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Res²CLIP shifts multimodal alignment into a unified residual space to solve cross-granularity mismatch and cross-category generalization degradation in few-shot generalist anomaly detection, using a novel residual-to-residual alignment framework with three branches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CLIP-based anomaly detection methods struggle with (1) coarse-grained text prompts failing to capture fine-grained foreground-background differences (cross-granularity mismatch), and (2) fine-tuning on auxiliary datasets breaks CLIP's open-world generalization due to domain shift (cross-category degradation).

Method: Proposes Res²CLIP, a residual-to-residual alignment framework with three branches: text prompt-based, visual prompt-based, and a novel residual-to-residual alignment branch. All learnable optimizations are constrained within the residual domain, and alignment objectives focus on relative anomaly deviations rather than class-specific features.

Result: Experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed architecture for few-shot generalist anomaly detection.

Conclusion: Shifting multimodal alignment into a unified residual space simultaneously resolves both cross-granularity mismatch and cross-category generalization degradation, enabling effective few-shot generalist anomaly detection.

Abstract: Few-shot Generalist Anomaly Detection requires models to generalize to novel categories without retraining, posing significant challenges in real-world scenarios with scarce samples and rapidly changing categories. Existing CLIP-based methods face two major challenges: coarse-grained unified text prompts struggle to adapt to fine-grained foreground-background differences, causing cross-granularity mismatch; and fine-tuning on auxiliary datasets disrupts CLIP's inherent open-world generalization due to domain shift, leading to cross-category generalization degradation. To address these, we propose to shift multimodal alignment entirely into a unified residual space, where residual representations naturally eliminate fine-grained normal feature differences across regions and class-specific biases, simultaneously resolving both problems. Based on this insight, Res$^2$CLIP, the first residual-to-residual alignment framework that symmetrically bridges visual and text modalities within CLIP's residual space, is designed. The framework is developed from a residual perspective into three branches: a text prompt-based branch, a visual prompt-based branch, and a novel residual-to-residual alignment branch. All learnable optimizations are constrained within the residual domain, and the residual alignment optimization objectives are designed to force the model to focus on relative anomaly deviations rather than optimizing class-specific features. Experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our architecture. The code is available at https://github.com/hito2448/Res2CLIP.

</details>


### [96] [MAgSeg: Segmentation of Agricultural Landscapes in High-Resolution Satellite Imagery using Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16179)
*Piyush Tiwary,Utkarsh Ahuja,Depanshu Sani,Aishwarya Jayagopal,Sagar Gubbi,Subhashini Venugopalan,Alok Talekar,Vaibhav Rajan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAgSeg is a decoder-free MLLM segmentation approach that addresses context length bottlenecks and domain alignment gaps to effectively segment complex smallholder agricultural landscapes in the Global South from high-resolution satellite imagery, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agricultural landscape segmentation in the Global South is challenging due to fragmented plots, high intra-class variance, and scarce labeled data. Existing MLLM approaches suffer from context length bottlenecks and domain alignment gaps when understanding satellite features.

Method: MAgSeg is a decoder-free MLLM segmentation approach that uses a novel instruction tuning data format enabling scalable fine-tuning on high-resolution satellite imagery. It learns from global image context while generating text tokens for only a patch within the image.

Result: Extensive evaluations on datasets spanning three countries in the Global South show MAgSeg significantly outperforms state-of-the-art MLLM baselines.

Conclusion: MAgSeg offers a scalable solution to map smallholder agricultural environments, effectively addressing key limitations of current MLLM approaches.

Abstract: Agricultural landscape segmentation in the Global South is challenging as it is characterized by fragmented plots, high intra-class variance, and a scarcity of labeled training data. Recent advances in segmentation have been made by Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, current approaches encounter critical context length bottlenecks and a domain alignment gap in understanding satellite features. We address these limitations through MAgSeg, a novel, decoder-free MLLM segmentation approach. MAgSeg is an architecturally efficient approach that enables standard MLLMs to perform segmentation of complex smallholder agricultural landscapes from high-resolution satellite imagery, without requiring auxiliary vision decoders. We introduce a novel instruction tuning data format designed to enable scalable fine-tuning and post-training on high resolution satellite imagery, which enables MAgSeg to learn from the global context of the image while generating text tokens for only a patch within the image. Extensive evaluations on datasets spanning three countries in the Global South demonstrate that MAgSeg significantly outperforms state-of-the-art MLLM baselines, offering a scalable solution to map smallholder agricultural environments.

</details>


### [97] [Offline Semantic Guidance for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16241)
*Jin Shi,Brady Zhang,Yishun Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLA-AD is a distillation framework that uses a Vision-Language Model to provide offline semantic supervision (task phase anchors and multi-frame direction descriptions) for distilling large VLA teachers (e.g., OpenVLA-7B, π₀.5-4B) into lightweight student policies (158M params), achieving 44× size reduction, 3.28× speedup, and near-lossless performance (≤0.53% gap).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Billion-parameter VLA policies are too large and slow for real-time closed-loop robotic control, motivating the need for a distillation approach that reduces model size and inference cost without sacrificing performance.

Method: VLA-AD uses a VLM as an offline semantic supervisor to augment teacher-provided 7-DoF action targets with high-level semantic signals—task phase anchors and multi-frame operating-direction descriptions—during training only. At test time, the lightweight student runs independently without the teacher or VLM.

Result: On LIBERO benchmarks, VLA-AD distills OpenVLA-7B into a 158M-parameter student (44× reduction, 0.27% avg gap), achieving 12.5 Hz inference (3.28× speedup). It also generalizes to a π₀.5-4B teacher, with students matching or outperforming the teacher on most suites (≤0.53% gap on libero_goal).

Conclusion: Offline semantic guidance from VLMs substantially improves the efficiency, robustness (e.g., to noisy gripper commands), and deployability of VLA policy distillation, enabling real-time closed-loop control with minimal performance loss.

Abstract: Billion-parameter Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies have recently shown impressive performance in robotic manipulation, yet their size and inference cost remain major obstacles for real-time closed-loop control. We introduce \textbf{VLA-AD}, a distillation framework that uses a Vision-Language Model as an offline semantic supervisor to transfer large VLA teachers into lightweight student policies. Instead of relying only on low-level action imitation, VLA-AD augments teacher-provided 7-DoF action targets with high-level semantic guidance, including task phase anchors and multi-frame operating-direction descriptions. These auxiliary signals are used only during training: at test time, the student policy runs independently, with neither the VLA teacher nor the VLM required. We evaluate VLA-AD on three LIBERO benchmark suites. Using OpenVLA-7B as the teacher, our method produces a 158M-parameter student, yielding a $44\times$ reduction in model size while matching the teacher with only a $0.27\%$ average relative gap. The resulting policy runs at 12.5 Hz on an RTX 4090, achieving a $3.28\times$ inference speedup over OpenVLA-7B. We further show that the same semantic distillation pipeline generalizes to a different $π_{0.5}$-4B teacher, where the student outperforms the teacher on two suites and remains within $0.53\%$ on \texttt{libero\_goal}. Additional analysis indicates that phase-level supervision and multi-frame directional cues make the student less sensitive to noisy teacher actions, such as erroneous high-frequency gripper changes. Overall, VLA-AD demonstrates that offline semantic guidance from VLMs can substantially improve the efficiency, robustness, and deployability of VLA policy distillation.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [98] [TVRN: Invertible Neural Networks for Compression-Aware Temporal Video Rescaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15579)
*Xinmin Feng,Li Li,Dong Liu,Feng Wu*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: TVRN is an end-to-end, compression-aware framework for frame-rate rescaling that uses an invertible architecture with temporal wavelet transform and high-frequency reconstruction to preserve details, plus a surrogate network for handling non-differentiable lossy codecs, outperforming existing methods under industrial compression settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing frame-rate rescaling methods jointly optimize temporal downscaling and upscaling but fail to fully exploit the reciprocal nature of these operations, causing high-frequency information loss. They also overlook the impact of lossy codecs on low-frame-rate videos, limiting practical applicability.

Method: 1) Invertible architecture combining Multi-Input Multi-Output Temporal Wavelet Transform with a high-frequency reconstruction module to regularize lost high-frequency details. 2) A surrogate network to approximate gradients of non-differentiable lossy codecs for end-to-end training. 3) An asymmetric architecture with compression-aware features learned via a learning-to-rank strategy for robustness across compression levels.

Result: TVRN outperforms existing methods in reconstruction quality under industrial video compression settings.

Conclusion: The proposed compression-aware end-to-end framework effectively addresses high-frequency information loss and codec degradation in frame-rate rescaling, achieving superior reconstruction quality in real-world industrial compression scenarios.

Abstract: To fit diverse display and bandwidth constraints, high-frame-rate videos are temporally downscaled to low-frame-rate (LFR) and later upscaled, requiring joint optimization for effective frame-rate rescaling. However, existing methods typically link the two operations via training objectives, without fully exploiting their reciprocal nature, which may cause high-frequency information loss. Moreover, they overlook the impact of lossy codecs on LFR videos, limiting real-world applicability. In this work, we propose an end-to-end framework for compression-aware frame-rate rescaling, named TVRN. To regularize high-frequency information lost during frame-rate downscaling, TVRN adopts an invertible architecture that combines a Multi-Input Multi-Output Temporal Wavelet Transform with a high-frequency reconstruction module. To enable end-to-end training through non-differentiable lossy codecs, we design a surrogate network that approximates their gradients. Finally, to improve robustness under various compression levels, we extend TVRN to an asymmetric architecture by incorporating compression-aware features learned via a learning-to-rank strategy. Extensive experiments show that TVRN outperforms existing methods in reconstruction quality under industrial video compression settings. Source code is publicly available at https://github.com/fengxinmin/TVRN_public.

</details>


### [99] [Degradation-Aware Blur-Segmentation of Brain Tumor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15671)
*Yuchun Wang,Xiaosong Li,Gefei Liang,Yang Liu*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: DABSeg is a synchronous deblurring and segmentation network for 3D MRI brain tumor segmentation that explicitly handles motion artifacts and blur, using a feature-domain motion-deblurring stem, blur-aware cross-modal attention, multi-scale residual aggregation, and a joint loss with imbalanced weighting for small targets, achieving state-of-the-art results on BraTS2020 under both clear and degenerative conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing segmentation methods assume artifact-free MRI images, but inevitable patient motion during scanning introduces blur and artifacts that degrade boundary/texture features and lead to poor segmentation. This gap between ideal assumptions and realistic imaging conditions motivates a unified approach that handles both blur removal and segmentation jointly.

Method: DABSeg consists of three key components: (1) a feature-domain motion-deblurring stem that compensates for blur and rebalances intensity; (2) a backbone network with a blur-aware cross-modal cross-attention module and multi-scale residual aggregation for effective modality complementarity; (3) a joint loss combining weighted Dice with a clear-reference reconstruction term, using imbalanced weights for small targets to improve learning on small lesions and border regions.

Result: Systematic comparisons and ablation experiments on the BraTS2020 dataset under both clear and degenerative conditions demonstrate that DABSeg surpasses state-of-the-art methods in tumor Dice score and boundary precision, validating the effectiveness of degradation-aware cross-task collaborative learning.

Conclusion: The degradation-aware cross-task collaborative learning approach improves robustness and clinical utility of multi-modal 3D brain tumor segmentation under realistic degenerative conditions. The source code is publicly available.

Abstract: Multimodal 3D MRI brain tumor segmentation is a pivotal step in radiotherapy target delineation, surgical planning and post-treatment assessment. Existing methods often assume artifact-free MRI images. However, inevitable patient motion during scanning introduces artifacts and blur that degrade boundary and texture features, leading to poor segmentation performance. To bridge this gap, we introduce Degradation-Aware Blur-Segmentation Net (DABSeg), a synchronous deblurring 3D multimodal MRI segmentation network that unifies blur removal and accurate segmentation. Specifically, we propose a feature-domain motion-deblurring stem to compensate for blur and rebalance intensity. Concurrently, the backbone network embeds a blur-aware cross-modal cross-attention module and multi-scale residual aggregation to yield effective modality complementarity. Notably, we optimize a joint loss that combines weighted Dice with a clear-reference reconstruction term, where imbalanced weights are applied to small targets to boost learning intensity and predictive stability for small lesions and border regions. Systematic comparisons and ablation experiments on the BraTS2020 dataset under both clear and degenerative conditions consistently demonstrate that DABSeg surpasses state-of-the-art methods in tumor Dice score and boundary precision. These results validate the effectiveness of degenerative-aware cross-task collaborative learning in improving the robustness and clinical utility of multi-modal 3D brain tumor segmentation under realistic degenerative conditions. The source code is available at https://github.com/YuchunWang24/DABSeg_ICPR

</details>


### [100] [Highly Detailed and Generalizable Broadleaf Tree Crown Instance Segmentation from UAV Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15673)
*Mitsutaka Nakada,Takahiko Ikebata,Kengo Ikebata,Yuji Mizuno,Yusuke Onoda,Ryuichi Takeshige,Kyaw Kyaw Htoo,Kanehiro Kitayama,Robert Ong,Masanori Onishi*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A deep-learning model (based on Mask2Former) trained on 18,507 manually annotated crown polygons from Japanese broadleaf forests achieves high-performance individual tree crown segmentation from UAV RGB imagery, generalizing to other regions including tropical rainforests, and is integrated into DF Scanner Pro software for practical forest monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tree crown delineation in broadleaf forests is more challenging than in other forest types due to diverse crown shapes and lack of clearly defined treetops, motivating the need for a robust deep-learning-based solution using high-quality annotations.

Method: Manual delineation of 18,507 crown polygons from orthomosaic images across seven Japanese forests by skilled annotators; development of a model based on Mask2Former with multiple backbone architectures; training on high-quality RGB imagery.

Result: The best model achieved high segmentation performance in structurally complex broadleaf forests using only RGB imagery, and maintained performance when applied to geographically distinct forests in Japan and biologically distinct tropical rainforests in Borneo.

Conclusion: Using a large number of high-quality annotated datasets is critical for achieving detailed and generalizable crown segmentation across diverse forest ecosystems. The model is integrated into DF Scanner Pro for practical UAV-based forest monitoring.

Abstract: We present a highly detailed instance segmentation model for delineating individual tree crowns in natural broadleaf forests using aerial imagery acquired by unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). Tree crown delineation in broadleaf forests is more challenging than in other forest types due to diversity of crown shapes and the lack of clearly defined treetops. To address this issue, we developed a deep-learning-based crown segmentation model trained on high-quality annotated crown outlines. We manually delineated 18,507 crown polygons from orthomosaic images collected across seven forests in Japan by skilled annotators, and developed a model based on Mask2Former with multiple backbone architectures. The best model achieved high segmentation performance in structurally complex broadleaf forests using only RGB imagery. This performance was maintained when applied to geographically distinct forests within Japan, as well as to biologically distinct tropical rainforests in Borneo. These results demonstrate that using a large number of high-quality annotated datasets is critical for achieving detailed and generalizable crown segmentation across diverse forest ecosystems. The developed model has been integrated into DF Scanner Pro, a software that supports practical forest monitoring using UAVs, and this implementation is expected to enable a wide range of users to analyze tree-level information in broadleaf forest from UAVs.

</details>


### [101] [Evaluation of Anatomical Shape Priors in Deep Learning-Based Cardiac Multi-Compartment Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15707)
*Michael Hudler,Franz Thaler,Martin Urschler*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Standard 3D U-Net already captures implicit anatomical regularities; handcrafted shape priors provide marginal or inconsistent improvements for cardiac CT segmentation, suggesting future work needs more expressive learned priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Whole-heart multi-compartment CT segmentation is clinically important, but standard CNNs do not explicitly enforce anatomical plausibility.

Method: Evaluated lightweight explicit shape priors (shape-aware losses and spatial label distribution heatmap-guided U-Net variants) against a standard 3D U-Net on MM-WHS CT and WHS++ datasets.

Result: Standard 3D U-Net remained a very strong baseline; handcrafted priors yielded marginal/inconsistent changes and often degraded performance.

Conclusion: Baseline already captures substantial implicit anatomical regularities; future gains require more expressive learned priors rather than simple handcrafted anatomical shape constraints.

Abstract: Whole-heart multi-compartment CT segmentation is clinically important, but standard CNNs do not explicitly enforce anatomical plausibility. Based on statistics derived from the training data, we evaluate whether lightweight explicit shape priors, implemented as shape-aware losses and spatial label distribution heatmap-guided U-Net variants, improve 3D cardiac segmentation on MM-WHS CT and WHS++. Across all experiments, a standard 3D U-Net surprisingly remained a very strong baseline, with handcrafted priors yielding at best marginal and inconsistent changes and often degrading performance. These results suggest that the baseline already captures substantial implicit anatomical regularities and that future gains will likely require more expressive learned priors rather than simple handcrafted anatomical shape constraints.

</details>


### [102] [Video Quality Evaluation Methodology and Result of AV2 Compression Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15800)
*Zhijun Lei,Vibhoothi Vibhoothi,Dzung Hoang,Yixin Du,Ramzi Khsib*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: AV2 achieves ~30-34% bitrate reduction over AV1 under new Common Test Conditions with updated evaluation methods including adaptive streaming, UGC, and extended chroma formats.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To define standardized evaluation methodology (CTC) for AV2 and demonstrate its compression efficiency gains over AV1 for next-generation streaming.

Method: AV2 CTC introduces convex-hull-based adaptive streaming config, user-generated content, and extended chroma formats; tested AV2 v13.0 against AV1 baseline using BD-rate metrics (PSNR-YUV and VMAF) under random access.

Result: AV2 achieves 29.81% (PSNR-YUV) and 33.79% (VMAF) BD-rate reductions over AV1 under random access configuration.

Conclusion: AV2 demonstrates significant compression efficiency, validating its suitability for next-generation streaming applications.

Abstract: The Alliance for Open Media (AOMedia) has developed the AV2 video coding standard to supersede AV1, aiming for substantial compression efficiency gains across diverse media applications. This paper details the quality and performance evaluation methodology defined in the AV2 Common Test Conditions (CTC), which introduces new evaluation methods and content, including convex-hull-based adaptive streaming (AS) configuration, user-generated content (UGC), and extended chroma formats. We present the coding gains of the AV2 (v13.0) against the AV1 baseline. Experimental results show that AV2 achieves significant Bjøntegaard-Delta Rate (BD-rate) reductions of 29.81\% and 33.79\% for PSNR-YUV and VMAF, respectively, under random access configuration, validating the efficiency of AV2 for next-generation streaming applications.

</details>


### [103] [Layer Selection in Feature-Based Losses Affects Image Quality and Microstructural Consistency in Deep Learning Super-Resolution of Brain Diffusion MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15895)
*David Lohr,Rene Werner*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The study finds that using deeper VGG16 layers for feature-based loss in diffusion MRI super-resolution creates grid artifacts, while the shallowest layer preserves quality even at 9× super-resolution, emphasizing careful layer selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution diffusion MRI is limited by hardware and long scan times, driving the need for computational super-resolution methods that preserve diffusion signal fidelity.

Method: UNets were trained on 7T HCP data for 2D super-resolution of DWI, with ablation studies comparing different VGG16 layers for feature-based loss against an L1 baseline.

Result: Deeper VGG16 layers and their combinations introduced grid-like artifacts in DWIs and derived diffusion parameters. The shallowest layer produced no such artifacts and showed strong consistency with ground truth even at 9× super-resolution.

Conclusion: Careful selection of VGG16 layer depth is critical for artifact-free diffusion MRI super-resolution; the shallowest layer is recommended over deeper layers.

Abstract: Clinical application of high-resolution diffusion MRI is hindered by hardware limitations and prohibitive scan times, motivating computational super-resolution. This study investigates the efficacy of a feature-based loss function in preserving diffusion signal consistency in deep learning super-resolution. Using 7T data from the human connectome project to generate pairs of low- and high-resolution diffusion weighted images (DWI), we trained UNets for 2D super-resolution. Ablation and isolation studies evaluated different VGG16-layers for feature-based losses against an image-based L1 baseline. Deeper layers and combinations thereof resulted in grid-like artifacts in super-resolution DWIs, which persisted in diffusion parameters like quantitative and fractional anisotropy. No such artifacts were present when using the shallowest layer. Downstream analysis for this layer showed great consistency with the ground truth, even for 9-fold super-resolution. Image SNR and used VGG16-layer depths modulated artifact appearance and severity, mandating careful selection of contributing layers for application in and beyond diffusion MRI.

</details>


### [104] [From Full and Partial Intraoral Scans to Crown Proposal: A Classification-Guided Restoration Assistance Pipeline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15241)
*Rabin Kunwar,Dikshya Parajuli,Rujal Acharya,Romik Gosai,Prince Panta,Kundan Siwakoti,Shuvangi Adhikari,Saugat Kafley,Louis Digiorgio,Amit Regmi,Akio Tanaka,Masahiko Inada,Yuriko Komagamine,Kennta Kashiwazaki,Manabu Kanazawa*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A three-phase pipeline for single-unit crown restoration from partial intraoral scans: (I) pose standardization, (II) classify-then-align segmentation achieving DSC 0.9249, and (III) context-aware retrieval plus Blender-based crown generation in 2.5-3.5 minutes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-unit crown restoration is common but existing methods fail: segmentation networks trained on full arches don't work on partial scans, and end-to-end generative methods produce over-smoothed surfaces lacking occlusal detail.

Method: Three-phase pipeline: (I) data preparation and pose standardization; (II) classify-then-align segmentation using DGCNN classifier, RANSAC+ICP registration, and graph-cut optimization; (III) context-aware retrieval via DGCNN embeddings and cosine similarity, followed by spline-guided alignment and Blender Python API refinement.

Result: Macro-average DSC 0.9249, Recall 0.8919, Precision 0.9615 across 17 classes; tooth with neighbors DSC 0.9468-0.9569, sub-millimeter centroid errors (0.2666-0.2774 mm); full pipeline runs 2.5-3.5 minutes per crown.

Conclusion: The pipeline offers a practical alternative to end-to-end generative approaches, producing patient-specific crown proposals efficiently from partial scans with high segmentation accuracy.

Abstract: Single-unit crown restoration is among the most common procedures in clinical dentistry, with CAD/CAM workflows now designing crowns directly from intraoral scans. Partial scans are often preferred over full-arch scans for single-unit cases due to fewer stitching errors, yet most segmentation networks trained on full arches fail on partial scans, while end-to-end generative crown methods often produce over-smoothed surfaces that lose occlusal detail. We propose an end-to-end pipeline that takes a raw intraoral scan and target FDI tooth number as input and outputs an initial, patient-specific crown proposal for clinician refinement. The pipeline has three phases: (I) data preparation and pose standardization; (II) segmentation routed by scan type; and (III) crown proposal generation via context-aware retrieval and Blender-based fitting. We address partial-scan segmentation through a classify-then-align strategy: a DGCNN classifier categorizes the scan into one of five anatomical types, then coarse-to-fine RANSAC+ICP registration standardizes the jaw coordinate frame, followed by graph-cut optimization to refine tooth-gingival boundaries. Trained on 1,958 partial scans, the pipeline achieves macro-average DSC 0.9249, Recall 0.8919, and Precision 0.9615 across 17 semantic classes; a fine-tuned full-arch model reaches DSC 0.9347. The prepared tooth and its mesial and distal neighbors achieve DSC 0.9468-0.9569 with sub-millimeter Centroid Errors (0.2666-0.2774 mm). These centroids anchor a retrieval module using DGCNN embeddings and cosine similarity over neighboring and opposing teeth, followed by spline-guided alignment and Blender Python API refinement. The pipeline produces a preliminary crown shell in 2.5-3.5 minutes, offering a practical alternative to end-to-end generative approaches.

</details>


### [105] [DIPA: Distilled Preconditioned Algorithms for Solving Imaging Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15456)
*Romario Gualdrón-Hurtado,Roman Jacome,Leon Suarez,Henry Arguello*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: DIPA introduces preconditioning operators optimized via teacher-guided distillation to improve reconstruction quality in imaging inverse problems, surpassing traditional use of preconditioning for mere convergence acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imaging inverse problems face challenges from ill-conditioned sensing matrices. Traditional preconditioning only accelerates convergence; the authors aim to use preconditioning to also improve reconstruction quality.

Method: DIPA optimizes a preconditioning operator (PO) via teacher-guided distillation. The teacher uses a simulated, better-conditioned sensing matrix, while the student uses the physically feasible one. PO can be linear (L-DIPA, interpretable) or non-linear (N-DIPA, neural network).

Result: Validated across MRI, compressed sensing, and super-resolution imaging, demonstrating improved reconstruction quality.

Conclusion: Preconditioning can go beyond convergence acceleration to enhance reconstruction quality; teacher-guided distillation effectively transfers beneficial properties from a simulated ideal sensing system to a physically feasible one.

Abstract: Solving imaging inverse problems has usually been addressed by designing proper prior models of the underlying signal. However, minimizing the data fidelity term poses significant challenges due to the ill-conditioned sensing matrix caused by physical constraints in the acquisition system. Thus, preconditioning techniques have been adopted in classical optimization theory to address ill-conditioned data-fidelity minimization by transforming the algorithm gradient step to achieve faster convergence and better numerical stability. We extend the preconditioning concept beyond convergence acceleration and use it to improve reconstruction quality. We introduce DIPA: Distilled Preconditioned Algorithms, where a preconditioning operator (PO) is optimized using teacher-guided distillation criteria. Unlike standard model-compression KD, the teacher and student differ by the sensing operators available during reconstruction: the teacher uses a simulated, better-conditioned, and more informative sensing matrix, whereas the student uses the physically feasible sensing matrix. We design different distillation loss functions to transfer different properties of the teacher algorithm to the preconditioned student. The PO can be linear (L-DIPA), allowing interpretability, or non-linear (N-DIPA), parametrized by a neural network, offering better scalability. We validate the proposed PO design across several imaging modalities, including magnetic resonance imaging, compressed sensing, and super-resolution imaging.

</details>


### [106] [Text-RSIR: A Text-Guided Framework for Efficient Remote Sensing Image Transmission and Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15558)
*Hao Yang,Xianping Ma,Peifeng Ma,Man-On Pun*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a text-guided remote sensing image transmission system that sends low-resolution images with compact text descriptions instead of full-resolution data, reducing data volume to ~2% of original size, and uses a text-conditioned restoration model to reconstruct high-quality images via cross-modal learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution remote sensing imagery transmission suffers from limited bandwidth and high communication costs; conventional full-resolution pixel delivery is redundant and inefficient.

Method: An onboard text generator produces spatial and semantic summaries from low-resolution images, reducing data to ~2% original volume. A text-conditioned image restoration model uses cross-modal learning to recover fine spatial details and maintain semantic coherence on the ground side.

Result: Achieves reconstruction PSNRs of 16.36 dB (Alsat-2B), 26.87 dB (UC Merced), and 27.41 dB (Aerial Image datasets), demonstrating efficient and information-preserving transfer.

Conclusion: The proposed text-guided framework significantly reduces transmission bandwidth while preserving reconstruction quality, offering a practical solution for remote sensing image delivery.

Abstract: High-resolution remote sensing imagery is critical for environmental monitoring, urban mapping, and land cover analysis, but its transmission is often hindered by limited bandwidth and high communication costs. Conventional pipelines transmit full-resolution pixel data, resulting in redundant and inefficient delivery. This paper proposes a text-guided remote sensing image transmission system that replaces complete high-resolution data with low-resolution images accompanied by compact textual descriptions. An onboard text generator produces spatial and semantic summaries, reducing the transmitted data volume to approximately 2\% of the original size. For ground-based reconstruction, a text-conditioned image restoration model is introduced, which leverages cross-modal learning to recover fine spatial details and maintain semantic coherence. Experimental results on the Alsat-2B, UC Merced Land Use, and Aerial Image datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves reconstruction PSNRs of 16.36 dB, 26.87 dB, and 27.41 dB, respectively, enabling efficient and information-preserving image transfer for remote sensing applications. The implementation will be made publicly available at \href{https://github.com/haoyangofficial/textrssr}{GitHub}.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [107] [Joint Mobile User Positioning and Passive Target Sensing using Optimized Sequential Beamforming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15808)
*Aymen Hamrouni,Sofie Pollin,Hazem Sallouha*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a velocity-aware sequential beamforming framework that dynamically couples monostatic sensing and bistatic positioning for ISAC systems, using sequential Bayesian optimization to share information between phases and achieving centimeter-level accuracy with reduced runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ISAC frameworks assume static geometries and optimize MS and BP independently, neglecting user mobility and sequential information sharing between modalities.

Method: Derives Cramér-Rao bounds (CRBs) in the position domain to formulate a non-convex resource allocation problem, then introduces a sequential Bayesian optimization strategy where MS is executed first to build a structural prior (covariance) that regularizes the subsequent BP estimation stage, using a globally shared beamformer across both phases.

Result: The shared sequential design achieves centimeter-level positioning accuracy for both UE and passive targets, robust velocity estimation, and significantly reduced computational runtime compared to two-stage greedy approaches.

Conclusion: Optimizing a single shared beamformer globally across both MS and BP phases yields superior synergistic gains, efficiently balancing limited symbol resources in dynamic ISAC scenarios.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) relies on monostatic sensing (MS) and bistatic positioning (BP) to enable comprehensive environmental awareness and user localization. However, existing frameworks predominantly assume static geometries and optimize these modalities independently, neglecting user mobility and sequential information sharing. In this paper, we propose a velocity-aware sequential beamforming framework that dynamically couples MS and BP in time. We derive the Cramer-Rao bounds (CRBs) in the position domain to formulate a non-convex resource allocation problem. Instead of relying on static weighted-sum tradeoffs, we introduce a sequential Bayesian optimization strategy where MS is executed first to construct a reliable structural prior on the UE and passive targets (PTs). This covariance prior is subsequently passed to the UE to regularize the BP estimation stage. We demonstrate that optimizing a single shared beamformer globally across both phases yields superior synergistic gains compared to a two-stage greedy approach. Simulation results validate that the shared sequential design efficiently balances limited symbol resources, achieving centimeter-level positioning accuracy for both the UE and PTs, robust velocity estimation, and a significantly reduced computational runtime.

</details>


### [108] [Agent-Native Wireless Communications: Architecture, Opportunities, and the Road Ahead](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15873)
*Yuanwei Liu,Xu Gan,Zhaolin Wang,Shan Shan,Zongyao Zhao,Zhiguo Ding*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This article proposes an agent-native wireless communication framework that integrates AI agents with communication systems, organized around "agents for communications" (using agents to design/operate networks) and "communications for agents" (providing wireless support for agent operations), leveraging O-RAN and deployable computing infrastructure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Future wireless networks need autonomous operation that can adapt to time-varying radio conditions and evolving service objectives, requiring tighter integration between agent intelligence and communication systems.

Method: The framework is built on deployable computing infrastructure, programmable O-RAN software, and controllable communication interfaces. It separates into two directions: agents for communications (agent-generated software, adaptive optimization) and communications for agents (single-agent loops, multi-agent coordination support).

Result: A systematic agent-native wireless communication framework characterizing the bidirectional coupling between agent intelligence and communication systems.

Conclusion: Research directions for measurable, safe, and interoperable deployment of agent-native wireless communications are outlined.

Abstract: Future wireless networks are moving toward autonomous service operation, where network control and resource management need to respond to time-varying radio conditions and evolving service objectives. To address this shift, this article develops an agent-native wireless communication framework that characterizes the interplay between agent intelligence and communication systems. In this framework, the coupling is organized around \emph{agents for communications} and \emph{communications for agents}. For agent-native operation, the architecture is organized around deployable computing infrastructure, programmable open radio access network (O-RAN) software, and controllable communication interfaces. Based on this architecture, \emph{agents for communications} addresses the use of agents in communication-system design and operation, including agent-generated communication software and agent-driven adaptive wireless optimization. On the other side, \emph{communications for agents} addresses wireless service support for agent operation, including network-supported single-agent loops and network-assisted multi-agent coordination. Finally, it outlines promising research directions for measurable, safe, and interoperable deployment of agent-native wireless communications.

</details>


### [109] [Robust Beamforming for Near-Field STAR-RIS-Enabled ISCPT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15954)
*Zahra Rostamikafaki,Francois Chan,Claude D'Amours*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a STAR-RIS-aided near-field ISCPT framework that maximizes harvested power under imperfect CSI, using AO with S-procedure and SROCR for beamforming and penalty-based SCA for RIS coefficients, showing performance gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of integrating sensing, communication, and power transfer in near-field scenarios with imperfect channel knowledge, leveraging STAR-RIS for full-space coverage.

Method: Formulates a robust harvested power maximization problem with constraints on rate, security, and sensing gain. Uses AO: (1) S-procedure for semi-infinite constraints + SROCR for rank-one beamforming; (2) penalty-based SCA for STAR-RIS coefficient optimization.

Result: Simulations in near-field show notable gains in harvested power while meeting secrecy and beampattern targets, outperforming conventional baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed STAR-RIS-aided near-field ISCPT framework effectively enhances harvested power under imperfect CSI while satisfying communication, security, and sensing requirements.
