TL;DR: CAPS is a framework that reduces the cost of pairwise self-verification in LLM reasoning by adaptively allocating verifier compute along two axes (evidence and distribution), achieving superior or comparable results while using ~25% of the verifier-token budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pairwise self-verification is the strongest aggregation primitive for test-time scaling, but it's expensive because each judgment reads two full solutions, and existing methods apply uniform comparisons regardless of informativeness.

Method: CAPS allocates verifier compute non-uniformly along two axes: (1) evidence axis — adapts how much of each candidate the judge sees; (2) distribution axis — adapts how comparisons are spread across candidates. This is structured as a four-stage cascade with an optional rescue subroutine, with a closed-form verifier-token cost.

Result: CAPS outperforms the leading pairwise verifier on 14 of 20 suites across 4 self-verifying models and 5 benchmarks (code and math), using 25.4% of the verifier-token budget on code tasks, and outperforms pointwise self-verification on all 20 suites.

Conclusion: CAPS offers an efficient, interpretable alternative to uniform pairwise verification, with a diagnostic check (verifier accuracy at partial vs. full evidence) that helps determine pre-deployment suitability.

Abstract: Parallel reasoning, where a generator samples many candidate solutions and an aggregator selects the best, is one of the most effective forms of test-time scaling in large language models, and pairwise self-verification has become its strongest aggregation primitive. Yet pairwise verification carries a heavy cost: each judgment reads two complete solutions in full, and existing methods perform tens of such judgments per problem regardless of whether the comparison is informative. We introduce CAPS (Cascaded Adaptive Pairwise Selection), an inference-only framework that allocates verifier compute non-uniformly along two orthogonal axes: an evidence axis that adapts how much of each candidate the judge sees, and a distribution axis that adapts how comparisons are spread across the pool. CAPS instantiates these into a four-stage cascade with an optional rescue subroutine, and admits a closed-form verifier-token cost in which the per-candidate marginal cost is roughly halved relative to uniform full-evidence schedules. On four self-verifying models (Qwen3-14B, GPT-OSS-20B, Qwen3-4B-Instruct/Thinking) and five reasoning benchmarks spanning code (LiveCodeBench-v5/v6, CodeContests) and math (AIME 2025, HMMT 2025), CAPS outperforms the leading pairwise verifier on 14 of 20 suites while using 25.4% of its verifier-token budget on code, and outperforms pointwise self-verification on all 20. The trade-off suites admit an interpretable diagnostic in terms of the verifier's accuracy at partial versus full evidence, providing a concrete pre-deployment check for cascade suitability.

</details>


### [136] [RTL-BenchMT: Dynamic Maintenance of RTL Generation Benchmark Through Agent-Assisted Analysis and Revision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15537)
*Jing Wang,Shang Liu,Hangan Zhou,Zhiyao Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces RTL-BenchMT, an automated agentic framework that dynamically maintains RTL generation benchmarks by identifying and fixing flawed cases and detecting overfitting, reducing the need for manual maintenance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RTL benchmarks suffer from flawed cases and overfitting issues that are difficult to resolve through manual engineering efforts alone, creating a need for automated maintenance.

Method: The authors propose RTL-BenchMT, an automated agentic framework with two key applications: (1) automatically identifying and revising flawed benchmark cases, and (2) automatically detecting and updating overfitting cases.

Result: The framework conducts thorough analysis of flawed and overfitting cases, producing a refined benchmark suite that will be open-sourced.

Conclusion: RTL-BenchMT systematically reduces human maintenance costs for RTL benchmarks by automating the detection and correction of both flawed and overfitting cases.

Abstract: This paper introduces RTL-BenchMT, an agentic framework for dynamically maintaining RTL generation benchmarks. Large Language Models (LLMs) assisted automated RTL generation is one of the most important directions in EDA research. However, current RTL benchmarks face two critical challenges: (1) flawed cases in the benchmarks and (2) overfitting to the benchmarks. Both challenges are difficult to resolve purely by manual engineering effort. To address these issues and systematically reduce human maintenance costs, we propose an automated agentic framework, RTL-BenchMT. RTL-BenchMT focuses on two key applications: (1) automatically identifying and revising flawed benchmark cases and (2) automatically detecting and updating overfitting cases. With the assistance of RTL-BenchMT, we conduct a thorough, in-depth analysis of flawed and overfitting cases and produce a refined benchmark suite that will be open-sourced to the community.

</details>


### [137] [DRS-GUI: Dynamic Region Search for Training-Free GUI Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15542)
*Yichao Liu,Huawen Shen,Liu Yu,Shiyu Liu,Zeyu Chen,Yu Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DRS-GUI is a training-free dynamic region search framework that uses MCTS-based action planning and a UI Perceptor with human-like perceptual actions (Focus, Shift, Scatter) to improve GUI grounding accuracy by 14% on ScreenSpot-Pro.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLM-based GUI agents struggle to accurately ground instruction-relevant elements from high-resolution screenshots cluttered with irrelevant UI components.

Method: DRS-GUI introduces a lightweight UI Perceptor with three human-like perceptual actions (Focus, Shift, Scatter) and an Action Planner based on Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to dynamically explore interfaces and generate region proposals, using a region quality reward to prune redundant UI elements.

Result: Achieves a 14% improvement on ScreenSpot-Pro for both general and GUI-specific MLLMs (Qwen2.5-VL-7B and UGround-V1-7B).

Conclusion: DRS-GUI significantly enhances grounding performance and generalization in a training-free manner, and can be seamlessly integrated into existing MLLMs.

Abstract: GUI agents powered by Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive capability in understanding and executing user instructions. However, accurately grounding instruction-relevant elements from high-resolution screenshots cluttered with irrelevant UI components remains challenging for existing approaches. Inspired by how humans dynamically adjust their perceptual scope to locate task-related regions on complex screens, we propose DRS-GUI, a training-free dynamic region search framework for GUI grounding that can be seamlessly integrated into existing MLLMs. DRS-GUI introduces a lightweight UI Perceptor that performs three human-like perceptual actions (Focus, Shift, and Scatter) to progressively explore the interface and generate region proposals. To dynamically schedule these actions, we further design an Action Planner based on Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). A region quality reward is employed to evaluate and select the highly instruction-relevant region, efficiently pruning redundant UI elements. Experiments demonstrate that DRS-GUI yields a 14\% improvement on ScreenSpot-Pro for general and GUI-specific MLLMs (Qwen2.5-VL-7B and UGround-V1-7B), significantly enhancing grounding performance and generalization.

</details>


### [138] [Position: Artificial Intelligence Needs Meta Intelligence -- the Case for Metacognitive AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15567)
*Sergei Chuprov,Richard D. Lange,Leon Reznik,Paulo Shakarian,Raman Zatsarenko,Dmitrii Korobeinikov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes using metacognition (self-monitoring and adaptive resource allocation) as a design principle for AI, demonstrating its benefits for accuracy, security, and efficiency, with a case study in Federated Learning and a new software framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for more accurate, secure, and efficient AI by leveraging metacognitive strategies inspired by psychology and resource-rational AI, particularly when problem difficulty or cost of mistakes varies.

Method: The authors argue for metacognition as a design principle, identifying challenges in embedding self-monitoring and adaptive resource allocation into AI. They provide a concrete Federated Learning case study and introduce a novel software framework for building metacognition-enabled AI applications.

Result: Improved learning efficiency, effectiveness, and security in a Federated Learning case study, demonstrating the practical translation of metacognitive principles into AI.

Conclusion: Metacognition is a viable general design principle for AI, with open theoretical and implementation problems identified, and the proposed framework enables further community experimentation.

Abstract: This position paper argues for metacognition as a general design principle for creating more accurate, secure, and efficient AI. The metacognitive solution involves systems monitoring their own states and judiciously allocating resources depending on each problem instance's difficulty or cost of mistakes. Drawing inspiration both from past work on resource-rational AI and from well-documented metacognitive strategies in psychology and cognitive science, we identify specific challenges in embedding these strategies into AI design and highlight open theoretical and implementation problems. We showcase these principles through a tangible example of improved learning efficiency, effectiveness, and security in a Federated Learning (FL) case study. We show how these principles can be translated into practice with a novel software framework developed specifically to allow the community to design, deploy, and experiment with metacognition-enabled AI applications.

</details>


### [139] [STAR: A Stage-attributed Triage and Repair framework for RCA Agents in Microservices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15581)
*Junle Wang,Xingchuang Liao,Wenjun Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STAR is a framework that decomposes LLM-based RCA agents into four structured stages, enabling stage-localized bug detection and repair via auditing, routing, and counterfactual evaluation, consistently improving diagnosis accuracy across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based RCA agents are promising for incident diagnosis but suffer from fragile reliability—errors in early stages propagate and corrupt final results. Existing approaches treat failures monolithically, lacking fine-grained debuggability.

Method: STAR decomposes RCA into four stages: Evidence Package (EP), Hypothesis Set (HS), Analysis Structure (AS), and Decision Report (DR). It uses stage-wise auditing, budget-aware Fast/Slow Routing, counterfactual candidate evaluation for decisive stage localization, and stage-specific patch-and-replay repair on LangGraph.

Result: Evaluated on public benchmark and production dataset with two RCA agents and three foundation models, STAR consistently improves root cause localization and fault type classification, identifies decisive faulty stages with high accuracy, and repairs most incorrect traces within 1-2 replay rounds.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling where an RCA agent fails (stage localization) is an effective path toward reliable, debuggable, and self-repairing agentic RCA systems.

Abstract: LLM-based root cause analysis (RCA) agents have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for incident diagnosis in microservice AIOps. However, their reliability remains fragile: an error in early evidence collection, hypothesis formulation, or causal analysis can propagate through the reasoning trace and eventually corrupt the final diagnosis. In this paper, we present \textbf{STAR}, a \emph{Stage-attributed Triage and Repair} framework for repairing erroneous RCA traces. STAR explicitly decomposes an RCA workflow into four structured stages, namely \emph{Evidence Package} (EP), \emph{Hypothesis Set} (HS), \emph{Analysis Structure} (AS), and \emph{Decision Report} (DR), and treats agent failure as a stage-localizable reasoning bug rather than a monolithic end-to-end error. Built on top of LangGraph, STAR performs stage-wise auditing, budget-aware \emph{Fast/Slow Routing}, \emph{decisive stage localization via counterfactual candidate evaluation}, and stage-specific patch-and-replay repair.
  We evaluate STAR on a public large-scale benchmark and a real-world production dataset, using two RCA agent workflows and three foundation models. Experimental results show that STAR consistently improves both root cause localization and fault type classification over strong baselines. Moreover, STAR identifies the decisive faulty stage with high accuracy, repairs most initially incorrect traces within one or two replay rounds, and benefits substantially from both Fast/Slow Routing and counterfactual stage evaluation. These results suggest that explicitly modeling \emph{where} an RCA agent fails is an effective path toward reliable, debuggable, and self-repairing agentic RCA systems.

</details>


### [140] [See Before You Code: Learning Visual Priors for Spatially Aware Educational Animation Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15585)
*Yuejia Li,Ke He,Junheng Li,Shutong Chen,Jingkang Xia,Zhiyue Su,Junchi Zhang,Mang Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OmniManim is a render-feedback-aware framework for generating educational animations with structured visual planning and post-render diagnostics to fix visual defects like overlap, misalignment, and broken continuity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models can generate code for educational animations, but the rendered outputs often suffer from visual defects (overlap, misalignment, broken continuity) that cannot be detected from code alone and only become apparent after execution.

Method: OmniManim introduces a render-feedback-aware framework with shared scene state, explicit visual planning (Vision Agent with coarse-to-fine bounding-box denoising and interpolation-aware optimization), structured post-render diagnostics, and localized repair.

Result: On EduRequire-500, OmniManim improves measured render quality over both single-model baselines and existing multi-agent frameworks. Ablation studies confirm that explicit visual planning components (coarse spatial prior, bounding-box refinement, interpolation-aware optimization) are central to performance gains.

Conclusion: The formalization of render-feedback-aware constrained code generation and the proposed OmniManim framework effectively address visual defects in LLM-generated educational animations through structured planning and post-render feedback.

Abstract: Large language models can generate executable code for educational animations, but the resulting renders often exhibit visual defects, including element overlap, misalignment, and broken animation continuity. These defects cannot be reliably detected from the code alone and become apparent only after execution. We formalize this problem as render-feedback-aware constrained code generation: given a natural language specification, the model must generate executable code whose rendered output satisfies structured quality criteria that can be evaluated only after rendering. To address this problem, we introduce OmniManim, a render-feedback-aware educational animation generation framework built around a shared scene state, explicit visual planning, structured post-render diagnostics, and localized repair. Within OmniManim, the Vision Agent is a task-specific visual planning module: it predicts sparse keyframe layouts with coarse-to-fine bounding-box denoising and optimizes an interpolation-aware objective to reduce intermediate-frame failures induced by downstream animation interpolation. We further construct two datasets, ManimLayout-1K and EduRequire-500, and provide a reproducible evaluation protocol covering executability, instructional quality, visual quality, and efficiency. On EduRequire-500, OmniManim improves measured render quality over both single-model baselines and existing multi-agent frameworks. Systematic ablation studies further verify that explicit visual planning, especially its coarse spatial prior, bounding-box refinement, and interpolation-aware optimization, is central to these gains.

</details>


### [141] [TopoEvo: A Topology-Aware Self-Evolving Multi-Agent Framework for Root Cause Analysis in Microservices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15611)
*Junle Wang,Xingchuang Liao,Wenjun Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TopoEvo is a topology-aware, self-evolving multi-agent framework for root cause analysis in microservices that uses metric-orthogonal multimodal alignment, vector-quantized symptom tokens, and a Hypothesis-Evidence-Test workflow to overcome noise, cascading failures, and topology drift, outperforming LLM-based agents by reducing symptom-amplification bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Root cause analysis in microservices is challenging due to noisy heterogeneous observability data, cascading failure propagation causing symptom-amplification bias, and non-stationary topology drift from autoscaling/updates. Existing LLM-based RCA agents are topology-agnostic and misattribute root causes to salient downstream victims.

Method: TopoEvo introduces (1) Metric-orthogonal Multimodal Alignment (MOMA) for decomposing metric embeddings into complementary subspaces with contrastive alignment of logs/traces, (2) Vector Quantization (VQ) to discretize topology-enhanced states into symptom tokens, (3) a multi-agent Hypothesis-Evidence-Test (HET) workflow for propagation-consistent reasoning, and (4) a Self-Evolving Mechanism for hierarchical incident memory and test-time adaptation under drift.

Result: The paper demonstrates that TopoEvo effectively addresses symptom-amplification bias by leveraging topology-aware representations, discrete symptom tokens for evidence grounding, and multi-agent verification to separate root causes from amplified downstream symptoms, while maintaining robustness through self-evolution under topology drift.

Conclusion: TopoEvo provides a principled framework combining graph representation learning with structured multi-agent reasoning to overcome key limitations of existing LLM-based RCA approaches, offering auditable, propagation-consistent explanations for microservice root cause analysis.

Abstract: Root cause analysis (RCA) in microservices is challenging due to (i) noisy and heterogeneous multimodal observability (metrics, logs, traces), (ii) cascading failure propagation that amplifies downstream symptoms, and (iii) non-stationary topology drift induced by autoscaling and rolling updates. Recent LLM-based RCA agents can generate tool-grounded explanations, yet they often remain topology-agnostic and suffer from \emph{symptom-amplification bias}, misattributing the root cause to salient downstream victims. We propose \textbf{TopoEvo}, a topology-aware self-evolving multi-agent framework that couples graph representation learning with structured, topology-constrained reasoning. TopoEvo first introduces \emph{Metric-orthogonal Multimodal Alignment} (MOMA), which decomposes metric embeddings into complementary subspaces and contrastively aligns logs and traces to reduce modality redundancy and sparsity, yielding stable node representations for graph encoding. It then applies \emph{Vector Quantization} (VQ) to discretize topology-enhanced states into auditable \emph{symptom tokens} with a symptom lexicon, enabling reliable retrieval and token-level evidence grounding. On top of these discrete topology cues, TopoEvo performs a multi-agent \emph{Hypothesis--Evidence--Test} (HET) workflow to explicitly verify propagation-consistent explanations and separate initiating anomalies from amplified downstream symptoms. Finally, a \emph{Self-Evolving Mechanism} refreshes hierarchical incident memory and performs conservative test-time adaptation with high-confidence pseudo-labels to maintain robustness under drift.

</details>


### [142] [ColPackAgent: Agent-Skill-Guided Hard-Particle Monte Carlo Workflows for Colloidal Packing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15625)
*Lijie Ding,Changwoo Do*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ColPackAgent is an agent framework that autonomously runs colloidal packing simulations via an MCP tool server and agent skill, enabling reliable execution of structured Monte Carlo simulation workflows across different modes (interactive, autonomous, autoresearch), with demonstrated applications in cube packing, binary disk-capsule systems, and hard-disk freezing transitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose LLM agents tend to describe simulation workflows rather than reliably execute them when lacking dedicated tools and workflow instructions. This work aims to bridge that gap by providing a structured agent framework for colloidal packing simulations.

Method: ColPackAgent integrates a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server exposing a custom colpack Python package (wrapping HOOMD-blue hard-particle Monte Carlo) with a four-stage workflow skill. It operates in three modes: interactive (with human feedback), autonomous (from end-to-end prompt), and autoresearch (following a program file).

Result: Demonstrated across multiple examples (cube particles in 3D, binary disk-capsule in 2D, 2D hard-disk freezing transition). A 17-stage prompt benchmark across multiple LLMs provides stage-level reliability analysis of workflow following.

Conclusion: Pairing a domain Python package with MCP tools and a portable agent skill offers a practical route to turn simulation toolkits into agent-assisted research workflows.

Abstract: We introduce ColPackAgent, an agent framework that autonomously runs Monte Carlo simulations of colloidal packing through a Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool server and an agent skill, whether as a standalone agent or inside an existing agent system. By harnessing the MCP server and agent skill, ColPackAgent executes a structured workflow for colloidal packing simulations, which are central to studies of phase behavior, self-assembly, and materials design. Without dedicated simulation tools and workflow instructions, general-purpose Large Language Model (LLM) agents tend to describe such workflows rather than execute them reliably. The MCP server exposes a custom-built colpack Python package that wraps HOOMD-blue hard-particle Monte Carlo, and the skill encodes a four-stage workflow contract. ColPackAgent can carry out the workflow interactively with human feedback, autonomously from an end-to-end prompt, or as autoresearch following a provided program file. We demonstrate the system in different modes with several colloidal packing simulation examples such as cube particles in 3D, a binary system of disks and capsules in 2D, and the 2D hard-disk freezing transition using autoresearch. We also compare model performance on this workflow across a panel of LLMs with 17 stage-specific prompts. This benchmark provides a stage-level check of how reliably different models follow the setup, planning, and analysis workflow. Together, these results show that pairing a domain Python package with MCP tools and a portable agent skill provides a practical route for turning a simulation toolkit into an agent-assisted research workflow.

</details>


### [143] [PRISM: Prompt Reliability via Iterative Simulation and Monitoring for Enterprise Conversational AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15665)
*Keshava Chaitanya,Jahnavi Gundakaram*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PRISM is a closed-loop framework that treats prompt engineering as a continuous reliability problem, automatically generating tests, simulating conversations, diagnosing failures, and repairing prompts on a daily basis to combat LLM behavioral drift. Evaluated on 35 enterprise agents, it reduced authoring time from 2 days to under 30 minutes and achieved 99% production reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prompt optimization frameworks treat prompt quality as a one-time compile-time problem, leaving the critical issue of detecting and repairing prompt regressions caused by silent LLM behavior changes (behavioral drift) in production deployments unaddressed.

Method: PRISM takes plain-language requirements, configured tools/memory variables, and an initial draft prompt; automatically generates test cases from requirements; simulates multi-turn conversations against a platform-faithful LLM environment; evaluates pass/fail using LLM-as-judge; diagnoses root causes; and surgically repairs the prompt, iterating until all tests pass. It runs on a scheduled daily basis.

Result: Evaluated on 35 enterprise agents over 3 weeks on the Yellow.ai V3 platform: median prompt authoring time reduced from 2 days to under 30 minutes; 99% production reliability achieved; production regressions from LLM behavioral drift identified and repaired within a 24-hour detection window.

Conclusion: Continuous, simulation-driven prompt optimization is both tractable and necessary for reliable enterprise conversational AI at scale.

Abstract: Deploying large language model (LLM)-driven conversational agents in enterprise settings requires prompts that are simultaneously correct at launch and resilient to the non-deterministic behavioral drift that characterizes production LLM deployments. Existing prompt optimization frameworks address prompt quality as a one-time compile-time problem, leaving open the equally critical question of how to detect and repair prompt regressions caused by silent LLM behavior changes over time. We present PRISM (Prompt Reliability via Iterative Simulation and Monitoring), a closed-loop framework that treats prompt engineering as a continuous reliability engineering problem rather than a one-time authorship task. PRISM takes as input plain-language agent requirements, a set of configured tools and memory variables, and an initial draft prompt. It automatically generates test cases from requirements, simulates full multi-turn conversations against a platform-faithful LLM environment, evaluates pass/fail using an LLM-as-judge, diagnoses root causes of failures, and surgically repairs the prompt -- iterating until all tests pass. Critically, PRISM is designed to run on a scheduled basis (daily), treating LLM behavioral drift as a first-class reliability concern. We evaluate PRISM across 35 enterprise conversational agents over a three-week deployment period on the Yellow.ai V3 platform. PRISM reduces median prompt authoring time from 2 days to under 30 minutes, achieves 99% production reliability across all evaluated agents, and successfully identifies and repairs production regressions caused by LLM behavioral drift within a 24-hour detection window. Our results suggest that continuous, simulation-driven prompt optimization is both tractable and necessary for reliable enterprise conversational AI at scale.

</details>


### [144] [Nudging Beyond the Comfort Zone: Efficient Strategy-Guided Exploration for RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15726)
*Chanuk Lee,Sangwoo Park,Minki Kang,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NudgeRL introduces Strategy Nudging for structured, diversity-driven exploration in RLVR by conditioning rollouts on lightweight strategy contexts, outperforming standard GRPO with up to 8× larger rollout budgets on math benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR's effectiveness is limited by exploration—policy only improves on trajectories it has already sampled. Brute-force scaling of rollouts is computationally expensive, and existing methods provide limited control over what is explored.

Method: Proposes (1) Strategy Nudging: conditioning rollouts on lightweight strategy-level contexts to induce diverse reasoning trajectories without oracle supervision; (2) a unified objective decomposing reward into inter- and intra-context components with a distillation objective to transfer behaviors back to the base policy.

Result: NudgeRL outperforms standard GRPO with up to 8× larger rollout budgets and outperforms oracle-guided RL baseline on average across five challenging math benchmarks.

Conclusion: Structured, context-driven exploration is an efficient and scalable alternative to brute-force rollout scaling and feasibility-oriented methods based on privileged information.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a scalable paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. However, its effectiveness is fundamentally limited by exploration: the policy can only improve on trajectories it has already sampled. While increasing the number of rollouts alleviates this issue, such brute-force scaling is computationally expensive, and existing approaches that modify the optimization objective provide limited control over what is explored. In this work, we propose NudgeRL, a framework for structured and diversity-driven exploration in RLVR. Our approach introduces Strategy Nudging, which conditions each rollout on lightweight, strategy-level contexts to induce diverse reasoning trajectories without relying on expensive oracle supervision. To effectively learn from such structured exploration, we further propose a unified objective, which decomposes the reward signal into inter- and intra-context components and incorporates a distillation objective to transfer discovered behaviors back to the base policy. Empirically, NudgeRL outperforms standard GRPO with up to 8 times larger rollout budgets, while outperforming oracle-guided RL baseline on average across five challenging math benchmarks. These results demonstrate that structured, context-driven exploration can serve as an efficient and scalable alternative to both brute-force rollout scaling and feasibility-oriented methods based on privileged information. Our code is available at https://github.com/tally0818/NudgeRL.

</details>


### [145] [Can We Trust AI-Inferred User States. A Psychometric Framework for Validating the Reliability of Users States Classification by LLMs in Operational Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15734)
*Izabella Krzeminska,Michal Butkiewicz,Ewa Komkowska*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper empirically tests whether LLM-based metrics for assessing user states in adaptive systems are psychometrically reliable at the individual score level. Using replication evaluations across three bimodal LLMs, it finds that only 31 of 213 metrics met stability criteria, demonstrating that reliability cannot be assumed—even though some unstable metrics retain utility in aggregated analyses. A replicable evaluation framework is proposed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The widespread use of LLMs to assess user states in conversational/adaptive systems assumes that individual-level metric scores are stable and interpretable—an assumption that lacks empirical validation. This paper aims to test that assumption using psychometric reliability standards.

Method: Replication evaluation procedures were conducted across three bimodal large language models (GPT-4o audio, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash). Both individual score reliability and aggregated reliability were analyzed to distinguish metrics suitable for real-time adaptation from those only valuable in aggregate.

Result: Only 31 out of 213 metrics met reliability criteria. Individual score stability was lacking across models, preventing real-time interpretation. However, individually unstable metrics still showed analytical utility in post-hoc studies (e.g., identifying rules governing interactions and relationships with satisfaction, trust, and engagement).

Conclusion: Metric reliability cannot be assumed as a default property in interpretive domains. The paper proposes a replicable evaluation framework to enable measurable assessments of metric applicability, supporting more responsible AI design of adaptive systems that require explicit validation and ongoing monitoring of reliability.

Abstract: The use of large language models to assess user states in conversational and adaptive systems is based on the assumption that the metrics used for such assessment are stable and interpretable at the level of individual scores. This paper empirically tests this assumption, focusing on the psychometric reliability of artificial intelligence (AI) measures of user states.
  This study employed replication evaluation procedures to assess the repeatability of a broad set of metrics across three different bimodal large language models (GPT-4o audio, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash). Analyses include both individual score reliability and aggregated reliability, allowing us to distinguish metrics potentially useful for real-time adaptation from those that retain their value only in aggregated analyses.
  The results demonstrate that metric reliability cannot be considered a default property in interpretive domains. The lack of stability at the level of individual scores precludes the interpretation of such scores as indicators of user state in real-time adaptive systems, even if these metrics demonstrate stability after aggregation. At the same time, the study indicates that individually unstable metrics can retain analytical utility in post-hoc studies, identifying rules governing interactions and their relationships with user experience parameters such as satisfaction, trust, and engagement.
  The main contribution of this work, besides quantifying the severity of the problem (only 31 of 213 metrics met the criteria), is the proposal of a replicable evaluation framework, enabling measurable evaluations of metric applicability. This approach supports more responsible AI design of adaptive systems, in which the interpretation of results requires explicit validation of reliability and monitoring for violations over time.

</details>


### [146] [ALSO: Adversarial Online Strategy Optimization for Social Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15768)
*Xiang Li,Liping Yi,Mingze Kong,Min Zhang,Zhongxiang Dai,QingHua Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ALSO is a framework for online strategy optimization in multi-agent social simulation, treating multi-turn interactions as adversarial bandit problems with a neural surrogate for reward prediction, outperforming static baselines on Sotopia.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based social agents rely on static personas and methods like offline RL or external planners assume stationarity, failing in non-stationary multi-agent social environments where strategies must dynamically adapt.

Method: Formulates multi-turn interaction as an adversarial bandit problem (static personas + dynamic strategy instructions as arms), and uses a lightweight neural surrogate to predict rewards from interaction histories for sample-efficient online adaptation.

Result: ALSO consistently outperforms static baselines and existing optimization methods in dynamic environments on the Sotopia benchmark.

Conclusion: Adversarial online strategy optimization is effective for building robust social agents that can dynamically adapt in non-stationary multi-agent settings.

Abstract: Social simulation provides a compelling testbed for studying social intelligence, where agents interact through multi-turn dialogues under evolving contexts and strategically adapting opponents. Such environments are inherently non-stationary, requiring agents to dynamically adjust their strategies over time. However, most Large Language Model (LLM) based social agents rely on static personas, while existing approaches for enhancing social intelligence, such as offline reinforcement learning or external planners, are ill-suited to these settings, typically assuming stationarity and incurring substantial training overhead. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{ALSO} (\textbf{A}dversarial on\textbf{L}ine \textbf{S}trategy \textbf{O}ptimization), the first framework for online strategy optimization in multi-agent social simulation. ALSO advances social adaptation through two key contributions. (1) ALSO formulates multi-turn interaction as an adversarial bandit problem, where combinations of static personas and dynamic strategy instructions are treated as arms, providing a principled solution to non-stationarity without relying on environmental stability assumptions. (2) To predict rewards and generalize sparse feedback in multi-turn dialogues, ALSO introduces a lightweight neural surrogate to predict rewards from interaction histories, enabling sample-efficient exploration and continuous online adaptation. Experiments on the Sotopia benchmark demonstrate that ALSO consistently outperforms static baselines and existing optimization methods in dynamic environments, validating the effectiveness of adversarial online strategy optimization for building robust social agents.

</details>


### [147] [SaaS-Bench: Can Computer-Use Agents Leverage Real-World SaaS to Solve Professional Workflows?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15777)
*Kean Shi,Zihang Li,Tianyi Ma,Zengji Tu,Jialong Wu,Xinbo Xu,Qingyao Yang,Ruoyu Wu,Weichu Xie,Ming Wu,Jason Zeng,Michael Heinrich,Elvis Zhang,Liang Chen,Kuan Li,Baobao Chang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SaaS-Bench is a new benchmark evaluating LLM-based Computer-Using Agents on 106 realistic, long-horizon tasks across 23 SaaS systems in 6 domains, where even the best models complete fewer than 4% of tasks end-to-end.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing web/GUI agent benchmarks rely on simplified, isolated, or short-horizon tasks that fail to assess agent capabilities in realistic professional workflows. SaaS environments are ideal for evaluation as they involve dynamic states, cross-app coordination, domain knowledge, and long-horizon dependencies.

Method: The authors built SaaS-Bench using 23 deployable SaaS systems across 6 professional domains, with 106 realistic tasks. Tasks require long-horizon execution, include both text-only and multimodal settings, and are evaluated via weighted verification checkpoints measuring both strict completion and partial progress.

Result: Representative LLM-based agents perform poorly, with the strongest model completing fewer than 4% of tasks end-to-end, exposing major limitations in planning, state tracking, cross-application context maintenance, and error recovery.

Conclusion: Current LLM-based agents are far from capable of handling realistic SaaS workflows, and SaaS-Bench provides a challenging and realistic foundation for future CUA research.

Abstract: Computer-Using Agents (CUAs) are rapidly extending large language models (LLMs) beyond text-based reasoning toward action execution in more complex environments, such as web browsers and graphical user interfaces (GUIs). However, existing web and GUI agent benchmarks often rely on simplified settings, isolated tasks, or short-horizon interactions, making it difficult to assess capabilities of agents in realistic professional workflows. Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) environments are a natural choice for CUA evaluation, as they host a large share of modern digital work and naturally involve dynamic system states, cross-application coordination, domain-specific knowledge, and long-horizon dependencies. To this end, we introduce SaaS-Bench, a benchmark built on 23 deployable SaaS systems across six professional domains, containing 106 tasks grounded in realistic work scenarios. These tasks require long-horizon execution, cover both text-only and multimodal settings, and are evaluated with weighted verification checkpoints that measure strict task completion and partial progress. Experiments show that representative LLM-based agents struggle on SaaS-Bench, with even the strongest model completing fewer than 4% of tasks end-to-end, exposing limitations in planning, state tracking, cross-application context maintenance, and error recovery. Code are available at https://github.com/UniPat-AI/SaaS-Bench for reproduction.

</details>


### [148] [Agentic Discovery of Neural Architectures: AIRA-Compose and AIRA-Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15871)
*Alberto Pepe,Chien-Yu Lin,Despoina Magka,Bilge Acun,Yannan Nellie Wu,Anton Protopopov,Carole-Jean Wu,Yoram Bachrach*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents two frameworks (AIRA-Compose and AIRA-Design) where LLM agents autonomously design foundation model architectures. AIRA-Compose discovers 14 architectures (Transformer-based and hybrid) that outperform Llama 3.2 by up to 3.8% and scale up to 71% faster. AIRA-Design produces attention mechanisms and training scripts reaching within 2.3-2.6% of human SOTA on long-range tasks. The work demonstrates AI agents can match/surpass hand-designed baselines, marking progress toward recursive self-improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether LLM agents can autonomously design foundation model architectures (beyond standard Transformers) as a step toward recursive self-improvement, reducing the need for human expert design.

Method: A dual-framework approach: (1) AIRA-Compose uses 11 agents for high-level architecture search under a 24-hour budget, evaluating million-parameter candidates and extrapolating to 350M-3B scales; (2) AIRA-Design uses 20 agents for low-level mechanistic implementation, writing novel attention mechanisms and training scripts.

Result: 14 architectures across two families (AIRAformers, AIRAhybrids) discovered. At 1B scale, they outperform Llama 3.2 by 2.4-3.8%. AIRAformer-C scales 54-71% faster than baselines; AIRAhybrid-C outscales Nemotron-2 by 23%. On LRA, agent designs reach within 2.3-2.6% of human SOTA. Greedy Opus 4.5 achieves 0.968 validation BPD on Autoresearch.

Conclusion: AI agents can autonomously discover architectures and algorithmic optimizations that match or surpass hand-designed baselines, establishing a paradigm for discovering next-generation foundation models and marking progress toward recursive self-improvement.

Abstract: Toward recursive self-improvement, we investigate LLM agents autonomously designing foundation models beyond standard Transformers. We introduce a dual-framework approach: AIRA-Compose for high-level architecture search, and AIRA-Design for low-level mechanistic implementation. AIRA-Compose uses 11 agents to explore fundamental computational primitives under a 24-hour budget. Agents evaluate million-parameter candidates, extrapolating top designs to 350M, 1B, and 3B scales. This yields 14 architectures across two families: AIRAformers (Transformer-based) and AIRAhybrids (Transformer-Mamba). Pre-trained at 1B scale, these consistently outperform Llama 3.2 and Composer-found baselines. On downstream tasks, AIRAformer-D and AIRAhybrid-D improve accuracy by 2.4% and 3.8% over Llama 3.2. Furthermore, AIRA-Compose finds models with highly efficient scaling frontiers: AIRAformer-C scales 54% and 71% faster than Llama 3.2 and Composer's best Transformer, while AIRAhybrid-C outscales Nemotron-2 by 23% and Composer's best hybrid by 37%. AIRA-Design tasks 20 agents with writing novel attention mechanisms for long-range dependencies and high-performing training scripts. On the Long Range Arena benchmark, agent-designed architectures reach within 2.3% and 2.6% of human state-of-the-art on document matching and text classification. On the Autoresearch benchmark, Greedy Opus 4.5 achieves 0.968 validation bits-per-byte under a fixed time budget, surpassing the published minimum. Together, these frameworks show AI agents can autonomously discover architectures and algorithmic optimizations matching or surpassing hand-designed baselines. This establishes a powerful paradigm for discovering next-generation foundation models, marking a clear step toward recursive self-improvement.

</details>


### [149] [Imperfect World Models are Exploitable](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15960)
*Logan Mondal Bhamidipaty,Esmeralda S. Whitammer,David Abel,Mykel J. Kochenderfer,Subramanian Ramamoorthy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper formally defines and analyzes model exploitation in RL, showing it's akin to reward hacking but provably harder to avoid, especially in large policy sets, and introduces a relaxed notion with a safe horizon for avoidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formally define model exploitation in reinforcement learning and understand its relationship to reward hacking, particularly examining whether similar inevitability results hold.

Method: Develops a general theory connecting reward hacking and model exploitation, analyzes inevitability conditions across policy sets of varying sizes, and introduces a relaxed exploitation notion with a safe horizon.

Result: Model exploitation is essentially unavoidable on large policy sets; the conditions preventing unhackability in finite sets do not preclude exploitation; a relaxed notion with a safe horizon is introduced.

Conclusion: The findings establish a formal bridge between reward hacking and model exploitation, revealing fundamental limits on safe planning in world models.

Abstract: We propose a novel definition of model exploitation in reinforcement learning. Informally, a world model is exploitable if it implies that one policy should be strictly preferred over another while the environment's true transition model implies the reverse. We analogize our definition with a prior characterization of reward hacking but show that the associated proof of inevitability does not transfer to exploitation. To overcome this obstruction, we develop a general theory of reward hacking and model exploitation that proves that exploitation is essentially unavoidable on large policy sets and yields the corresponding claim for hacking as a special case. Unfortunately, we also find that the conditions that guarantee unhackability in finite policy sets have no counterpart that precludes exploitation. Consequently, we introduce a relaxed notion of exploitation and derive a safe horizon within which it can be avoided. Taken together, our results establish a formal bridge between reward hacking and model exploitation and elucidate the limits of safe planning in world models.

</details>


### [150] [PAGER: Bridging the Semantic-Execution Gap in Point-Precise Geometric GUI Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15963)
*Jingxuan Wei,Xi Bai,Shan Liu,Caijun Jia,Zheng Sun,Xinglong Xu,Siyuan Li,Linzhuang Sun,Bihui Yu,Conghui He,Cheng Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces precision-sensitive GUI tasks requiring point-level accuracy, presents PAGE Bench (4,906 problems, 224K+ pixel-level actions), and proposes PAGER—a topology-aware agent that uses dependency-structured planning and reinforcement learning to bridge the Semantic-Execution Gap (88%+ action type accuracy but <6% task success), achieving 4.1x higher success than general baselines and over 62% step success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GUI agents rely on a region-tolerant paradigm where nearby pixels in the same component are valid. However, precise geometric construction (e.g., drawing diagrams) requires point-level accuracy and fails under this assumption. Local coordinate errors cause cascading topological failures due to ontological dependencies among geometric primitives. This gap motivates the need for a new benchmark and approach for precision-sensitive GUI tasks.

Method: PAGER is a topology-aware agent that decomposes construction into (1) dependency-structured planning and pixel-level execution, (2) pixel-grounded supervised tuning to establish executable action grammar, and (3) precision-aligned reinforcement learning that mitigates rollout-induced exposure bias through state-conditioned geometric feedback.

Result: General multimodal models exceed 88% action type accuracy yet remain below 6% task success (Semantic-Execution Gap). PAGER delivers 4.1x higher task success than the strongest general baseline and raises step success from below 9% (GUI-specialized agents) to over 62%, achieving state-of-the-art for point-precise GUI control.

Conclusion: Precision-sensitive GUI tasks expose a fundamental limitation of current GUI agents. The proposed PAGE Bench and PAGER demonstrate that topology-aware decomposition and precision-aligned learning are effective solutions, substantially narrowing the Semantic-Execution Gap and establishing new benchmarks for point-level GUI control.

Abstract: Large vision-language models have significantly advanced GUI agents, enabling executable interaction across web, mobile, and desktop interfaces. Yet these gains largely rely on a forgiving region-tolerant paradigm, where many nearby pixels inside the same component remain valid. Precise geometric construction breaks this assumption: actions must land on points in continuous canvas space rather than tolerant regions. Because geometric primitives carry ontological dependencies, a local coordinate error can induce cascading topological failures that distort downstream objects and invalidate the final construction. We identify this regime as precision-sensitive GUI tasks, requiring point-level accuracy, geometry-aware verification, and robustness to dependency-driven error propagation. To benchmark it, we introduce PAGE Bench, with 4,906 problems and over 224K process-supervised, pixel-level GUI actions. We further propose PAGER, a topology-aware agent that decomposes construction into dependency-structured planning and pixel-level execution. Pixel-grounded supervised tuning establishes executable action grammar, while precision-aligned reinforcement learning mitigates rollout-induced exposure bias through state-conditioned geometric feedback. Experiments reveal a pronounced Semantic-Execution Gap: general multimodal models can exceed 88% action type accuracy yet remain below 6% task success. PAGER closes this gap, delivering 4.1x higher task success than the strongest evaluated general baseline and raising step success rate from below 9% for GUI-specialized agents to over 62%, establishing a new state of the art for point-precise GUI control.

</details>


### [151] [Deterministic Event-Graph Substrates as World Models for Counterfactual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15967)
*Fabio Rovai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces event-graph substrates—world models using append-only RDF triple logs with structured intervention vocabularies for exact counterfactual queries. The approach proves a duality between explanatory and counterfactual queries, achieves strong results on CLEVRER (exceeding NS-DR symbolic oracle) and on a new Smallville counterfactual benchmark (exceeding Llama-3.1-8B by 18.80 points), all with a domain-agnostic, inspectable, 1,400-line interpreter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current world models for counterfactual reasoning often rely on learned components that lack inspectability, exactness, and cross-domain transferability. The authors aim to develop a substrate that is fully interpretable at the triple level, supports precise counterfactuals, and generalizes without any learned components.

Method: The method formalizes event-graph substrates as append-only RDF triple logs with structured intervention vocabularies. A duality theorem reduces both explanatory and counterfactual queries to the same causal-ancestor traversal. The implementation is a 1,400-line CLEVRER-DSL interpreter built on a domain-agnostic substrate runtime.

Result: On CLEVRER (n=75,618), the substrate exceeds the NS-DR symbolic oracle on all four question categories (by 9.89, 20.26, 17.65, and 0.80 percentage points) and exceeds the parametric ALOE baseline on descriptive and explanatory categories. On the new twin-EventLog benchmark (500 Park-canonical Smallville specifications), it exceeds Llama-3.1-8B with full context by 18.80 points in joint accuracy.

Conclusion: Event-graph substrates offer a promising alternative to learned world models for counterfactual reasoning, providing exactness, inspectability, and domain transferability without learned components. The duality reduction between query types enables efficient implementation, and empirical results demonstrate strong performance on both synthetic (CLEVRER) and naturalistic (Smallville) benchmarks.

Abstract: We study event-graph substrates: a class of world models that represent agent state as an append-only log of typed RDF triples and answer counterfactual queries by forking the log under a structured intervention vocabulary. Substrates are inspectable at the triple level, support exact counterfactuals, and transfer across domains without learned components. We formalize the class, prove a duality between explanatory and counterfactual queries that reduces both to the same causal-ancestor traversal, and evaluate a 1,400-line CLEVRER-DSL interpreter atop a domain-agnostic substrate runtime at full CLEVRER validation scale (n=75,618). The substrate exceeds the NS-DR symbolic oracle on all four per-question categories (by 9.89, 20.26, 17.65, and 0.80 percentage points), and exceeds the parametric ALOE baseline on descriptive and explanatory while lagging on predictive and counterfactual. We also introduce twin-EventLog, a 500-specification Park-canonical Smallville counterfactual benchmark on which the substrate exceeds Llama-3.1-8B with full context by 18.80 points joint accuracy.

</details>


### [152] [Learning Bilevel Policies over Symbolic World Models for Long-Horizon Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15975)
*Dillon Z. Chen,Till Hofmann,Toryn Q. Klassen,Sheila A. McIlraith*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BISON combines low-level imitation learning for manipulation with high-level symbolic planning, using bilevel policies to achieve efficient long-horizon planning that generalizes to large-scale problems beyond what end-to-end methods can handle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning excels at fine motor control but struggles with long-horizon planning, while symbolic abstractions enable efficient long-horizon reasoning. The authors aim to combine both strengths to build embodied AI agents capable of reliable long-horizon planning.

Method: BISON uses bilevel policies (π^hl, π^ll): a neural policy π^ll learned from low-level demonstrations for manipulation, and a high-level symbolic policy π^hl constructed from symbolic abstractions of demonstrations with inductive generalization, enabling hierarchical planning.

Result: BISON generalizes to longer horizons and more objects than VLA and end-to-end methods on extended MetaWorld benchmarks, with superior time and memory efficiency. HL policies can solve problems with 10,000 objects in under a minute when ignoring LL execution.

Conclusion: Combining low-level imitation learning with high-level symbolic planning via bilevel policies is an effective and efficient approach for long-horizon embodied AI planning, outperforming purely end-to-end methods in scalability and generalization.

Abstract: We tackle the challenge of building embodied AI agents that can reliably solve long-horizon planning problems. Imitation learning from demonstrations has shown itself to be effective in training robots to solve a diversity of complex tasks requiring fine motor control and manipulation over low-level (LL), continuous environments. Yet, it remains a difficult endeavour to generate long-horizon plans from imitation learning alone. In contrast, high-level (HL), symbolic abstractions facilitate efficient and interpretable long-horizon planning. We propose to combine the strengths of LL imitation learning for manipulation and control, and HL symbolic abstractions for long-horizon planning. We realise this idea via \emph{bilevel policies} of the form $(π^{\mathrm{hl}}, π^{\mathrm{ll}})$, consisting of a neural policy $π^{\mathrm{ll}}$ learned from LL demonstrations, and an HL symbolic policy $π^{\mathrm{hl}}$ that is constructed from symbolic abstractions of the LL demonstrations combined with inductive generalisation. We implement these ideas in the BISON system. Experiments on extended MetaWorld benchmarks demonstrate that BISON generalises to long horizons and problems with greater numbers of objects than those solved by VLA and end-to-end methods, and is more time and memory efficient in training and inference. Notably, when ignoring LL execution, BISON's HL policies can solve HL problems with 10,000 relevant objects in under a minute. Project page: https://dillonzchen.github.io/bison

</details>


### [153] [Petri Net Induced Heuristic Search for Resource Constrained Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15983)
*Ido Lublin,Dor Atzmon,Izack Cohen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper formulates RCPSP as optimal search over a Petri net reachability graph, solves it with A* using a combined critical-path/resource heuristic, and shows it outperforms MIP solvers on PSPLIB benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an exact method for RCPSP that outperforms MIP-based approaches by leveraging heuristic search over Petri net state spaces.

Method: Formulates RCPSP as search over a Timed Transition Petri Net with Resources reachability graph, uses A* with a consistent heuristic combining critical path and resource lower bounds.

Result: Outperforms MIP baselines (SCIP, CBC) in success rate and solve time on PSPLIB benchmarks; A* degrades with resource tightness, MIP with formulation size.

Conclusion: Heuristic search with Petri net semantics is a viable exact approach for RCPSP, with complementary strengths to MIP depending on problem characteristics.

Abstract: We formulate the Resource-Constrained Project Scheduling Problem (RCPSP) as optimal search over the reachability graph of a Timed Transition Petri Net with Resources, using relative-delay tokens so that scheduling decisions correspond to transition firings in the induced state space. We solve the resulting problem with $A^*$ guided by a heuristic that combines Critical Path and resource-based lower bounds, and prove that it is consistent under our token-based time semantics. Experiments on the PSPLIB benchmarks show that the approach outperforms strong exact Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MIP) baselines (SCIP, CBC) in both success rate and solve time. Per-instance analysis shows that heuristic search and MIP degrade along independent axes, resource tightness for $A^*$ and formulation size for MIP, with resource strength mediating which solver benefits from scale.

</details>


### [154] [ScreenSearch: Uncertainty-Aware OS Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16024)
*Michael Solodko,Justin Wagle*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ScreenSearch is a system for desktop GUI exploration that combines structural screen retrieval and deduplication with ambiguity-aware PUCT graph-bandit search. It collects over 1M screenshots across 11 desktop apps, revealing a novelty-ambiguity trade-off where some policies reduce ambiguity but discover little frontier, showing that ambiguity reduction alone is insufficient as an exploration objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Desktop GUI agents operate under partial observability where visually similar screens can correspond to different workflow states, making locally plausible actions lead to sharply different outcomes. This requires both expanding the reachable frontier and reducing ambiguity before committing to actions.

Method: ScreenSearch uses: (1) structural screen retrieval converting UIA trees into location-aware features with sparse token search and metadata filters; (2) a shared deduplicated state graph across VM workers; (3) ambiguity-aware PUCT graph-bandit using matched-action outcome dispersion as an ambiguity signal; (4) frontier rewards to drive exploration and replay-start policy evaluation.

Result: Across 11 desktop applications, ScreenSearch collected over 1M screenshots and over 30K deduplicated states, yielding large exploration corpora with cross-application and within-application diversity. The study reveals a novelty-ambiguity trade-off, and appendix ablations show stronger proposal priors improve unique-state discovery.

Conclusion: State identity, proposal quality, and ambiguity-aware search all matter for deciding when to probe and when to commit. Ambiguity reduction alone is not a sufficient exploration objective.

Abstract: Desktop GUI agents operate under partial observability: visually similar screens can correspond to different underlying workflow states, so locally plausible actions can lead to sharply different outcomes. We frame this as a problem of computer/OS state exploration, where effective behavior requires both expanding the reachable frontier and reducing ambiguity before committing. We present ScreenSearch, a system that combines structural screen retrieval and deduplication with an ambiguity-aware PUCT graph-bandit for large-scale desktop exploration. The retrieval layer converts UIA trees into location-aware structural features, indexes related screens through sparse token search and metadata filters, and maintains a shared deduplicated state graph across VM workers. On top of this graph, we define a scalable ambiguity signal based on matched-action outcome dispersion. If similar screens produce different next states under the same action signature, the state should be probed further rather than treated as resolved. We use this signal together with frontier rewards to drive large-scale exploration and replay-start policy evaluation over the shared graph. Across 11 desktop applications, ScreenSearch collects over 1M screenshots and over 30K deduplicated states, yielding large exploration corpora with substantial cross-application and within-application diversity. On a fixed replay-start slice, we observe a clear novelty--ambiguity trade-off: some policies reduce ambiguity quickly while discovering little frontier. Ambiguity reduction alone is therefore not a sufficient exploration objective. Appendix ablations show that stronger proposal priors can materially improve unique-state discovery during corpus building. These results suggest that state identity, proposal quality, and ambiguity-aware search all matter when deciding when to probe and when to commit.

</details>


### [155] [Reasoners or Translators? Contamination-aware Evaluation and Neuro-Symbolic Robustness in Tax Law](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16052)
*Parisa Kordjamshidi,Samer Aslan,Madhavan Seshadri,Leslie Barrett,Enrico Santus*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether LLMs' legal reasoning performance is genuine or due to data contamination, and compares monolithic LLMs with neuro-symbolic frameworks, finding the latter more reliable, robust, and generalizable for tax law reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLMs' performance in automated legal reasoning reflects genuine reasoning ability or is inflated by data contamination, and to find more reliable alternatives.

Method: The authors conduct a contamination detection protocol, build a novel test suite with case and rule variations for generalization probing, and systematically compare monolithic LLMs with hybrid neuro-symbolic systems that translate statute text into formal representations for symbolic inference.

Result: Performance of LLMs can be inflated by contamination. Legal reasoning is inherently compositional. Neuro-symbolic frameworks demonstrate superior reliability, robustness, and generalization to unobserved situations compared to monolithic LLMs.

Conclusion: Neuro-symbolic frameworks provide a more reliable and robust foundation for legal AI, with better generalization to unseen documents and novel legal scenarios.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have significantly enhanced automated legal reasoning. Yet, it remains unclear whether their performance reflects genuine legal reasoning ability or artifacts of data contamination. We present a comprehensive empirical study of tax law reasoning approaches and implement a contamination detection protocol to rigorously assess LLM reliability. We show that performance can be inflated by contamination. Building on this analysis, we conduct a systematic evaluation, comparing monolithic LLMs with hybrid systems that translate statutory text into formal representations and delegate inference to symbolic solvers. We build a novel test suite designed to probe generalization to unseen documents via case and rule variations. Our findings indicate that legal reasoning is inherently compositional and that neuro-symbolic frameworks offer a more reliable and robust foundation for legal AI, as well as improved generalization to unobserved situations.

</details>


### [156] [Sign-Separated Finite-Time Error Analysis of Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16103)
*Donghwan Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper provides a sign-separated finite-time error analysis for constant step-size Q-learning, revealing a max-induced asymmetry where positive errors propagate via Bellman maximum while negative errors are bounded by an optimal-policy LTI system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the asymmetric error dynamics in Q-learning, particularly the overestimation phenomenon caused by the Bellman max operator.

Method: Decomposes Q-learning error into positive and negative parts using switching-system representation; negative part bounded by LTI system with optimal policy, positive part controlled by linear switching system.

Result: Negative-side LTI certificate is no slower than positive-side switching certificate; max-induced asymmetry identified; finite-time bounds provided for both deterministic and stochastic recursions.

Conclusion: The asymmetry explains Q-learning's overestimation bias, with positive errors amplified by the max operator while negative errors are naturally constrained.

Abstract: This paper develops a sign-separated finite-time error analysis for constant step-size Q-learning. Starting from the switching-system representation, the error is decomposed into its componentwise negative and positive parts. The negative part is dominated by a lower comparison linear time-invariant (LTI) system associated with a fixed optimal policy, whereas the positive part is controlled by a linear switching system. The resulting bounds show that the negative-side LTI certificate is no slower than the positive-side switching certificate and may produce a faster exponential envelope. The analysis identifies a max-induced asymmetry in Q-learning error dynamics. This asymmetry is connected to overestimation: positive action-wise errors can be selected and propagated by the Bellman maximum, whereas negative errors admit an optimal-policy lower comparison. Finite-time bounds are provided for both deterministic and stochastic constant-step-size recursions.

</details>


### [157] [ShopGym: An Integrated Framework for Realistic Simulation and Scalable Benchmarking of E-Commerce Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16116)
*Chinmay Savadikar,Mingyu Zhao,Yuanzheng Zhu,Han Li,Shuang Xie,Alberto Castelo,Tianfu Wu,Lingyun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ShopGym is a framework for creating realistic, controllable, and reproducible e-commerce simulation environments to benchmark web agents, using ShopArena to convert live storefronts into sandbox shops and ShopGuru to synthesize benchmark tasks across seven skill categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing e-commerce evaluation methods force a tradeoff between realism (live storefronts are non-stationary and irreproducible) and control (hand-built sandboxes lack diversity and scalability). The field lacks a scalable way to build evaluation settings that are simultaneously realistic, diverse, controllable, inspectable, and reproducible.

Method: ShopGym consists of two layers: (1) ShopArena, which converts live seed storefronts into self-contained sandbox shops via anonymized specifications and validated generation; (2) ShopGuru, which synthesizes benchmark tasks across seven skill categories grounded in each shop's catalog, navigation, policies, and interactions. Validation used graph-based structural analysis and agent-based behavioral evaluation across 224 tasks and six sandbox shops.

Result: Synthetic shops preserve key structural properties of live storefronts. Agent performance on synthetic shops positively correlates with performance on live storefronts, indicating that the framework provides valid evaluation signals.

Conclusion: ShopGym provides a scalable methodology for constructing e-commerce evaluation environments that balance realism, diversity, controllability, inspectability, and reproducibility, enabling more rigorous scientific comparison of web agents.

Abstract: Developing and evaluating e-commerce web agents requires environments that preserve meaningful task structure while enabling controllable, reproducible, and scalable scientific comparison. Existing methodologies force a tradeoff: live storefronts provide realism but are non-stationary, difficult to inspect, and irreproducible, while hand-built sandbox benchmarks provide control but cover only a narrow range of layouts, catalogs, policies, and interaction patterns. We argue that the core bottleneck is methodological: the field lacks a scalable way to construct evaluation settings that are simultaneously realistic, diverse, controllable, inspectable, and reproducible. We introduce ShopGym, an integrated framework for realistic simulation and scalable benchmarking of e-commerce web agents. ShopGym is a framework for constructing e-commerce simulation environments and grounded benchmark tasks. Its simulation layer, ShopArena, converts live seed storefronts into self-contained sandbox shops through anonymized shop specifications and a staged, validated generation process. On top of these simulated storefronts, ShopGuru synthesizes benchmark tasks across seven skill categories, grounding each task in the shop's catalog, navigation structure, policies, and interaction affordances. Together, ShopArena and ShopGuru produce self-contained, resettable, inspectable, and stable evaluation artifacts that preserve structural properties and agent-evaluation signals relevant to shopping tasks. We validate the framework through graph-based structural analysis and agent-based behavioral evaluation with 224 generated tasks across six sandbox shops: three constructed with synthetic data and three with real data. Our results show that the synthetic shops preserve key structural properties of live storefronts, with agent performance on synthetic shops positively correlated with performance on live storefronts.

</details>


### [158] [Property-Guided LLM Program Synthesis for Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16142)
*Augusto B. Corrêa,André G. Pereira,Jendrik Seipp*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes property-guided LLM program synthesis, where instead of scoring programs numerically, the LLM receives concrete counterexamples when a formally defined property is violated. Applied to PDDL planning for synthesizing direct heuristic functions, this approach generates 7x fewer programs, solves more tasks without search, and requires orders of magnitude less computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM program synthesis relies on simple numeric scores (e.g., pass rate) that provide no guidance on why a program failed, leading to high LLM inference and evaluation costs due to generating many candidates hoping some succeed.

Method: A counterexample-guided repair loop: (1) generate one candidate program, (2) check a formally defined property over a training set, (3) return the first violating case as a concrete counterexample to the LLM, (4) repeat until the property holds. Applied to PDDL planning to synthesize direct heuristic functions.

Result: Synthesized heuristics were effectively direct on virtually all test tasks across ten planning domains. Compared to best prior method: generates 7x fewer programs per domain, solves more tasks without search, and requires orders of magnitude less computation for candidate evaluation.

Conclusion: Whenever a problem admits a verifiable property, property-guided LLM synthesis can reduce cost and improve program quality compared to score-based approaches.

Abstract: LLMs have shown impressive success in program synthesis, discovering programs that surpass prior solutions. However, these approaches rely on simple numeric scores to signal program quality, such as the value of the solution or the number of passed tests. Because a score offers no guidance on why a program failed, the system must generate and evaluate many candidates hoping some succeed, increasing LLM inference and evaluation costs. We study a different approach: property-guided LLM program synthesis. Instead of scoring programs after evaluation, we check whether a candidate satisfies a formally defined property. When the property is violated, we stop the evaluation early and provide the LLM with a concrete counterexample showing exactly how the program failed. This feedback drastically reduces both the number of program generations and the evaluation cost, and can guide the LLM to generate stronger programs. We evaluate this approach on PDDL planning domains, asking the LLM to synthesize direct heuristic functions: every state reachable by strictly improving transitions has a strictly improving successor. A heuristic with this property leads hill-climbing algorithm directly to a goal state. A counterexample-guided repair loop generates one candidate program, checks the property over a training set, and returns the first case that violates the property. We evaluate our approach on ten planning domains with an out-of-distribution test set. The synthesized heuristics are effectively direct on virtually all test tasks, and compared to the best prior generation method our approach generates seven times fewer programs per domain on average, solves more tasks without using search, and requires several orders of magnitude less computation to evaluate candidates. Whenever a problem admits a verifiable property, property-guided LLM synthesis can reduce cost and improve program quality.

</details>


### [159] [Look Before You Leap: Autonomous Exploration for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16143)
*Ziang Ye,Wentao Shi,Yuxin Liu,Yu Wang,Zhengzhou Cai,Yaorui Shi,Qi Gu,Xunliang Cai,Fuli Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Exploration Checkpoint Coverage to measure agent exploration, proposes an Explore-then-Act training paradigm that interleaves exploration and task-execution rollouts, and shows that systematic exploration is essential for building generalizable agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents often fail in unfamiliar environments due to premature exploitation—acting on prior knowledge before gathering sufficient environment-specific information. Autonomous exploration is identified as a critical yet underexplored capability.

Method: 1) Proposes Exploration Checkpoint Coverage (ECC) as a verifiable metric for exploration quality. 2) Develops a training strategy interleaving task-execution rollouts and exploration rollouts, each optimized by verifiable rewards. 3) Introduces Explore-then-Act paradigm decoupling information-gathering from task execution.

Result: Agents trained with standard task-oriented RL exhibit narrow, repetitive behaviors that hurt downstream performance, while the proposed exploration-aware training strategy improves generalization and real-world readiness.

Conclusion: Learning to systematically explore is imperative for building generalizable and real-world-ready agents; the Explore-then-Act paradigm effectively addresses the premature exploitation problem.

Abstract: Large language model based agents often fail in unfamiliar environments due to premature exploitation: a tendency to act on prior knowledge before acquiring sufficient environment-specific information. We identify autonomous exploration as a critical yet underexplored capability for building adaptive agents. To formalize and quantify this capability, we introduce Exploration Checkpoint Coverage, a verifiable metric that measures how broadly an agent discovers key states, objects, and affordances. Our systematic evaluation reveals that agents trained with standard task-oriented reinforcement learning consistently exhibit narrow and repetitive behaviors that impede downstream performance. To address this limitation, we develop a training strategy that interleaves task-execution rollouts and exploration rollouts, with each type of rollout optimized by its corresponding verifiable reward. Building on this training strategy, we propose the Explore-then-Act paradigm, which decouples information-gathering from task execution: agents first utilize an interaction budget to acquire grounded environmental knowledge, then leverage it for task resolution. Our results demonstrate that learning to systematically explore is imperative for building generalizable and real-world-ready agents.

</details>


### [160] [An Algebraic Exposition of the Theory of Dyadic Morality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16153)
*Kush R. Varshney*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper formalizes the theory of dyadic morality using structural causal modeling, introducing psychological operators for moral judgment computation, addressing scalability through node collapse and sequential processing, and applying the framework to AI policy design and empirical measurement of mind perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a mathematically rigorous formalization of the theory of dyadic morality (TDM) that can enable neurosymbolic AI systems to compute moral judgments faithfully to human moral cognition.

Method: The authors formalize TDM using structural causal modeling (SCM) notation, define three psychological operators (typecasting, completion, valence-dependent inference), and address scalability via node collapse and sequential processing.

Result: Demonstrates concrete applications to AI policy design: detecting conflicting obligations, structuring helpfulness policies that preserve user agency, and designing post-failure communication as causal interventions. Recommends scoped, contextual measurement of mind perception.

Conclusion: The algebraic formalization enables neurosymbolic AI systems to compute morality with mathematical rigor while remaining faithful to human moral cognition.

Abstract: This paper provides an algebraic exposition of the theory of dyadic morality (TDM), a psychological model of moral judgment grounded in a simple two-node template: an intentional agent causing harm to a vulnerable patient. We formalize TDM using structural causal modeling (SCM) notation and identify three psychological operators (typecasting operator, completion operator, and valence-dependent inference mechanism) that extend standard SCM to capture how people compute moral judgments under constraints.
