We address scalability challenges arising from TDM's dyadic limitation, showing how moral cognition compresses multi-node scenarios through node collapse and sequential processing. Drawing on this algebraic framework, we demonstrate concrete applications to AI policy design: detecting conflicting obligations, structuring helpfulness policies to preserve user agency, and designing post-failure communication as causal interventions. Finally, we recommend scoped, contextual measurement of mind perception over universal averaging to operationalize the theory empirically. This algebraic formalization enables neurosymbolic AI systems to compute morality in a way that is both mathematically rigorous and faithful to human moral cognition.

</details>


### [161] [Formal Methods Meet LLMs: Auditing, Monitoring, and Intervention for Compliance of Advanced AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16198)
*Parand A. Alamdari,Toryn Q. Klassen,Sheila A. McIlraith*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes techniques using Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) combined with formal methods and machine learning to monitor and audit AI systems (especially LLMs) for behavioral constraints throughout the development lifecycle, demonstrating that small-model labelers can match frontier LLM judges in detecting violations and that predictive/intervening monitors reduce violation rates while preserving task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI governance needs concrete monitoring and auditing methods to ensure AI-enabled products comply with safety constraints, norms, and regulations throughout their development lifecycle, from pre-deployment testing to runtime monitoring.

Method: The authors combine formal methods (Linear Temporal Logic - LTL) with machine learning to create techniques for offline auditing, online runtime monitoring, predictive monitoring (sampling-based), and intervening monitors that preempt violations. They evaluate against LLM baseline methods.

Result: LTL-based auditing/monitoring outperforms LLM baselines in detecting temporally extended behavioral constraint violations. Small-model labelers match or exceed frontier LLM judges. Predictive/intervening monitors significantly reduce violation rates of LLM agents while preserving task performance. LLMs show degraded temporal reasoning with increasing event distance, constraints, and propositions.

Conclusion: Formal methods like LTL provide superior foundations for AI monitoring and auditing compared to pure LLM-based approaches, enabling reliable runtime violation detection and prevention.

Abstract: We examine one particular dimension of AI governance: how to monitor and audit AI-enabled products and services throughout the AI development lifecycle, from pre-deployment testing to post-deployment auditing. Combining principles from formal methods with SoTA machine learning, we propose techniques that enable AI-enabled product and service developers, as well as third party AI developers and evaluators, to perform offline auditing and online (runtime) monitoring of product-specific (temporally extended) behavioral constraints such as safety constraints, norms, rules and regulations with respect to black-box advanced AI systems, notably LLMs. We further provide practical techniques for predictive monitoring, such as sampling-based methods, and we introduce intervening monitors that act at runtime to preempt and potentially mitigate predicted violations. Experimental results show that by exploiting the formal syntax and semantics of Linear Temporal Logic (LTL), our proposed auditing and monitoring techniques are superior to LLM baseline methods in detecting violations of temporally extended behavioral constraints; with our approach, even small-model labelers match or exceed frontier LLM judges. Our predictive and intervening monitors significantly reduce the violation rates of LLM-based agents while largely preserving task performance. We further show through controlled experiments that LLMs' temporal reasoning shows a pronounced degradation in accuracy with increasing event distance, number of constraints, and number of propositions.

</details>


### [162] [Context, Reasoning, and Hierarchy: A Cost-Performance Study of Compound LLM Agent Design in an Adversarial POMDP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16205)
*Igor Bogdanov,Chung-Horng Lung,Thomas Kunz,Jie Gao,Adrian Taylor,Marzia Zaman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A controlled study of compound LLM agents in cyber defense (CybORG CAGE-2) finds that programmatic state abstraction and hierarchical decomposition outperform costly deliberation tools—and that combining deliberation with hierarchy actually degrades performance (a "deliberation cascade").


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practitioners lack guidance on which design choices for compound LLM agents (what to see, how to reason, how to decompose tasks) improve performance vs. merely increase inference costs, especially in adversarial partially observable environments.

Method: Controlled study in CybORG CAGE-2 (POMDP cyber defense) across 5 model families, 6 models, 12 configurations (3,475 episodes) with token-level cost accounting, varying context representation, deliberation tools, and hierarchical decomposition.

Result: (1) Programmatic state abstraction improves mean return by up to 76% over raw observations. (2) Distributing deliberation across hierarchy degrades performance (up to 3.4× worse return) while using 1.8-2.7× more tokens—a "deliberation cascade." (3) Hierarchy without deliberation achieves best absolute performance; context engineering is more cost-effective than deliberation.

Conclusion: For structured adversarial POMDPs, invest in programmatic infrastructure and clean task decomposition rather than deeper per-agent reasoning, as these strategies can interfere when combined.

Abstract: Deploying compound LLM agents in adversarial, partially observable sequential environments requires navigating several design dimensions: (1) what the agent sees, (2) how it reasons, and (3) how tasks are decomposed across components. Yet practitioners lack guidance on which design choices improve performance versus merely increase inference costs. We present a controlled study of compound LLM agent design in CybORG CAGE-2, a cyber defense environment modeled as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). Reward is non-positive, so all configurations operate in a failure-mitigation mode. Our evaluation spans five model families, six models, and twelve configurations (3,475 episodes) with token-level cost accounting. We vary context representation (raw observations vs. a deterministic state-tracking layer with compressed history), deliberation (self-questioning, self-critique, and self-improvement tools, with optional chain-of-thought prompting), and hierarchical decomposition (monolithic ReAct vs. delegation to specialized sub-agents). We find that: (1) Programmatic state abstraction delivers the largest returns per token spent (RPTS), improving mean return by up to 76% over raw observations. (2) Distributing deliberation tools across a hierarchy degrades performance relative to hierarchy alone for all five model families, reaching up to 3.4$\times$ worse mean return while using 1.8-2.7$\times$ more tokens. We call this destructive pattern a deliberation cascade. (3) Hierarchical decomposition without deliberation achieves the best absolute performance for most models, and context engineering is generally more cost-effective than deliberation. These findings suggest a design principle for structured adversarial POMDPs: invest in programmatic infrastructure and clean task decomposition rather than deeper per-agent reasoning, as these strategies can interfere when combined.

</details>


### [163] [Confirming Correct, Missing the Rest: LLM Tutoring Agents Struggle Where Feedback Matters Most](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16207)
*Tahreem Yasir,Wenbo Li,Sam Gilson,Sutapa Dey Tithi,Xiaoyi Tian,Tiffany Barnes*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks seven LLM feedback agents in propositional logic tutoring and finds they perform well on optimal steps but systematically fail at diagnosing suboptimal and incorrect solutions, suggesting inherent architectural limits and a gap between diagnosis and effective feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The distinction between optimal, suboptimal, and incorrect student solutions is central to intelligent tutoring systems (ITS) but remains untested for LLM-based tutors. As LLMs are increasingly used as conversational complements to ITS, evaluating their diagnostic precision is essential.

Method: The authors created a benchmark of seven LLM feedback agents in propositional logic, using knowledge-graph-derived ground truth across 10,836 solution-feedback pairs and three feedback conditions.

Result: Models achieved near-ceiling performance on optimal steps but systematically over-rejected valid suboptimal reasoning and over-validated incorrect solutions. These failures persisted regardless of solution context, suggesting architectural rather than informational limits. Accurate diagnosis did not reliably produce pedagogically actionable feedback.

Conclusion: LLMs are better suited for hybrid architectures where KG-grounded models handle diagnosis while LLMs support open-ended scaffolding and dialogue.

Abstract: Effective tutoring requires distinguishing optimal, valid but suboptimal, and incorrect student solutions, a distinction central to intelligent tutoring systems (ITS) but untested for LLM-based tutors. As LLMs are increasingly explored as conversational complements to ITS, evaluating their diagnostic precision is essential. We present a benchmark of seven LLM feedback agents in propositional logic using knowledge-graph-derived ground truth across 10,836 solution--feedback pairs and three feedback conditions. Models achieved near-ceiling performance on optimal steps but systematically over-rejected valid but suboptimal reasoning and over-validated incorrect solutions, precisely where adaptive tutoring matters most. These failures persisted across models regardless of solution context, suggesting architectural rather than informational limits. Moreover, accurate diagnosis did not reliably produce pedagogically actionable feedback, revealing a gap between diagnostic judgment and instructional effectiveness. Our findings suggest that LLMs are better suited for hybrid architectures where KG-grounded models handle diagnosis while LLMs support open-ended scaffolding and dialogue.

</details>


### [164] [Fully Open Meditron: An Auditable Pipeline for Clinical LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16215)
*Xavier Theimer-Lienhard,Mushtaha El-Amin,Fay Elhassan,Sahaj Vaidya,Victor Cartier-Negadi,David Sasu,Lars Klein,Mary-Anne Hartley*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Fully Open Meditron, the first fully open pipeline for building LLM-based clinical decision support systems (CDSS), comprising a clinician-audited corpus, reproducible training framework, and transparent evaluation protocol. Multiple FO models trained with this pipeline achieve state-of-the-art results, with Apertus-70B-MeditronFO reaching 53.8% (+6.6 points) on medical benchmarks, demonstrating that fully open pipelines can match or exceed proprietary systems while maintaining auditability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based clinical decision support systems are largely opaque—most "open" models only release weights while hiding data provenance, curation procedures, and generation pipelines. Fully Open models exposing the complete training stack do not currently exist in medicine, creating a gap in scrutable, auditable, and reproducible CDSS.

Method: The authors construct a clinician-audited training corpus unifying eight public medical QA datasets with three synthetic extensions (exam-style QA, guideline-grounded QA from 46,469 clinical practice guidelines, and clinical vignettes). The pipeline enforces decontamination, gold-label resampling, and end-to-end validation by a four-physician panel. They evaluate using LLM-as-a-judge calibrated against 204 human raters, applying the recipe to five FO base models.

Result: All MeditronFO variants outperform their bases. Apertus-70B-MeditronFO achieves +6.6 points (47.2%→53.8%) on aggregate medical benchmarks, setting a new FO SoTA. Gemma-3-27B-MeditronFO is preferred over MedGemma in 58.6% of comparisons and outperforms it on HealthBench (58% vs 55.9%).

Conclusion: Fully open pipelines can achieve state-of-the-art domain-specific performance in medical LLM-CDSS without sacrificing auditability or reproducibility, offering a viable path toward transparent clinical AI systems.

Abstract: Clinical decision support systems (CDSS) require scrutable, auditable pipelines that enable rigorous, reproducible validation. Yet current LLM-based CDSS remain largely opaque. Most "open" models are open-weight only, releasing parameters while withholding the data provenance, curation procedures, and generation pipelines that determine model behavior. Fully Open (FO) models, which expose the complete training stack end-to-end, do not currently exist in medicine. We introduce Fully Open Meditron, the first fully open pipeline for building LLM-CDSS, comprising a clinician-audited training corpus, a reproducible data construction and training framework, and a use-aligned evaluation protocol. The corpus unifies eight public medical QA datasets into a normalized conversational format and expands coverage with three clinician-vetted synthetic extensions: exam-style QA, guideline-grounded QA derived from 46,469 clinical practice guidelines, and clinical vignettes. The pipeline enforces system-wide decontamination, gold-label resampling of teacher generations, and end-to-end validation by a four-physician panel. We evaluate using an LLM-as-a-judge protocol over expert-written clinical vignettes, calibrated against 204 human raters. We apply the recipe to five FO base models (Apertus-70B/8B-Instruct, OLMo-2-32B-SFT, EuroLLM-22B/9B-Instruct). All MeditronFO variants are preferred over their bases. Apertus-70B-MeditronFO improves +6.6 points over its base (47.2% to 53.8%) on aggregate medical benchmarks, establishing a new FO SoTA. Gemma-3-27B-MeditronFO is preferred over MedGemma in 58.6% of LLM-as-a-judge comparisons and outperforms it on HealthBench (58% vs 55.9%). These results show that fully open pipelines can achieve state-of-the-art domain-specific performance without sacrificing auditability or reproducibility.

</details>


### [165] [FORGE: Self-Evolving Agent Memory With No Weight Updates via Population Broadcast](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16233)
*Igor Bogdanov,Chung-Horng Lung,Thomas Kunz,Jie Gao,Adrian Taylor,Marzia Zaman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FORGE is a staged, population-based protocol that uses LLM agents to generate and evolve natural-language memories (rules, examples, or both) to improve decision-making in a network-defense task (CybORG CAGE-2). It outperforms zero-shot and Reflexion baselines across 4 LLM families, showing up to 7.7× improvement in returns and reducing catastrophic failures to ~1%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether LLM agents can improve decision-making through self-generated memory without gradient updates, addressing the challenge that LLMs often perform poorly in zero-shot settings on complex tasks like network-defense POMDPs.

Method: FORGE uses a Reflexion-style inner loop (reflection agent converts failed trajectories into rules, examples, or mixed artifacts) plus an outer loop that broadcasts the best-performing instance's memory across the population and graduates converged instances via a criterion to save compute.

Result: Across 4 LLM families (Gemini, Grok, Llama, Qwen), FORGE improves returns by 1.7-7.7× over zero-shot and 29-72% over Reflexion, reducing major failures (below -100) to ~1%. Population broadcast is critical; examples work best for most models but rules are more cost-effective; weaker models benefit more.

Conclusion: FORGE effectively enables LLM agents to improve via self-generated memory without stronger model distillation. Population broadcast is the key mechanism. The approach is especially beneficial for weaker models, suggesting it may help close capability gaps. Evidence is limited to the CAGE-2 B-line scenario.

Abstract: Can LLM agents improve decision-making through self-generated memory without gradient updates? We propose FORGE (Failure-Optimized Reflective Graduation and Evolution), a staged, population-based protocol that evolves prompt-injected natural-language memory for hierarchical ReAct agents. FORGE wraps a Reflexion-style inner loop, where a dedicated reflection agent (using the same underlying LLM, no distillation from a stronger model) converts failed trajectories into reusable knowledge artifacts: textual heuristics (Rules), few-shot demonstrations (Examples), or both (Mixed), with an outer loop that propagates the best-performing instance's memory to the population between stages and freezes converged instances via a graduation criterion. We evaluate on CybORG CAGE-2, a stochastic network-defense POMDP at a 30-step horizon against the B-line attacker, where all four tested LLM families (Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Grok-4-Fast, Llama-4-Maverick, Qwen3-235B) exhibit strongly negative, heavy-tailed zero-shot rewards. Compared against both a zero-shot baseline and a Reflexion baseline (isolated single-stream learning), FORGE improves average evaluation return by 1.7-7.7$\times$ over zero-shot and by 29-72% over Reflexion in all 12 model-representation conditions, reducing major-failure rates (below $-100$) to as low as $\sim$1%. We find that (1) population broadcast is critical mechanism, with a no-graduation ablation confirming that broadcast carries the performance gains while graduation primarily saves compute; (2) Examples achieves the strongest returns for three of four models, Rules offers the best cost-reliability profile with $\sim$40% fewer tokens; and (3) weaker baseline models benefit disproportionately, suggesting FORGE may mitigate capability gaps rather than amplify strong models. All evidence is confined to CAGE-2 B-line; cross-family findings are directional evidence.

</details>


### [166] [Prospective multi-pathogen disease forecasting using autonomous LLM-guided tree search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16238)
*Sarah Martinson,Michael P. Brenner,Martyna Plomecka,Brian P. Williams,Nicholas G. Reich,Zahra Shamsi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An autonomous LLM-guided tree search system generates, evaluates, and optimizes infectious disease forecasting software, achieving ensemble performance matching or exceeding human-curated CDC models during the 2025-2026 US respiratory season.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Probabilistic infectious disease forecasting is crucial for public health but is bottlenecked by labor-intensive manual model curation, limiting scalability to granular resolutions and emerging pathogens.

Method: The system uses LLM-guided tree search to iteratively generate, evaluate, and optimize executable forecasting code. It incorporates log-scale distance optimization to prevent reward hacking and an automated judge-in-the-loop for structural fidelity to scientific theories.

Result: In prospective real-time evaluation during 2025-2026, the system autonomously discovered diverse models for influenza, COVID-19, and RSV. Its ensemble matched or outperformed human-curated CDC hub ensembles, including in data-scarce "cold start" scenarios for RSV.

Conclusion: This framework overcomes the modeling labor bottleneck by autonomously translating epidemiological theory into accurate, transparent code, enabling rapid deployment of expert-level disease forecasting at unprecedented scales.

Abstract: Probabilistic forecasting of infectious diseases is crucial for public health but relies on labor-intensive manual model curation by expert modeling teams. This bespoke development bottlenecks scalability to granular geographic resolutions or emerging pathogens. Here, we present an autonomous system using Large Language Model (LLM)-guided tree search to iteratively generate, evaluate, and optimize executable forecasting software. In a fully prospective, real-time evaluation during the 2025-2026 US respiratory season, the system autonomously discovered methodologically diverse models for influenza, COVID-19, and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV). Aggregating these machine-generated models yielded an ensemble that consistently matched or outperformed the gold-standard, human-curated Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) hub ensembles out-of-sample. The system successfully navigated data-scarce "cold start" scenarios for RSV. Moreover, controlled retrospective ablations revealed that optimizing log-scale distance metrics prevents reward hacking, while an automated judge-in-the-loop ensures structural fidelity to complex scientific theories. By autonomously translating epidemiological theory into accurate, transparent code, this framework overcomes the modeling labor bottleneck, enabling rapid deployment of expert-level disease forecasting at unprecedented scales.

</details>


### [167] [Probabilistic Dating of Historical Manuscripts via Evidential Deep Regression on Visual Script Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06475)
*Ranjith Chodavarapu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A probabilistic deep regression model for dating historical manuscripts from visual features, achieving 5.4 years MAE on DIVA-HisDB with superior calibration and uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods treat manuscript dating as coarse century-level classification, losing granularity. The authors aim to predict continuous year estimates with well-calibrated uncertainty.

Method: Uses an EfficientNet-B2 backbone with a Normal-Inverse-Gamma (NIG) output head for evidential deep regression, trained with negative-log-likelihood and evidence regularization.

Result: Test MAE of 5.4 years, PICP=92.6% (best calibration), outperforms MC Dropout and Deep Ensembles at 5x lower inference cost. Most certain 20% patches achieve 0.5 years MAE.

Conclusion: Evidential deep regression is highly effective for historical manuscript dating, providing accurate predictions with well-calibrated uncertainty that correlates with actual error.

Abstract: We introduce a probabilistic approach for dating historical manuscript pages from visual features alone. Instead of aggregating centuries into classes as is standard in the previous literature, we pose dating as an evidential deep regression problem over a continuous year axis, allowing our neural network to output a full predictive distribution with decomposed aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in a single forward pass. Our architecture combines an EfficientNet-B2 backbone with a Normal-Inverse-Gamma (NIG) output head trained with a joint negative-log-likelihood and evidence-regularization objective. On the DIVA-HisDB benchmark (150 pages, 3 medieval codices, 151,936 patches), our model scores a test MAE of 5.4 years, well below the 50-year century-label supervision granularity, with 93\% of patches within 5 years and 97\% within 10 years. Our approach achieves \textbf{PICP=92.6\%}, the best calibration among all compared methods, in a single forward pass, outperforming MC Dropout (PICP=88.2\%, 50 passes) and Deep Ensembles (PICP=79.7\%, 5 models) at $5\times$ lower inference cost. Uncertainty decomposition shows aleatoric uncertainty is a strong predictor of dating error (Spearman $ρ=0.729$), and a selective prediction about the most certain 20\% of patches can provide \textbf{0.5 years MAE}. We show that predicted uncertainty increases as image degradation worsens, spatial decomposition maps explain which script regions cause aleatoric uncertainty, and page-level aggregation reduces MAE to 4.5 years with $ρ=0.905$ between uncertainty and page-level error.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [168] [Learning Selective Merge Policies for Deadline-Constrained Coded Caching via Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15236)
*Amirhossein Yousefiramandi*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: A DRL-based approach using graph-attention policy networks and PPO to solve deadline-constrained coded delivery, achieving 40.9% reduction in packet expiration ratio and learning to selectively merge at ~31.8% rate rather than aggressive merging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing coded caching methods don't consider strict user deadlines for delay-sensitive applications (like video streaming). The challenge is that merging messages helps current multi-casting but can harm subsequent ones, requiring intelligent online selection.

Method: Formulated as a masked discrete-action queue-state control problem; trained a graph-attention policy network via proximal policy optimization (PPO) to decide which messages to merge online under deadline constraints.

Result: Reduces broadcast-packet expiration ratio ρ by 40.9% (0.208 vs. 0.352) against best baseline SACM++; best broadcast-efficiency score σ among coded multi-casting methods on Track A; policy learns to merge at only ~31.8% rate.

Conclusion: Selective merging outperforms aggressive merging for tight-deadline applications, which the DRL policy learns naturally.

Abstract: With the coded caching, the server can use the information the users have cached to serve multiple users at a time by sending a single coded multi-casting message, i.e., the merged message, thereby relieving the peak network loads. However, for the delay-sensitive applications of the users, like the video streaming services, it becomes essential to choose which messages to merge online, considering the strict deadlines for each request. The problem, however, is that while the merge is helpful for the formation of the current coded multi-casting message, it can be harmful for the subsequent ones. We proposed a DRL-based solution that formulates the deadline-constrained coded delivery as a masked discrete-action queue-state control problem, while we trained a graph-attention policy network via proximal policy optimization. The policy network reduces the broadcast-packet expiration ratio $ρ$ by $40.9%$ ($0.208$ vs. $0.352$) with respect to the best coded multi-casting baseline (SACM++) on the uniform-demand benchmark, while also attaining the best broadcast-efficiency score $σ$ across the Track A battery among the coded multi-casting methods. The interesting fact we observed is that for the applications of the users with tight deadlines, the method of selective merging is better than the method of aggressive merging, i.e., the policy network learns to merge at only $\approx 31.8%$ rate, even though the same observation holds across the variations within the same simulator family.

</details>


### [169] [Covert Bayesian Quickest Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16140)
*Yun-Feng Lo,Matthieu R. Bloch*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies covert quickest change detection in a Bayesian infinite-horizon setting, introducing the expected covertness budget (ECB) metric and establishing second-order asymptotic bounds on detection delay under constraints on false alarm and covertness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable legitimate entities to detect changes via active probing while remaining covert from adversaries monitoring the channel, balancing detection speed with stealth requirements.

Method: Introduces expected covertness budget (ECB) as a covertness metric; derives second-order asymptotic converse bounds; proposes a constant-sensing-probability Shiryaev-type policy for achievability.

Result: Establishes second-order asymptotic converse bound on average detection delay as PFA→0 under ECB constraints, quantifying the maximum square-root-order covert sensing gain; the proposed scheme matches this bound.

Conclusion: The constant-sensing-probability Shiryaev-type policy achieves optimal second-order performance, enabling covert quickest change detection with quantified trade-offs between detection delay, false alarm, and covertness.

Abstract: We investigate the problem of covert quickest change detection in a Bayesian and infinite-horizon setting. A legitimate entity seeks to detect a change in the state of a discrete memoryless channel as quickly as possible by actively probing it. Simultaneously, the entity must ensure its probing remains covert from an adversary monitoring the channel for active sensing. We introduce the expected covertness budget (ECB) as an analytically tractable covertness metric that bounds from above the relative entropy between the observation sequences induced by active and passive sensing. Under constraints on both the probability of false alarm (PFA) and the ECB, we establish a second-order asymptotic converse bound on the average detection delay as the PFA constraint approaches zero, for any positive ECB constraint, explicitly quantifying the maximum square-root-order covert sensing gain possible. Furthermore, we propose an achievability scheme utilizing a constant-sensing-probability Shiryaev-type policy and show that it matches the second-order asymptotic converse. We illustrate our result with a numerical example.

</details>


### [170] [How Far Back in Time a Digital Twin Reflects the State of the Physical Object: Age of Staleness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16176)
*Ismail Cosandal,Sennur Ulukus*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel metric called age of staleness (AoS) to measure information freshness in communication networks, addressing limitations of the existing age of information (AoI) metric by incorporating semantic aspects. The authors analyze a Markov source scenario, derive closed-form expressions for AoS, and formulate an optimization problem for multiple sources using a polyblock algorithm. The metric is particularly suited for digital twin networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing age of information (AoI) metric is semantic-agnostic and does not consider the dynamics of random processes, making it insufficient for applications like remote monitoring. There is a need for a metric that combines freshness and semantic aspects to quantify remote estimator performance.

Method: The paper defines a new metric called age of staleness (AoS), analyzes a simple scenario with an n-ary symmetric Markov source under constant sampling to derive a closed-form AoS expression, then extends to multiple Markov sources where sampling rate allocation is optimized using a polyblock algorithm that exploits the monotonicity of the objective function.

Result: Closed-form expression for AoS is obtained, showing it is a monotonically decreasing function of the sampling rate. A near-optimal solution for multi-source optimization is achieved via the polyblock algorithm despite the non-convex nature of the problem.

Conclusion: The AoS metric is particularly well-suited for digital twin networks (DTN), where multiple physical objects are monitored under a total sampling rate constraint to maintain their digital representations (DTs).

Abstract: The groundbreaking metric age of information (AoI) has been introduced to measure information freshness in communication networks. As transformational as it is, AoI metric falls short in some applications, such as remote monitoring, since it is a semantic-agnostic metric which does not consider the dynamics of the random process. There is a need to quantify the performance of a remote estimator via a metric that combines freshness and semantic aspects. To this end, in this paper, we introduce a novel metric coined age of staleness (AoS) that measures when the last time that the current estimation was correct. First, we analyze a simple scenario where an $n$-ary symmetric Markov source is observed by a monitor via a constant sampling rate, obtain a closed-form expression for the AoS, and show that it is a monotonically decreasing function of the sampling rate. Next, we consider multiple distinct Markov sources, and formulate an optimization problem, where the remote monitor allocates the total sampling rate to tracking the sources. Although the optimization problem is non-convex, its structure is suitable for obtaining a near-optimal solution using the polyblock algorithm, which leverages the monotonicity of the objective function. While the new AoS metric could be applicable in many scenarios, we believe it is particularly well-suited for a digital twin network (DTN) where multiple physical objects (POs) are monitored with a total sampling rate constraint to maintain a digital representation of them, namely, their digital twin (DT).

</details>


### [171] [Fundamental Performance Limits of Non-Coherent ISAC: A Data-Aided Sensing Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16196)
*Dongsheng Peng,Chengkai Zhao,Yihong Li,Zhiqing Wei,Jun Chen,Ping Chen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper investigates a bistatic MIMO ISAC system with co-located sensing and communication receivers, comparing pilot sensing (PS) and data-aided sensing (DAS) schemes under unknown CSI. The DAS scheme achieves a 3 dB SNR gain in low-SNR and faster scaling in high-SNR compared to PS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze and compare the performance of pilot sensing versus data-aided sensing in bistatic MIMO ISAC systems under block-fading channels with unknown CSI, particularly quantifying the advantages of using data symbols for sensing.

Method: The paper characterizes communication rate-sensing distortion functions for both PS and DAS schemes. For DAS, random matrix theory (RMT) is used to derive a closed-form asymptotic expression for sensing distortion.

Result: The DAS scheme shows a strict 3 dB effective SNR improvement in the low-SNR regime and a strictly faster performance scaling rate in the high-SNR limit compared to the PS scheme.

Conclusion: Data-aided sensing significantly outperforms pilot-only sensing in bistatic MIMO ISAC systems, with quantified gains that are especially pronounced in both low-SNR and high-SNR regimes.

Abstract: In this paper, we investigate a bistatic multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system over block-fading channels, focusing on the scenario where the sensing and communication receivers (Rxs) are co-located. Under the assumption of unknown channel state information (CSI) at the Rx, two schemes are considered: pilot sensing (PS) and data-aided sensing (DAS). The communication rate-sensing distortion functions for both schemes are characterized. For the DAS scheme, a closed-form asymptotic expression for the sensing distortion is derived by using random matrix theory (RMT). The asymptotic performance analysis explicitly quantifies the significant gains of the DAS scheme, revealing a strict $3$ dB effective SNR improvement in the low-SNR regime and a strictly faster performance scaling rate in the high-SNR limit compared to the PS scheme.

</details>


### [172] [Preemption Revisited: Multi-Threshold Preemption Policies for AoI Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16225)
*Sahan Liyanaarachchi,Sennur Ulukus,Nail Akar*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies optimal preemption policies for status update systems with random arrivals, showing that multi-threshold policies outperform traditional probabilistic and single-threshold policies by leveraging both packet age and system age.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the study of optimal preemption policies from generate-at-will models to systems with random update arrivals, and to demonstrate the effectiveness of threshold-based policies in this more realistic setting.

Method: The authors introduce an analytical framework for evaluating the AoI of multi-threshold preemption policies and analyze the structure of optimal preemption policies under random update arrivals.

Result: Multi-threshold policies, which consider both the age of the packet and the age of the system, significantly outperform traditional probabilistic and single-threshold policies in terms of AoI.

Conclusion: Threshold-based preemption policies are effective for random arrival systems, and utilizing dual age metrics (packet age + system age) yields substantial AoI gains.

Abstract: The study of optimal preemption policies for status update systems has been a recurring topic in the age of information (AoI) literature, where threshold-based structures have been shown to be optimal under a generate-at-will update generation model under certain assumptions. In this work, we study the effectiveness of threshold-based policies for a system with random update arrivals. In this regard, we introduce an analytical framework for evaluating the AoI of multi-threshold preemption policies and present interesting characteristics of the structure of the optimal preemption policy. We show the effectiveness of these threshold-based policies over the traditional probabilistic preemption policies and single-threshold policies, where we observe that significant gains in terms of AoI can be obtained by utilizing both the age of the packet and the age of the system when designing these preemption policies.

</details>


### [173] [Breaking the Finite-Sample Barrier in Entropy Coupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16229)
*Shahab Asoodeh,Jun Chen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces the minimum list entropy coupling, a measure of residual uncertainty when marginals are fixed but observations can be arbitrarily dependent. It shows that dependent observations can eliminate uncertainty exactly after finitely many samples (zero-entropy regime), unlike independent observations which only reduce uncertainty exponentially. The work characterizes conditions for zero entropy, provides a greedy algorithm, and applies the framework to representation learning and randomness extraction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize how dependence among marginally constrained observations can break a finite-sample barrier, addressing the gap between classical independent-observation models and scenarios where dependent observations are allowed.

Method: Introduces the minimum list entropy coupling $H(P\|Q_1,\dots,Q_m)$, studies its properties, characterizes the zero-entropy regime via necessary and sufficient conditions, develops a greedy algorithm with monotone approximation guarantees, and applies the framework to distribution-matching representation learning and randomness extraction.

Result: Shows a sharp dichotomy: independent observations reduce residual uncertainty exponentially, while dependent observations can eliminate it exactly after finitely many samples. Under mild support assumptions, zero entropy is achieved with $O(\log(1/P_{\min}))$ observations. Provides structural criteria for the zero-entropy regime and a greedy algorithm with guarantees.

Conclusion: The minimum list entropy coupling provides a principled framework to understand finite-sample limits in tasks such as representation learning and randomness extraction, where zero entropy corresponds to exact recovery and exact extraction.

Abstract: Dependence among marginally constrained observations can break a finite-sample barrier. To formalize this phenomenon, we introduce the \emph{minimum list entropy coupling} $H(P\|Q_1,\dots,Q_m)$, the minimum conditional entropy $H(X|Y_1,\dots,Y_m)$ over all joint distributions with prescribed discrete marginals $X\sim P$ and $Y_i\sim Q_i$. Unlike classical formulations based on independent observations, our model allows $Y_1,\dots,Y_m$ to be arbitrarily dependent while keeping each marginal fixed. This enlarged coupling space reveals a sharp dichotomy: independent observations reduce residual uncertainty exponentially, whereas dependent observations can eliminate it exactly after finitely many samples. We characterize this zero-entropy regime through necessary and sufficient conditions and give concrete structural criteria under which it occurs. In particular, under mild support assumptions, zero entropy is achieved with $O(\log(1/P_{\min}))$ observations, where $P_{\min}$ is the minimum nonzero mass of $P$. We also develop a greedy algorithm with monotone approximation guarantees for computing $H(P\|Q_1,\dots,Q_m)$. Finally, we show that the same framework formalizes finite-sample limits in distribution-matching representation learning and randomness extraction, where zero entropy corresponds to exact recovery and exact extraction.

</details>


### [174] [PrismQuant: Rate-Distortion-Optimal Vector Quantization for Gaussian-Mixture Sources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15507)
*Bumsu Park,Chanho Park,Youngmok Park,Namyoon Lee*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper develops a rate-distortion theory for Gaussian-mixture sources and proposes PrismQuant, a practical transform coding scheme that asymptotically achieves the conditional RD bound by losslessly transmitting component labels and applying component-matched KLT with scalar quantization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical transform coding (KLT + reverse waterfilling) is RD-optimal for Gaussian sources but breaks down for multimodal sources where no single covariance can capture heterogeneous local geometries and the RD function has no closed form.

Method: 1) Theoretical analysis showing the genie-aided conditional RD function for Gaussian mixtures is governed by a single global reverse-waterfilling level shared across all components and eigenmodes. 2) PrismQuant: losslessly transmit component labels (rate H(C)/n), encode residual using component-matched KLT + scalar quantization, achieving vanishing asymptotic gap to the bound.

Result: On synthetic Gaussian mixtures, PrismQuant closely approaches the theoretical RD bound. On real-world CSI data, it achieves competitive/superior performance compared to transformer-based learned codecs with over 10x smaller model size.

Conclusion: The mixture structure incurs only a component label cost. PrismQuant provides a practical, efficient, and theoretically grounded approach for compressing multimodal sources.

Abstract: For a Gaussian source under mean-squared error (MSE), classical transform coding is rate--distortion (RD) optimal: the Karhunen--Loeve transform (KLT) diagonalizes the covariance, reverse waterfilling allocates the bits, and scalar quantization closes the loop. This elegant story breaks down for multimodal sources, where no single covariance can capture heterogeneous local geometries, and the RD function loses its closed form. We revisit this problem through Gaussian-mixture sources and develop a constructive RD theory for them. Our key finding is that the mixture structure incurs only a component label cost. Conditioned on the active mixture component, each branch is Gaussian; the challenge is allocating bits across heterogeneous branches. We prove that the genie-aided conditional RD function is governed by a single global reverse-waterfilling level shared across all components and eigenmodes. Building on this result, we introduce PrismQuant, which transmits the component label losslessly and encodes the residual using the component-matched KLT, followed by scalar quantization, achieving a rate of H(C)/n bits per source dimension of the converse, with a vanishing asymptotic gap. We further develop a practical implementation based on EM-driven Gaussian-mixture learning, component-adaptive KLTs, and entropy-constrained scalar quantization (ECSQ). Experiments on synthetic Gaussian mixtures show that PrismQuant closely approaches the theoretical RD bound, while experiments on real-world channel-state-information (CSI) data demonstrate competitive or superior performance compared with transformer-based learned codecs at more than one order of magnitude smaller model size.

</details>


### [175] [GeoGS-CE: Learning Delay--Beam Channel Priors with 3D Gaussians for High-Mobility Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16094)
*Yumeng Zhang,Jiajia Guo,Chaozheng Wen,Chenghong Bian,Jun Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: GeoGS-CE is a two-stage channel estimation framework for high-mobility sparse-pilot scenarios that uses a 3D Gaussian scene representation and differentiable wireless rendering to model the delay-beam power spectrum as a stable geometric prior, enabling accurate full-band CFR reconstruction via linear MMSE estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wideband channel estimation in high-mobility scenarios (e.g., high-speed railways) is difficult because channels change rapidly, yet practical systems can only allocate sparse pilots due to dense user populations. The paper leverages the observation that such environments have stable delay-beam power spectra rich in geometric information.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) Offline stage: a 3D Gaussian representation modeling NLoS geometric scattering, combined with a leakage-aware differentiable wireless rendering process mapping NLoS Gaussians and an explicit virtual LoS component to the delay-beam power spectrum. (2) Online stage: predict the delay-beam power spectrum per user location as a covariance prior for linear MMSE-based full-band CFR reconstruction and tracking.

Result: Simulations on Guangshen high-speed railway channel data demonstrate that the geometric prior substantially improves CFR reconstruction accuracy over pilot-only and non-geometric baselines.

Conclusion: Exploiting geometric environmental priors (delay-beam power spectrum) enables accurate channel estimation in challenging high-mobility, sparse-pilot scenarios.

Abstract: Wideband channel estimation (CE) in high-mobility scenarios remains challenging because channel responses vary rapidly, while practical systems can allocate only sparse pilots to accommodate dense users. Fortunately, many high-mobility environments, such as high-speed railways, exhibit scheduled trajectories, predictable velocities, and a limited number of dominant propagation paths. These properties induce a delay--beam power spectrum that is more stable than the instantaneous complex channel frequency response (CFR), less sensitive to the random phase coherence, and rich in geometric information. To exploit such environmental properties, we propose GeoGS-CE, a two-stage channel estimation framework for sparse-pilot high-mobility scenarios. In the offline stage, GeoGS-CE jointly models: 1) a scene-level 3D Gaussian representation that captures the non-line-of-sight (NLoS) geometric scattering support, and 2) a leakage-aware differentiable wireless rendering process that maps the NLoS Gaussians, together with an explicit virtual line-of-sight (LoS) component, to the measured delay--beam power spectrum, while accounting for practical OFDM delay and array leakage effects. In the online stage, the delay--beam power spectrum is predicted for each user location and used as a strong covariance prior, enabling accurate full-band and full-array CFR reconstruction and tracking through a linear MMSE estimator. Simulations based on channels generated from a segment of the Guangshen high-speed railway show that the proposed geometric prior substantially improves CFR reconstruction over pilot-only and non-geometric baselines.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [176] [AgentStop: Terminating Local AI Agents Early to Save Energy in Consumer Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15206)
*Dzung Pham,Kleomenis Katevas,Ali Shahin Shamsabadi,Hamed Haddadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the overhead of running LLM-based agents locally on consumer hardware and proposes AgentStop, a lightweight supervisor that predicts and terminates unlikely-to-succeed trajectories early, reducing wasted energy by 15-20% with minimal utility drop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Local deployment of LLM agents preserves privacy and avoids API costs, but agentic workflows are highly resource-intensive (time, tokens, energy). The authors aim to address this inefficiency to enable sustainable on-device agents.

Method: The authors measure time, token, and energy overhead of local LLM agent execution. They introduce AgentStop, which uses low-cost execution signals (e.g., token-level log probabilities) to predict and preemptively terminate trajectories unlikely to succeed.

Result: AgentStop reduces wasted energy by 15-20% with less than 5% utility drop on web-based QA and coding benchmarks. Agentic execution increases GPU power draw, temperature, and battery drain compared to single-inference workloads.

Conclusion: Predictive early termination is a practical mechanism for enabling sustainable, privacy-preserving LLM agents on user devices.

Abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to automate complex, multi-step tasks such as coding or web-based question answering. While remote, cloud-based agents offer scalability and ease of deployment, they raise privacy concerns, depend on network connectivity, and incur recurring API costs. Deploying agents locally on user devices mitigates these issues by preserving data privacy and eliminating usage-based fees. However, agentic workflows are far more resource-intensive than typical LLM interactions. Iterative reasoning, tool use, and failure retries substantially increase token consumption, often expending significant compute without successfully completing tasks.
  In this work, we investigate the time, token, and energy overhead of locally deployed LLM-based agents on consumer hardware. Our measurements show that agentic execution increases GPU power draw, temperature, and battery drain compared to single-inference workloads. To address this inefficiency, we introduce AgentStop, a lightweight efficiency supervisor that predicts and preemptively terminates trajectories unlikely to succeed. Leveraging low-cost execution signals, such as token-level log probabilities, AgentStop can reduce wasted energy by 15-20% with minimal impact on task performance (<5% utility drop) for challenging web-based question answering and coding benchmarks. These findings position predictive early termination as a practical mechanism for enabling sustainable, privacy-preserving LLM agents on user devices. Our project code and data are available at https://github.com/brave-experiments/AgentStop.

</details>


### [177] [Quantization Undoes Alignment: Bias Emergence in Compressed LLMs Across Models and Precision Levels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15208)
*Plawan Kumar Rath,Rahul Maliakkal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that post-training quantization of LLMs (down to 3-4 bit) causes new stereotypical biases to emerge in 6-21% of previously unbiased items, while aggregate metrics like perplexity fail to detect this degradation. The study analyzes 3 instruction-tuned models across 5 precision levels using 911K inference records on the BBQ bias benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training quantization is widely used to reduce LLM costs, but its impact on model quality (especially fairness and bias) is poorly understood. Existing studies are limited: they compare only two conditions, rely on aggregate metrics, and evaluate single model families, which cannot distinguish gradual degradation from threshold-dependent safety failures.

Method: Controlled empirical study of three instruction-tuned models (Qwen2.5-7B, Mistral-7B, Phi-3.5-mini) at five precision levels (BF16 through 3-bit) on 12,148 BBQ bias benchmark items across 5 random seeds, totaling 911,100 inference records. Logistic regression was used to confirm dose-response patterns.

Result: 3-bit quantization causes 6-21% of previously unbiased items to develop new stereotypical behaviors following a clear dose-response pattern. Models' willingness to select "unknown" answers declines by 17.4%. At 4-bit, 2.5-5.6% of items already develop new biases, yet perplexity increases by under 3% at 4-bit and under 0.5% at 8-bit.

Conclusion: Aggregate evaluation metrics systematically miss fairness-critical degradation from quantization, underscoring the need for quality-aware compression protocols that explicitly test for bias emergence before deployment.

Abstract: Large Language Models are routinely compressed via post-training quantization to reduce inference costs and memory footprint for cloud and edge deployment, yet the impact of this compression on model quality remains poorly understood. Existing studies typically compare only two conditions (full-precision vs. a single quantized variant), rely on aggregate bias metrics, and evaluate a single model family, making it impossible to distinguish gradual degradation from threshold-dependent safety failures. We conduct a controlled empirical study of three instruction-tuned models (Qwen2.5-7B, Mistral-7B, Phi-3.5-mini) at five precision levels (BF16 through 3-bit) on 12,148 BBQ bias benchmark items across 5 random seeds, totaling 911,100 inference records. Our results reveal that 3-bit quantization causes 6-21% of previously unbiased items to develop new stereotypical behaviors, following a clear dose-response pattern confirmed via logistic regression, while models' willingness to select "unknown" answers declines by 17.4%. Crucially, these item-level changes are invisible to standard quality metrics: perplexity increases by less than 0.5% at 8-bit and under 3% at 4-bit across all three models, yet 2.5-5.6% of items already develop new biases at 4-bit. These findings demonstrate that aggregate evaluation metrics systematically miss fairness-critical degradation, underscoring the need for quality-aware compression protocols that explicitly test for bias emergence before deployment.

</details>


### [178] [Mask-Morph Graph U-Net: A Generalisable Mesh-Based Surrogate for Crashworthiness Field Prediction under Large Geometric Variation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15231)
*Haoran Li,Tobias Lehrer,Yingxue Zhao,Haosu Zhou,Philipp Stocker,Tobias Pfaff,Nan Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MMGUNet combines edge-specific graph layers with coarse-graph morphing and masked pretraining to achieve accurate, generalizable, and data-efficient surrogate modeling for nonlinear finite element crash simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonlinear finite element crash simulations are accurate but computationally expensive, limiting iterative design optimisation. Existing GNN-based surrogates trade off between generalisability (message-passing GNNs) and accuracy (edge-specific layers with fixed connectivity).

Method: MMGUNet: (1) morphs the coarsened graph hierarchy to each input mesh using feature-aligned barycentric parameterisation to retain fixed coarse graph connectivity while improving spatial correspondence; (2) applies node masking during supervised pretraining followed by parameter-efficient fine-tuning where edge-specific layers are frozen.

Result: Coarse-graph morphing improves test accuracy over fixed-coarse-graph baseline; masked supervised pretraining reduces train-test discrepancy and improves data efficiency during transfer; lower prediction error compared with external baselines in in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and cross-component transfer settings.

Conclusion: MMGUNet offers a practical route toward reusable, data-efficient mesh-based surrogate modeling for crashworthiness design exploration.

Abstract: Nonlinear finite element crash simulations are accurate but computationally expensive, limiting their use in iterative design optimisation. Machine-learning surrogate models based on graph neural networks (GNNs) offer a faster alternative. Message-passing GNNs are widely used for mesh simulation, and their shared node and edge update functions are relatively generalisable across varying graph structures. By contrast, non-shareable edge-specific aggregation layers can capture nonlinear relationships more accurately but usually require fixed graph connectivity, which limits generalisability. This paper presents Mask-Morph Graph U-Net (MMGUNet), a practical approach to addressing the limitation of hierarchical Graph U-Net architectures that use edge-specific downsampling and upsampling layers. Fixed coarse graph connectivity is required for edge-specific layers. To retain this while improving spatial correspondence, the proposed method morphs the coarsened graph hierarchy to each input mesh using feature-aligned barycentric parameterisation before constructing cross-graph edges. It further applies node masking during supervised pretraining, followed by parameter-efficient fine-tuning in which high-parameter edge-specific layers are frozen. The proposed approach is evaluated in in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and cross-component transfer settings using mean Euclidean distance and maximum intrusion percentage error. Results show that coarse-graph morphing improves test accuracy relative to a fixed-coarse-graph baseline, while masked supervised pretraining reduces the train-test discrepancy and improves data efficiency during transfer. The proposed model also achieves lower prediction error compared with external baselines. These results demonstrate a practical route toward reusable, data-efficient mesh-based surrogate modelling for crashworthiness design exploration.

</details>


### [179] [MuteBench: Modality Unavailability Tolerance Evaluation for Incomplete Multimodal Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15235)
*Wugeng Zheng,Ziwen Kan,Tianlong Chen,Chen Chen,Song Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MuteBench evaluates 6 fusion architectures on 9 clinical datasets under modality missing and within-modality missing failures, finding architecture family predicts robustness best, channel-independent models have trade-offs, and diffusion-based imputation helps under certain conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal physiological data is widely used in clinical AI but sensors routinely fail, causing modality missing (entire channel absent) and within-modality missing (contiguous time segment lost). No existing benchmark evaluates multiple fusion architectures under both failure modes at controlled severity levels across diverse clinical datasets.

Method: MuteBench covers 9 datasets from 7 clinical domains, 6 fusion architectures, and 2 missing-data modes over 125,000 samples. Systematic evaluation of robustness across architecture families, with a PTB-XL case study on diffusion-based imputation.

Result: Architecture family is the strongest robustness predictor (outweighing parameter count). Channel-independent models tolerate modality missing well but are sensitive to within-modality missing on short sequences. Curriculum modality dropout only protects up to the max dropout rate used in training. Channel count, sequence length, and modality alignment jointly determine which failure mode poses greater threat. Diffusion-based imputation helps most for models with sensitive expert routing.

Conclusion: MuteBench provides concrete guidance for selecting robust architectures and designing future multimodal fusion methods, though broader validation of diffusion-based imputation across datasets remains an open direction.

Abstract: Multimodal physiological data powers clinical AI systems from intensive care units to wearable devices, but sensors routinely fail in practice. Two failure modes are common: modality missing, where an entire channel is absent, and within-modality missing, where a contiguous time segment is lost. No existing benchmark evaluates multiple fusion architectures under both failure modes at controlled severity levels across diverse clinical datasets. We present MuteBench, a benchmark covering 9 datasets from 7 clinical domains, 6 fusion architectures, and 2 missing-data modes over 125,000 samples. Through this benchmark, we find that architecture family is the strongest predictor of robustness, outweighing parameter count. Channel-independent models tolerate modality missing well but can be sensitive to within-modality missing, especially on short sequences. Curriculum modality dropout protects reliably only up to the maximum dropout rate used in training. We also find that channel count, sequence length, and modality alignment jointly determine which failure mode poses the greater threat. Finally, a PTB-XL case study suggests that diffusion-based imputation can improve downstream classification under within-modality missing, with the largest gains for models whose expert routing is most sensitive to corrupted inputs, though broader validation across datasets remains an open direction. MuteBench provides practitioners with concrete guidance for both selecting existing architectures and informing the design of future robust multimodal fusion methods.

</details>


### [180] [Reducing the Safety Tax in LLM Safety Alignment with On-Policy Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15239)
*Yu Fu,Longxuan Yu,Haz Sameen Shahgir,Zhipeng Wei,Hui Liu,N. Benjamin Erichson,Yue Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes OPSA, an on-policy self-distillation method for safety alignment that mitigates the "safety tax" (tradeoff between safety and reasoning) by having a model learn from its own rollouts with per-token KL supervision from a frozen teacher copy, using a novel "teacher flip rate" metric to identify safety-activating contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety alignment suffers from a "safety tax" — improvement in robustness to harmful queries comes at the cost of reasoning ability. The authors identify off-policy training mismatch (distributions from humans/external models vs. the model's own policy) as an overlooked source of this tradeoff, motivating the need for on-policy alignment.

Method: OPSA performs on-policy self-distillation where the target model generates its own rollouts and receives dense per-token KL divergence supervision from a frozen teacher copy of itself, conditioned on a "privileged safety context." A novel "teacher flip rate" metric measures how often the privileged context converts unsafe responses to safe ones, used to search for contexts that activate latent safety reasoning.

Result: Across two reasoning-model families (R1-Distill, Qwen3) and five model scales, OPSA achieves better safety-reasoning tradeoffs than off-policy self-distillation and external-teacher distillation. Largest gains on smaller models: +8.85 points (R1-Distill-1.5B) and +5.49 points (Qwen3-0.6B). Gains persist across training-set sizes and adaptive jailbreak evaluations. Token-level analysis shows updates concentrate near early compliance-decision tokens.

Conclusion: On-policy self-distillation (OPSA) effectively mitigates the safety tax by addressing off-policy training mismatch. The method's token-level update concentration near compliance decisions provides a mechanistic explanation for preserving reasoning while improving safety. The teacher flip rate is a useful criterion for identifying effective safety contexts.

Abstract: Safety alignment often improves robustness to harmful queries at the cost of reasoning ability, a tradeoff known as the safety tax. A common cause is distributional mismatch: supervised fine-tuning trains the target model on safety demonstrations produced by humans, external models, or fixed self-generated traces, rather than on trajectories sampled from its own policy. We identify off-policy training mismatch as a second source of this tax and study on-policy self-distillation for safety alignment, which we call OPSA. The model generates its own rollouts and receives dense per-token KL supervision from a frozen teacher copy of itself conditioned on a privileged safety context. Because this teacher must be safer than the sampled student trajectory, we introduce \emph{teacher flip rate}: a criterion that measures how often a privileged context converts unsafe responses into safe ones. We use this signal to search for contexts that activate latent safety reasoning rather than merely elicit safe-looking demonstrations. Across two reasoning-model families and five model scales, OPSA achieves a stronger safety--reasoning tradeoff than off-policy self-distillation and external-teacher distillation under matched data and full-parameter fine-tuning, with the largest gains on smaller models (+8.85 points on R1-Distill-1.5B and +5.49 points on Qwen3-0.6B). The gains persist across training-set sizes and adaptive jailbreak evaluations. Token-level analyses further show that OPSA concentrates updates near early compliance-decision tokens, providing a mechanism for improving safety while preserving general reasoning.

</details>


### [181] [Logical Grammar Induction via Graph Kolmogorov Complexity: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Self-Healing Clinical Data Integrity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15242)
*Abolfazl Zarghani,Amir Malekesfandiari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Logic-GNN is a neuro-symbolic framework that treats clinical records as a structured "private language" governed by latent logical games, using Temporal Graph Neural Networks and Graph Kolmogorov Complexity to detect data entry errors by identifying "grammatical violations" that expand Minimum Description Length, achieving 0.94 F1-score on the Sina System dataset and outperforming baselines by 12%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Healthcare Information Systems suffer from human-induced data entry errors that existing statistical anomaly detection methods cannot reliably distinguish from legitimate clinical outliers (life-threatening but medically valid extreme values).

Method: The paper proposes Logic-GNN, which integrates Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNN) with Graph Kolmogorov Complexity to induce a symbolic grammar representing clinical interactions. Anomalies are defined as "grammatical violations" that cause significant expansion in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) of the clinical graph, with a self-healing mechanism for real-time corrections.

Result: Evaluated on the Sina System dataset (2M+ records), Logic-GNN achieves an F1-score of 0.94, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by 12% in distinguishing life-threatening medical outliers from data corruption.

Conclusion: Logic-GNN effectively differentiates between genuine clinical extremes and data entry errors by modeling medical records as a structured logical language, and introduces a practical self-healing mechanism for maintaining data integrity in real-time HIS environments.

Abstract: The reliability of Healthcare Information Systems (HIS) is frequently compromised by human-induced data entry errors, which existing statistical anomaly detection methods fail to distinguish from legitimate clinical extremes. This paper proposes Logic-GNN, a novel neuro-symbolic framework that treats clinical records as a structured ``private language'' governed by latent logical games. By integrating Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNN) with Graph Kolmogorov Complexity, we induce a symbolic grammar that represents the underlying logic of medical interactions. We define anomalies as ``grammatical violations'' that cause a significant expansion in the Minimum Description Length (MDL) of the clinical graph. Evaluated on the Sina System dataset (2M+ records), Logic-GNN achieves an F1-score of 0.94, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by 12\% in distinguishing between life-threatening medical outliers and data corruption. Our approach introduces a self-healing mechanism that suggests logical corrections to maintain data integrity in real-time HIS environments.

</details>


### [182] [Reading the Cell, Designing the Cure: Perturbation-Conditioned Molecular Diffusion for Function-Oriented Drug Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15243)
*Ziyu Xu,Zijian Zhang,Liang Wang,Zhiyuan Liu,Qiang Liu,Shu Wu,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CURE (Cellular Response Engine) is a multi-resolution transcriptome-guided diffusion framework for drug molecule design conditioned on desired transcriptomic state transitions, featuring a specialized TFE module to bridge biology-chemistry domain gaps and handle noisy transcriptomic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing drug design methods lack reliable target structures at scale or cannot capture system-level functional readouts from dysregulated pathways, motivating a phenotype-driven generative approach conditioned on transcriptomic perturbations.

Method: The paper proposes CURE, a multi-resolution transcriptome-guided diffusion framework with a Transcriptome Perturbation Functional Feature Extractor (TFE) that: (1) distills function-oriented perturbation embeddings, (2) aligns signatures to dual chemical views for cross-modal bridging, and (3) performs heterogeneity-aware aggregation for robust signal extraction.

Result: CURE consistently outperforms strong baselines in structural quality and functional consistency on both standard benchmarks and rigorous out-of-distribution protocols; validated via zero-shot gene-inhibitor design.

Conclusion: Transcriptome-based drug design is a viable phenotype-driven generative paradigm, with CURE demonstrating practical utility for zero-shot gene-inhibitor discovery when target structures are unavailable.

Abstract: When reliable target structures are unavailable at scale or phenotypes arise from dysregulated pathways, transcriptomic perturbations provide a system-level functional readout for drug action. In this work, we formalize \emph{Transcriptome-based Drug Design (TBDD)} as a generative inverse problem: designing drug molecules conditioned on desired transcriptomic state transitions. We analyze the inherently ill-posed nature of this task, which is further complicated by the profound domain gap between biology and chemistry and by the sparsity of transcriptomic signals. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{\themodel{}} (A \textbf{C}ell\textbf{U}lar \textbf{R}esponse \textbf{E}ngine), a multi-resolution transcriptome-guided diffusion framework. \themodel{} features a specialized \textbf{Transcriptome Perturbation Functional Feature Extractor (TFE)} that (1) distills function-oriented perturbation embeddings from pre/post states, (2) aligns these signatures to dual chemical views to bridge the cross-modal gap, and (3) performs heterogeneity-aware aggregation to extract robust state-specific signals from noisy transcriptomic data. Extensive evaluations on both standard benchmarks and rigorous out-of-distribution protocols demonstrate that \themodel{} consistently outperforms strong baselines in structural quality and functional consistency. Furthermore, we validate its practical utility via a zero-shot gene-inhibitor design task, highlighting the potential of phenotype-driven generative discovery.

</details>


### [183] [Privacy Evaluation of Generative Models for Trajectory Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15246)
*Stavros Bouras,Ioannis Kontopoulos,Chiara Pugliese,Francesco Lettich,Emanuele Carlini,Hanna Kavalionak,Chiara Renso,Konstantinos Tserpes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates privacy risks in generative trajectory models, finding that these models, despite their generative nature, remain vulnerable to membership inference attacks, highlighting a significant gap in privacy evaluation practices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Trajectory data is sensitive but essential for urban intelligence. Generative models are assumed to preserve privacy automatically, but this assumption is unverified, motivating the need for empirical privacy evaluation.

Method: The authors identify applicable empirical privacy evaluation methods and implement Membership Inference Attacks against representative generative trajectory models (GANs, VAEs, Diffusion Models).

Result: They demonstrate that generative trajectory models remain vulnerable to Membership Inference Attacks, confirming that generative nature does not guarantee privacy protection.

Conclusion: There is a significant gap in privacy evaluation for generative trajectory models, and empirical attack methods like Membership Inference Attacks should be adopted to assess actual privacy risks.

Abstract: Trajectory data is fundamental to modern urban intelligence, yet its sensitivity raises significant privacy concerns. Generative models such as Generative Adversarial Networks, Variational Autoencoders, and Diffusion Models have been developed to generate realistic synthetic trajectory data by capturing underlying spatiotemporal distributions and mobility patterns. Although these models are often assumed to preserve privacy due to their generative nature, this assumption does not necessarily hold. In this work, we investigate the intersection of generative trajectory modeling and privacy evaluation. By identifying applicable empirical methods for assessing privacy preservation in trajectory generation tasks, we demonstrate a significant gap in the evaluation of privacy for generative trajectory models. Motivated by this gap, we implement Membership Inference Attacks against representative models, demonstrating the feasibility of using such empirical privacy evaluation methods and showing that their generative nature does not eliminate privacy risks.

</details>


### [184] [GQLA: Group-Query Latent Attention for Hardware-Adaptive Large Language Model Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15250)
*Fanxu Meng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GQLA modifies MLA to support both MQA and GQA decoding paths from the same weights, enabling optimal performance across different hardware (H100 vs H20) without retraining, and includes a conversion method (TransGQLA) to adapt pretrained GQA models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLA used in DeepSeek-V2/V3 achieves excellent H100 roofline performance but its single MQA decoding path limits efficiency on commodity GPUs (e.g., H20), lacks tensor parallelism along the head axis, and cannot leverage Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) on such hardware.

Method: GQLA minimally modifies MLA trained weights to expose two algebraically equivalent decoding paths: an MQA-absorb path (like MLA) and a GQA path with per-group expanded cache. The runtime selects the appropriate path for the target hardware. TransGQLA extends TransMLA to convert pretrained GQA checkpoints into GQLA models.

Result: On H100, GQLA matches MLA's roofline performance via MQA-absorb path; on H20, it achieves better efficiency via GQA path with MTP and s_q=2. Supports up to 8-way zero-redundancy tensor parallelism. On LLaMA-3-8B, TransGQLA compresses KV cache to 28.125% of GQA baseline on MQA-absorb path while preserving GQA-level traffic on the per-group path.

Conclusion: GQLA provides a flexible, hardware-adaptive attention mechanism requiring minimal changes from MLA, enabling efficient deployment across diverse hardware without retraining.

Abstract: Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA), the attention used in DeepSeek-V2/V3, jointly compresses keys and values into a low-rank latent and matches the H100 roofline almost perfectly. Its trained weights, however, expose only one decoding path - an absorbed MQA form - which ties efficient inference to H100-class compute-bandwidth ratios, forfeits tensor parallelism along the head axis, and yields no Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) gain on commodity inference GPUs such as the export-restricted H20. We propose Group-Query Latent Attention (GQLA), a minimal modification of MLA whose trained weights expose two algebraically equivalent decoding paths over the same parameters: an MQA-absorb path identical to MLA's, and a GQA path with a per-group expanded cache. The runtime picks the path that matches the target hardware - no retraining, no custom kernels - so a single set of GQLA weights pins the rooflines of both H100 (MQA-absorb, s_q=1) and H20 (GQA + MTP, s_q=2), while supporting up to 8-way zero-redundancy tensor parallelism on the GQA path. To avoid pretraining from scratch we extend TransMLA into TransGQLA, which converts a pretrained GQA checkpoint into a GQLA model; on LLaMA-3-8B it compresses the per-token KV cache to 28.125% of the GQA baseline on the MQA-absorb path while structurally preserving GQA-level traffic on the per-group path.

</details>


### [185] [PDRNN: Modular Data-driven Pedestrian Dead Reckoning on Loosely Coupled Radio- and Inertial-Signalstreams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15252)
*Peter Bauer,Andreas Porada,Felix Ott,Christopher Mutschler,Tobias Feigl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A modular RNN-based PDR system (PDRNN) that predicts asynchronous sensor data streams and fuses orientation, velocity, and position estimates with uncertainty awareness, outperforming classic and ML methods on dynamic sports data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PDR systems struggle with asynchronous sensor streams, sampling rate discrepancies, unreliable transmission, and poor fusion of multimodal data during high-dynamic movements.

Method: Proposes PDRNN, a modular hybrid AI system using separate ML models for orientation, velocity/distance, and absolute positioning, with a final fusion model that incorporates uncertainty estimates. A simple RNN architecture forecasts asynchronous sensor data streams implicitly.

Result: Superior accuracy and precision on dynamic sports movement data compared to classic and other ML-based methods; effectively avoids error accumulation common in black-box approaches.

Conclusion: PDRNN offers better component control, forecast capabilities, and robustness despite increased complexity, making it suitable for high-dynamic PDR applications.

Abstract: Modern pedestrian dead reckoning (PDR) systems rely on fusing noisy and biased estimates of position, velocity, and calibrated orientation derived from loosely coupled sensors to determine the current pose of a localized object. However, discrepancies in the sampling rates of sensor-specific estimation methods and unreliable transmission pose significant challenges. And traditional methods often fail to effectively fuse multimodal sensor data during dynamic movements characterized by high accelerations, velocities, and rapidly varying orientations. To address these limitations, we propose a simple recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture capable of implicitly forecasting asynchronous sensor data streams from diverse estimation methods along reference trajectories. The proposed approach introduces PDRNN, a modular hybrid AI-assisted PDR system that handles each component as an independent ensemble of machine learning (ML) models to estimate both key parameter means and variances. Separate ML-based models are employed to estimate orientation, (un)directed velocity or distance from acceleration and gyroscope data, with optional absolute positioning from synchronized radio systems such as 5G for stabilization. A final fusion model combines these outputs, position, velocity, and orientation, while using uncertainty estimates to enhance system robustness. The modular design allows individual components to be updated, fine-tuned, or replaced without affecting the entire system.
