Result: DeltaPrompts yields up to 15% relative improvement averaged over 10 benchmarks (chart, document, perception-centric reasoning), even on top of a highly-optimized reasoning model (Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking). Gains hold across on-policy distillation, transfer to novel model families, and off-policy fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Non-zero divergence (Δ) is critical for effective scaling in VLM distillation. Actively targeting student failure modes through synthetic high-divergence prompts substantially outperforms standard heuristic-based or dataset-aggregated prompt selection.

Abstract: Distillation enables compact Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to obtain strong reasoning capabilities, yet the prompts driving this process are typically chosen via simple heuristics or aggregated from off-the-shelf datasets. We reveal a critical inefficiency in this approach: up to 69% of the prompts in standard chart / document reasoning datasets are effectively zero-delta, meaning the teacher and student already induce the exact same answer distribution. Training on these prompts provides minimal learning signal, causing student improvement to rapidly saturate regardless of data scale. To escape the zero-delta trap, we return to first principles: distillation fundamentally minimizes distributional divergence, and thus a prompt is valuable only if it exposes a functional capability gap between the teacher and student. We quantify this gap through answer divergence ($Δ$), demonstrating that non-zero divergence is critical for effective scaling. Building on this insight, we propose a staged synthesis pipeline that repurposes existing datasets as seeds, actively targeting student failure modes to produce better prompts. The result is DeltaPrompts, a diverse dataset of 200k synthetic, high-divergence reasoning problems. We evaluate DeltaPrompts across three distinct settings: on-policy distillation with the target teacher-student pair, transfer to a novel model family without regenerating the data, and off-policy fine-tuning of a non-reasoning model. Across all scenarios, DeltaPrompts drives substantial gains, yielding up to 15% relative improvement even on top of a highly-optimized reasoning model (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking) -- averaged over 10 benchmarks spanning chart, document and perception-centric reasoning.

</details>


### [239] [CTF4Nuclear: Common Task Framework for Nuclear Fission and Fusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15549)
*Stefano Riva,Carolina Introini,Antonio Cammi,Dean Price,Alexey Yermakov,Yue Zhao,Philippe M. Wyder,Judah Goldfeder,Jan Williams,Amy Sara Rude,Matteo Tomasetto,Joe Germany,Joseph Bakarji,Georg Maierhofer,Miles Cranmer,J. Nathan Kutz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Common Task Framework (CTF) for standardizing and benchmarking Machine Learning methods in nuclear engineering, using curated datasets and 12 evaluation metrics to address the need for rigorous, reproducible comparisons of data-driven surrogate models for complex nuclear systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nuclear system design and operation are challenged by complex multi-physics interactions. High-fidelity simulations are computationally expensive, and model-based approaches suffer from simplifying assumptions. A fair, standardized comparison of diverse ML methods is critically needed for safety-critical nuclear applications.

Method: The authors propose a Common Task Framework (CTF) with curated datasets from nuclear and nuclear-adjacent systems, 12 established metrics, and a new paradigm for system monitoring from sparse measurements. They benchmark standard ML baselines to illustrate the framework.

Result: The benchmarking of standard ML baselines revealed current method limitations across the curated datasets and metrics within the CTF.

Conclusion: The CTF aims to replace ad hoc ML comparisons with standardized evaluations on hidden test sets, improving rigor and reproducibility in scientific ML for nuclear engineering.

Abstract: The demand for clean energy is ever increasing, with new nuclear technologies presenting a complementary solution to renewable energies. However, designing and operating these systems is exceptionally difficult, given the complexity of the physical phenomena that interact to form the system dynamics. While high-fidelity simulations help to understand the non-linear, multi-physics interactions within a reactor, they are computationally expensive and rarely suitable for real-time applications. Furthermore, model-based approaches are inherently sensitive to simplifying assumptions required to derive their governing equations and parameters, leading to inevitable discrepancies with real-world measurements. In contrast, Machine Learning (ML) methods have the potential to generate reliable surrogate models which may be able to quickly predict the system's behaviour. However, the number of data-driven methods that can potentially be used for this task is large and diverse. In a safety-critical setting such as nuclear engineering, a fair comparison of different ML methods, and a clear understanding of their advantages and limitations, is of paramount importance. To address this, we introduce a Common Task Framework (CTF) for ML in nuclear engineering, building upon previous efforts in dynamical systems and seismology. This CTF considers a curated set of datasets from different nuclear and nuclear-adjacent systems. The CTF evaluates the performance of a method on 12 established metrics, alongside a new paradigm focused on system monitoring from sparse measurements only. We illustrate the framework by benchmarking standard ML baselines against these datasets, revealing current method limitations. Our vision is to replace ad hoc comparisons with standardized evaluations on hidden test sets, raising the bar for rigour and reproducibility in scientific ML for the nuclear industry.

</details>


### [240] [Characterizing Learning in Deep Neural Networks using Tractable Algorithmic Complexity Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15551)
*Pedram Bakhtiarifard,Sophia N. Wilson,Mahmoud Afifi,Jonathan Wenshøj,Raghavendra Selvan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces QuBD, a scalable method for estimating Kolmogorov-Chaitin-Solomonoff complexity of deep neural network weights, and shows that algorithmic complexity decreases during training, correlates with generalization, and is concentrated in most significant bit-planes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing algorithmic complexity estimators (CTM, BDM) are limited to small binary objects and cannot scale to modern DNNs, limiting the study of the "learning as compression" hypothesis in large-scale networks.

Method: QuBD quantizes network weights to a finite k-ary alphabet, then estimates KCS complexity by aggregating CTM estimates per bit-plane, yielding a tighter estimation gap than binarization-based methods.

Result: Algorithmic complexity of weights decreases during learning, scales with data budget, increases during overfitting, follows delayed generalization in grokking, and correlates with generalization performance. Most algorithmic information resides in the most significant bit-planes.

Conclusion: QuBD provides the first scalable, tractable KCS complexity estimates for large non-binary objects like DNN weights, offering practical diagnostics for post-training quantization and new insights into learning mechanisms.

Abstract: Training large-scale deep neural networks (DNNs) is resource-intensive, making model compression a practical necessity. The widely accepted ''learning as compression'' hypothesis posits that training induces structure in network weights, which enables compression. Measuring this structure through Kolmogorov-Chaitin-Solomonoff (KCS) complexity is appealing, but existing estimators based on the Coding Theorem Method (CTM) and the Block Decomposition Method (BDM) are limited to small binary objects and do not scale to modern DNNs. We introduce the Quantized Block Decomposition method (QuBD), which extends algorithmic complexity estimation to any $k$-ary object. QuBD first quantizes the network weights to a finite alphabet, then estimates the KCS complexity by aggregating per bit-plane CTM estimates. We show theoretically that QuBD yields a strictly tighter estimation gap with respect to true KCS complexity than binarization-based methods. Using QuBD, we study how the algorithmic complexity of neural network weights evolves during training, showing that it decreases as models learn, scales with data budget, increases during overfitting, follows the delayed generalization observed during grokking, and correlates with generalization performance. We further show that algorithmic information resides predominantly in the most significant bit-planes, which can serve as a practical diagnostic for determining appropriate post-training quantization levels. This work offers novel insights into learning mechanisms in DNNs by providing the first scalable, tractable estimates of KCS complexity for large, non-binary objects such as DNN weights.

</details>


### [241] [AstraFlow: Dataflow-Oriented Reinforcement Learning for Agentic LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15565)
*Haizhong Zheng,Yizhuo Di,Jiahui Wang,Shuowei Jin,Xueshen Liu,Yongji Wu,Z. Morley Mao,Ion Stoica,Jiawei Zhao,Beidi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AstraFlow is a dataflow-oriented RL system that decouples rollout, dataflow, and training into autonomous components, enabling efficient multi-policy collaborative training and elastic, heterogeneous, cross-region execution without system-level code changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM RL systems rely on trainer-centered control architectures that require dedicated system engineering for each new extension, making agentic RL prohibitively expensive and difficult to scale, especially for complex multi-policy workloads across diverse compute resources.

Method: AstraFlow replaces trainer-centered control with a dataflow-oriented architecture that decouples rollout services, dataflow management, and training into autonomous components, allowing native support for multi-policy agentic RL and efficient use of elastic, heterogeneous, cross-region compute resources.

Result: Across math, code, search, and AgentBench workloads, AstraFlow supports multi-policy training, elastic scaling, heterogeneous cross-region execution, and composable data algorithms without system-level code changes. In multi-policy collaborative training, it achieves comparable or better accuracy with 2.7x training speedup.

Conclusion: Dataflow-oriented architectures provide a principled abstraction for RL systems, enabling scalable and flexible agentic RL without costly system-level redesign for each new capability.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is increasingly used to improve the reasoning, coding, and tool-use capabilities of large language models, but agentic RL remains prohibitively expensive. Scaling RL to agentic LLMs requires supporting complex workloads, including multi-policy collaborative training, while efficiently using elastic, heterogeneous, and cross-region compute resources. Existing LLM RL systems support some of these capabilities, but each new extension often requires dedicated system engineering. This burden arises from trainer-centered control architectures and the lack of principled abstractions for RL system components. To address these limitations, we propose AstraFlow, a dataflow-oriented RL system that replaces conventional trainer-centered control with principled component abstractions. In AstraFlow, rollout services, dataflow management, and training are decoupled into autonomous components, enabling the system to natively support complex multi-policy agentic RL workloads and efficiently exploit diverse compute resources. We evaluate AstraFlow across math, code, search, and AgentBench workloads, showing that the same system supports multi-policy training, elastic scaling, heterogeneous cross-region execution, and composable data algorithms without system-level code changes. In multi-policy collaborative training, AstraFlow achieves comparable or better accuracy than existing RL systems while speeding up training time by 2.7x.

</details>


### [242] [Gaussian Relational Graph Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15575)
*Zezhong Ding,Jin Li,Xugang Wang,Xike Xie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GelGT is a Gaussian relational graph transformer that improves relational graph learning by capturing long-range dependencies through structure-semantic collaborative sampling and Gaussian graph attention with temporal encoding, achieving up to 13.8% better predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing relational graph learning methods struggle with long-range dependencies due to information decay in message-passing, and current relational graph transformers fail to jointly model structural, semantic, and temporal information.

Method: GelGT introduces (1) a structure-semantic collaborative sampling strategy to preserve structural connectivity while filtering irrelevant semantics, and (2) a Gaussian graph attention mechanism with a learnable Gaussian bias on sampled subgraphs to dynamically encode temporal dependencies.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art downstream task performance with up to 13.8% improvement in predictive performance on real-world datasets.

Conclusion: GelGT effectively addresses long-range dependency and multi-modal information joint modeling challenges in relational graph learning, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Relational graph learning models relational databases as graphs and has demonstrated superior performance on a wide range of relational predictive tasks. However, existing methods struggle to capture long-range dependencies due to information decay in their message-passing mechanisms, and recent relational graph transformers remain limited in jointly modeling structural, semantic, and temporal information. In this paper, we propose GelGT, a Gaussian relational graph transformer that explicitly addresses these challenges. GelGT introduces a structure-semantic collaborative sampling strategy to preserve structural connectivity while filtering irrelevant semantic information, and incorporates a Gaussian graph attention mechanism with a learnable Gaussian bias on the sampled subgraphs to dynamically encode temporal dependencies. Extensive experiments on various real-world datasets demonstrate that GelGT achieves state-of-the-art downstream task performance, with up to a 13.8% improvement in predictive performance.

</details>


### [243] [Embracing Biased Transition Matrices for Complementary-Label Learning with Many Classes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15586)
*Tan-Ha Mai,Chao-Kai Chiang,Han-Hwa Shih,Gang Niu,Masashi Sugiyama,Hsuan-Tien Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Complementary-label learning (CLL) is advanced by deliberately introducing biased (non-uniform) label generation, overcoming the bottleneck of scaling to many-class problems. The proposed BICL framework achieves over 7x accuracy improvements on CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet-200.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional CLL methods assume uniform complementary label distribution, which dilutes learning signals in large label spaces. Scaling to many-class classification remains an enduring bottleneck.

Method: Bias-Induced Constrained Labeling (BICL) — a framework that deliberately designs a biased (non-uniform) complementary label generation process, restricting labels to a subset of classes, spanning both data collection and training.

Result: Achieves more than sevenfold accuracy improvements over traditional methods on CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet-200.

Conclusion: The long-standing barrier of scaling CLL to many classes can be overcome by embracing biased label generation, establishing a new trajectory for real-world many-class CLL applications.

Abstract: Complementary-label learning (CLL) is a weakly supervised paradigm where instances are labeled with classes they do not belong to. Despite a decade of research, CLL methods remain competitive mainly on 10-class classification, with scaling to large label spaces continuing to be an enduring bottleneck. This limitation stems from the common assumption of uniform label generation in traditional methods, which fatally dilutes the learning signal in many-class settings. In this paper, we demonstrate that this long-standing barrier can be overcome by deliberately designing a biased (non-uniform) generation process that restricts complementary labels to a subset of classes. This finding motivates us to propose Bias-Induced Constrained Labeling (BICL), a principled framework spanning data collection to training that leverages this bias. BICL enables effective learning on CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet-200, achieving more than sevenfold accuracy improvements over traditional methods. Our findings establish a new trajectory for making CLL feasible for many classes in real-world applications.

</details>


### [244] [Offline Reinforcement Learning with Universal Horizon Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15603)
*Hojun Chung,Junseo Lee,Songhwai Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UHM generalizes geometric horizon models to predict future states under arbitrary horizons, using a winsorized horizon distribution for stable value learning in offline model-based RL, outperforming baselines on OGBench tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model-based RL suffers from compounding errors due to repeated model inference, and geometric horizon models (GHM) struggle with accurately modeling distant future states.

Method: Introduces universal horizon models (UHM) that predict future states under arbitrary horizons, paired with a winsorized horizon distribution to cap excessively large horizons for training stability.

Result: Outperforms competitive baselines on 100 OGBench tasks, especially on highly suboptimal datasets and long-horizon reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: UHM provides a flexible and stable approach for offline model-based RL, effectively addressing compounding errors and long-horizon challenges.

Abstract: Model-based reinforcement learning (RL) offers a compelling approach to offline RL by enabling value learning on imagined on-policy trajectories. However, it often suffers from compounding errors due to repeated model inference on self-generated states. While geometric horizon models (GHM) alleviate this issue through direct prediction over a discounted infinite-horizon future, they remain challenged in accurately modeling distant future states. To this end, we introduce universal horizon models (UHM), a generalization of GHM that directly predicts future states under arbitrary horizons. Leveraging this flexibility, we propose a scalable value learning method that employs a winsorized horizon distribution to stabilize training by capping excessively large horizons. Experimental results on 100 challenging OGBench tasks demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms competitive baselines, particularly on tasks with highly suboptimal datasets and those requiring long-horizon reasoning. Project page: https://rllab-snu.github.io/projects/UHM/

</details>


### [245] [VSPO: Vector-Steered Policy Optimization for Behavioral Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15604)
*Xuechen Zhang,Zijian Huang,Kai Yang,Weijia Zhang,Jiasi Chen,Samet Oymak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VSPO modifies GRPO with steering vectors to control behavioral preferences (e.g., verbosity, confidence) while maintaining accuracy, showing improved performance over reward shaping across reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models need to optimize accuracy while accommodating secondary behavioral preferences, but target behaviors are often rare in base models, creating a sparse behavioral reward bottleneck that hinders policy optimization.

Method: VSPO modifies GRPO by sampling rollouts with varying steering intensities, creating an on-policy latent self-distillation procedure where the model internalizes its steering vector, upsampling rare behaviors and enriching rollout diversity.

Result: VSPO achieves better iteration complexity than reward-shaped GRPO when steering distributions align with target behavior, and consistently improves behavioral control while maintaining/task accuracy across MATH, MMLU-Pro, and four target behaviors.

Conclusion: VSPO provably accelerates policy optimization under sparse behavioral rewards and outperforms reward shaping, teacher-trace distillation, and guidance-based baselines in both theory and experiments.

Abstract: Modern language models often need to optimize a primary accuracy objective while also accommodating secondary behavioral preferences, such as verbosity, agreeableness, or the level of technical expertise in its response. In practice, a base model may exhibit a desired behavior very rarely or not at all. Thus, endowing the model with a target behavior creates a sparse behavioral reward bottleneck. To address such multi-objective problems, we introduce Vector-Steered Policy Optimization (VSPO) which employs a steering vector associated with the target behavior to control the behavior intensity of the generated rollouts. VSPO is obtained by modifying GRPO to sample rollouts with varying steering intensities. This process can be interpreted as an on-policy latent self-distillation procedure where the model internalizes its steering vector. By varying steering intensities, VSPO upsamples rare behaviors and enriches rollout diversity, which alleviates the sparse reward issue and provably accelerates the policy optimization. Through comprehensive theory and experiments, we establish that VSPO has favorable properties compared to vanilla reward shaping and other alternative approaches. Specifically, under a bandit abstraction, VSPO provably achieves better iteration complexity than reward-shaped GRPO when the steering-induced distributions are sufficiently aligned with the target behavior. We evaluate VSPO across multiple reasoning benchmarks, including MATH and MMLU-Pro, for four target behaviors: explanation expertise, confidence expression, robustness to misleading context, and response verbosity. Our results show that VSPO consistently improves the control along target behavior while maintaining or improving task accuracy compared with reward shaping, teacher-trace distillation, and guidance-based baselines.

</details>


### [246] [Transformer-like Inference from Optimal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15608)
*Aditya Kudre,Heng-Sheng Chang,Prashant G. Mehta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper derives transformer-like architectures from first principles by reformulating next-token prediction as an optimal control problem, recovering decoder-only transformer layers as the solution (the "dual filter"), and showing through experiments that transformers exploit non-Markovian structure when embedding dimension is limited.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a principled derivation of transformer architectures from optimal control theory, moving beyond heuristic design by showing that decoder-only layer structures emerge naturally from solving the next-token prediction problem under optimal control theory.

Method: Derives inference architectures from first principles by reformulating the next-token prediction problem as an optimal control problem for two model classes: a nonlinear discrete-valued process model and a linear Gaussian baseline. The solution yields an algorithm called the "dual filter" with a layer structure mirroring decoder-only transformers.

Result: The dual filter's layer structure mirrors the structure of decoder-only transformers. Numerical experiments comparing optimal control to trained transformer attention weights reveal that transformers implicitly exploit non-Markovian structure when the embedding dimension is insufficient.

Conclusion: Transformer architectures can be derived from first principles via optimal control theory, providing a theoretical foundation for their design, and experiments show that limited embedding dimensions lead transformers to exploit non-Markovian patterns.

Abstract: Decoder-only transformers compute the conditional probability of the next token from a sequence of past observations. This paper derives, from first principles, inference architectures that solve the same prediction problem - and in doing so, recovers transformer-like layer operations as a consequence of optimal control theory. The framework is developed for two model classes: a nonlinear model of discrete-valued processes, directly motivated by the transformer, and a linear Gaussian model as a tractable baseline. For both model classes, the prediction objective is reformulated as an optimal control problem whose solution yields an explicit inference algorithm, the dual filter, with a layer structure that mirrors the layer structure of a decoder-only transformer. Numerical experiments provide a comparison of the optimal control to attention weights from a trained transformer. These experiments reveal that when the embedding dimension is insufficient, the transformer implicitly exploits non-Markovian structure.

</details>


### [247] [Position: Zeroth-Order Optimization in Deep Learning Is Underexplored, Not Underpowered](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15622)
*Sijia Liu,Yicheng Lang,Soumyadeep Pal,Changsheng Wang,Yancheng Huang,Chongyu Fan,James Diffenderfer,Bhavya Kailkhura,Yihua Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper argues that Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization, often dismissed as unscalable due to variance and query complexity, is actually underexplored rather than underpowered. The authors identify key limitations from current myopic practices and propose six positions covering algorithms, systems, and evaluation. They advocate for subspace/spectral approaches, leveraging ZO's forward-only nature for system advantages, and decoupling evaluation from task complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the prevailing view that ZO optimization is fundamentally unscalable for deep learning, and to show that its perceived limitations stem from narrow development practices rather than inherent weaknesses.

Method: The authors articulate six positions across three stacks: (1) revisiting estimator-centric ZO methods through variance control, variance-query tradeoffs, and directional derivatives; (2) identifying underexplored opportunities including subspace/spectral views for variance reduction, forward-only nature as a systems advantage, and de-obfuscating evaluation from task complexity.

Result: ZO optimization is not fundamentally underpowered but underexplored; current limitations arise from full-space, element-wise, estimator-centric designs rather than inherent constraints.

Conclusion: The field should rethink ZO optimization around its unique strengths—memory efficiency, forward-only computation, pipeline-friendliness—and pursue large-scale, system-aware, resource-efficient learning with ZO.

Abstract: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization, learning from finite differences of function evaluations without backpropagation, has recently regained attention in deep learning due to its memory efficiency and applicability to gray- or black-box pipelines. Yet, ZO methods are often dismissed as fundamentally unscalable because of estimator variance and unfavorable query complexity. We argue that this conclusion might be misguided: ZO optimization is underexplored, not underpowered. We show that many perceived limitations stem from myopic development practices, most notably full-space, element-wise, estimator-centric designs. We articulate six positions spanning the algorithmic, systems, and evaluation stack. First, we revisit the feasibility boundaries of estimator-centric ZO methods through variance control, variance-query tradeoffs, and directional-derivative lenses. Then, we identify three underexplored opportunities: (i) subspace and spectral views of ZO that enable interpretable variance reduction with graceful query scaling, (ii) the forward-only nature of ZO as a systems advantage for communication-efficient, pipeline-friendly, and resource-constrained training, and (iii) the need to de-obfuscate ZO evaluations from task complexity. We strongly advocate rethinking ZO optimization around its unique strengths and acting accordingly, opening a viable path toward large-scale, system-aware, and resource-efficient learning with ZO optimization.

</details>


### [248] [IO-SVD: Input-Output Whitened SVD for Adaptive-Rank LLM Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15626)
*Ali Abbasi,Chayne Thrash,Haoran Qin,Hamed Pirsiavash,Soheil Kolouri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IO-SVD is a post-training compression method that uses KL-aware double-sided whitening and heterogeneous rank allocation to reduce LLM storage/compute costs via low-rank factorization, achieving minimal performance degradation and practical speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs have high storage and compute costs that hinder deployment in resource-constrained and latency-sensitive settings. Existing SVD-based compression methods rely on input-only whitening, homogeneous rank allocation, or loss-agnostic heuristics, limiting their ability to preserve model quality under aggressive compression.

Method: IO-SVD forms a KL-aware double-sided whitening space (input-output whitening) for model weights. It uses second-order KL loss expansion over top-K token probabilities to build an output-side metric for predictive sensitivity, and input whitening for activation statistics. A heterogeneous rank-allocation strategy scores whitened singular components via first-order calibration loss estimates and prunes least sensitive ones under a global budget. For hybrid SVD-quantization, it uses loss-aware remapping to select low-rank factor rows for 8-bit quantization based on predicted loss change.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse LLM and VLM families show IO-SVD compresses models with minimal performance degradation while delivering practical inference speedups.

Conclusion: IO-SVD effectively addresses limitations of prior SVD-based compression by introducing KL-aware double-sided whitening and heterogeneous rank allocation, enabling better quality preservation under aggressive compression with real-world speed benefits.

Abstract: Large language models deliver strong performance across language and reasoning tasks, but their storage and compute costs remain major barriers to deployment in resource-constrained and latency-sensitive settings. SVD-based post-training compression offers a hardware-agnostic way to reduce model size and improve inference efficiency through low-rank factorization. However, existing methods often rely on input-only whitening spaces, homogeneous rank allocation, or loss-agnostic allocation heuristics, limiting their ability to preserve model quality under aggressive compression. We propose Input-Output Whitened SVD (IO-SVD), a post-training compression method that forms a KL-aware double-sided whitening space for model weights. Using a second-order expansion of the KL loss over the top-K token probabilities, IO-SVD constructs an output-side metric that captures predictive sensitivity, while input whitening captures activation statistics. We further introduce an efficient heterogeneous rank-allocation strategy that scores whitened singular components using first-order calibration loss estimates and prunes the least sensitive components under a global budget. Inspired by prior work that combines SVD truncation with quantization, we improve hybrid SVD-quantization compression through loss-aware remapping, which selects low-rank factor rows for 8-bit quantization based on the predicted loss change incurred by quantizing them. Extensive experiments across diverse LLM and VLM families, and inference-time analysis shows that IO-SVD compresses LLMs with minimal performance degradation while delivering practical inference speedups. Code is available at https://github.com/mint-vu/IO-SVD.git

</details>


### [249] [Perforated Neural Networks for Keyword Spotting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15647)
*Vishy Gopal,Aris Ilias Goutis,Ralph Crewe,Erin Yanacek,Rorry Brenner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Perforated Backpropagation, a technique that adds artificial Dendrite Nodes to CNNs, achieving better accuracy (0.933) with fewer parameters (1,500) compared to baseline (0.921 accuracy, 4,000 parameters) for keyword spotting on edge devices, winning Best Model at Edge Impulse 2025 Hackathon.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge machine learning faces strict trade-offs between memory, compute, and accuracy that existing compression techniques struggle to simultaneously improve; there is a need for methods that can enhance both model quality and deployment efficiency.

Method: Perforated Backpropagation with artificial Dendrite Nodes added to a standard CNN, trained on the Edge Impulse keyword spotting pipeline across 800 hyperparameter trials.

Result: Dendritic models outperformed traditional architectures at every parameter count and accuracy threshold. Best model: 0.933 accuracy with 1,500 parameters vs baseline 0.921 accuracy with 4,000 parameters.

Conclusion: Perforated Backpropagation is a powerful technique for edge AI, simultaneously improving model quality and deployment efficiency, making it a valuable addition to the edge engineer's toolkit.

Abstract: Edge machine learning presents a unique set of constraints not encountered in cloud-scale model deployment: strict memory budgets, limited compute, and non-negotiable accuracy thresholds must all be satisfied simultaneously. Existing compression and optimization techniques can trade one resource for another, but rarely improve both accuracy and model size at the same time. This paper presents the application of Perforated Backpropagation to keyword spotting on the Edge Impulse platform, an experiment that won the Best Model award at the Edge Impulse 2025 Hackathon in December 2025. By adding artificial Dendrite Nodes to a standard convolutional neural network trained on the Edge Impulse keyword spotting tutorial pipeline, we demonstrate that dendritic models outperform traditional architectures at every level of parameter count and at every accuracy threshold tested across 800 hyperparameter trials. The best dendritic model achieved a test accuracy of 0.933 with only 1,500 parameters, versus the baseline accuracy of 0.921 requiring approximately 4,000 parameters. These results suggest that Perforated Backpropagation is a powerful addition to the edge AI engineer's toolkit, offering simultaneous gains in both model quality and deployment efficiency.

</details>


### [250] [Towards Code-Oriented LM Embeddings for Surrogate-Assisted Neural Architecture Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15649)
*Pranav Somu,Advay Balakrishnan,Stepan Kravtsov,Aaron McDaniel,Jason Zutty*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes COLE (Code-Oriented LM Embeddings), a lightweight method using off-the-shelf language models to extract features from PyTorch code representations of neural architectures, achieving competitive NAS performance without expensive fine-tuning or complex representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NAS surrogate methods require expensive fine-tuning or complex representations; the authors aim to eliminate these overheads by leveraging the inductive bias of pre-trained language models on code.

Method: Represent neural architectures as PyTorch class definition text, extract embeddings using frozen off-the-shelf LMs (COLE), and train only a lightweight regression head for performance prediction.

Result: COLE outperforms other text-based encodings (e.g., ONNX-to-text) with frozen LMs on NAS-Bench-201 and einspace; achieves 34% reduction in evaluation budget to reach within 1% of the best architecture on CIFAR-100 when used with BANANAS algorithm.

Conclusion: COLE establishes a versatile, efficient, and foundation-level approach for NAS by leveraging code as a universal representation for neural architectures, eliminating the need for NAS-specialized fine-tuning.

Abstract: Developing effective surrogates (performance predictors) for Neural Architecture Search (NAS) typically requires expensive fine-tuning or the engineering of complex representations. We propose a low-cost embedding strategy that leverages the inductive bias of Language Models (LMs) to eliminate these overheads. By representing architectures as PyTorch class definition text, we demonstrate that off-the-shelf LMs act as competitive feature extractors without NAS-specialized fine-tuning. The final predictor is constructed by passing the extracted Code-Oriented LM Embeddings (COLE) through a lightweight regression head. We also investigate strategies to improve embedding quality and utilization. Our experiments on the NAS-Bench-201 and einspace search spaces reveal that raw code inputs yield higher predictive performance than other text-based encodings (e.g., ONNX-to-text encodings) when using frozen LMs. We also observe COLE drives superior surrogate-assisted search using the BANANAS algorithm in NAS-Bench-201. When optimizing for CIFAR-100 performance, replacing structural path encodings with COLE for architecture representation allows for a 34% decrease in the evaluation budget required to reach within 1% of the fittest architecture in the search space (by test accuracy). As any neural architecture can be represented as code, these findings establish COLE as a versatile and efficient foundation for advancing NAS.

</details>


### [251] [Sharp Spectral Thresholds for Logit Fixed Points](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15651)
*Tongxi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies the sharp stability threshold for softmax feedback systems (common in RL, game theory, and population dynamics), proving that the classical stability condition is too conservative. The correct threshold is β‖ΠWΠ‖_{\mathcal T→\mathcal T} < 2, which fills the previously missing pre-bifurcation regime where systems remain globally predictable despite being reward-responsive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine the exact stability boundary for softmax feedback systems, as classical theory only certifies stability in an over-regularized regime and misses a significant stable region where systems are both reward-responsive and globally predictable.

Method: The authors analyze finite-dimensional affine logit systems and derive a sharp dimension-free Euclidean threshold (β‖ΠWΠ‖_{\mathcal T→\mathcal T} < 2) via mathematical proof.

Result: The true stability threshold is β‖ΠWΠ‖_{\mathcal T→\mathcal T} < 2, which is strictly larger (more permissive) than the classical stability condition. This fills the pre-bifurcation regime and enlarges the certified stability boundary.

Conclusion: The softmax feedback system's phase transition occurs at a higher threshold than previously thought, with significant practical implications for reinforcement learning, game dynamics, and population models operating in the newly identified stable regime.

Abstract: Softmax feedback systems are a common mathematical core of entropy-regularized reinforcement learning, logit game dynamics, population choice, and mean-field variational updates. Their central stability question is simple: when does a self-reinforcing softmax system produce a unique and globally predictable outcome? Classical theory gives a conservative answer. By treating softmax as a unit-scale response, it certifies stability only in a strongly randomized regime.
  We prove that the classical approach misses an entire stable regime and does not identify the point at which the qualitative change truly occurs. For finite-dimensional affine logit systems, the sharp dimension-free Euclidean threshold is $$β\|ΠWΠ\|_{\mathcal T\to\mathcal T}<2,$$ rather than the previously used condition, which certifies stability only while the softmax system remains safely over-regularized. Our theorem fills the previously missing pre-bifurcation regime, extending stability guarantees for affine softmax feedback systems to reward-responsive yet globally predictable systems. It enlarges the certified stability boundary for these systems and identifies where the model genuinely undergoes a phase transition.

</details>


### [252] [On the Power of Adaptivity for $\varepsilon$-Best Arm Identification in Linear Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15663)
*Arnab Maiti,Yunbei Xu,Kevin Jamieson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies the sample complexity of ε-best arm identification in linear bandits, providing both non-adaptive and adaptive methods with matching lower bounds, and identifying action sets where adaptivity yields logarithmic or polynomial improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental sample complexity of ε-best arm identification in linear bandits, and to characterize when adaptive sampling provably outperforms non-adaptive methods.

Method: The paper proposes a non-adaptive fixed-design method with sample complexity involving a Gaussian width term w(𝒳), proves a matching lower bound, then studies adaptive sampling for structured action sets (hypercube, ℓ2 ball, m-sets, multi-task MAB), and finally constructs an action set where adaptivity yields polynomial improvement via an ℓ2-norm estimation subroutine.

Result: Non-adaptive methods achieve O(d log(1/δ)/ε² + w(𝒳)²/ε²) with matching lower bound. Adaptivity yields only logarithmic improvements for hypercube, ℓ2 ball, m-sets, and multi-task MAB. A constructed action set shows polynomial improvement for adaptivity.

Conclusion: The work fully characterizes non-adaptive sample complexity and reveals that while adaptivity offers limited gains for many natural action sets, there exist structured sets where adaptivity yields a polynomial separation—a key insight enabled by an ℓ2-norm estimation subroutine.

Abstract: We study the minimax sample complexity of $\varepsilon$-best arm identification in linear bandits. Given a compact action set $\mathcal{X}$ that spans $\mathbb{R}^d$ and an unknown reward vector $θ\in\mathbb{R}^d$, the goal is to output an arm $\widehat{x}\in\mathcal{X}$ such that $\langle \widehat{x},θ\rangle \ge \max_{x\in\mathcal{X}} \langle x,θ\rangle - \varepsilon$ with probability at least $1-δ$, using as few samples as possible.
  First, we present a non-adaptive fixed-design method with sample complexity $\mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{d\log(1/δ)}{\varepsilon^2}+\frac{w(\mathcal{X})^2}{\varepsilon^2}\right)$, where $w(\mathcal{X})$ is a Gaussian width term dependent on $\mathcal{X}$, and we prove a matching lower bound $Ω\!\left(\frac{d\log(1/δ)}{\varepsilon^2}+\frac{w(\mathcal{X})^2}{\varepsilon^2}\right)$ for all non-adaptive fixed-design methods.
  We then turn to adaptive sampling. We raise an important structural question: beyond the canonical basis, are there structured action sets for which adaptivity yields only logarithmic-factor improvements over the optimal non-adaptive rate? We answer in the affirmative for several natural action sets, namely the hypercube, the $\ell_2$ ball, $m$-sets, and multi-task multi-armed bandits.
  Finally, we provide the first construction of an action set $\mathcal{X}$ for which adaptivity yields a polynomial-factor improvement over every non-adaptive algorithm. A key ingredient behind this separation is an $\ell_2$-norm estimation subroutine: we design an adaptive algorithm that uses $\mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{d\log(1/δ)}{\varepsilon^2}\right)$ samples from the unit $\ell_2$ ball in $\mathbb{R}^d$ and outputs an estimate $\widehat r$ satisfying $|\widehat r-\|θ\|_2|\le \varepsilon$ with probability at least $1-δ$, where $θ$ is the unknown reward vector.

</details>


### [253] [Rule2DRC: Benchmarking LLM Agents for DRC Script Synthesis with Execution-Guided Test Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15669)
*Jinuk Kim,Junsoo Byun,Donghwi Hwang,Seong-Jin Park,Hyun Oh Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Rule2DRC is a large-scale benchmark with 1,000 rule-to-script tasks and 13,921 chip layouts for evaluating DRC script synthesis via execution-based scoring, plus SplitTester, a tester agent that improves Best-of-N selection using execution feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Translating natural language design rules into executable DRC scripts is labor-intensive and requires specialized expertise; existing benchmarks are small and evaluate by code similarity rather than execution correctness, and prior ML methods ignore execution feedback or require labeled test layouts as input.

Method: Rule2DRC provides 1,000 rule-to-script tasks with 13,921 evaluation layouts for execution-based functional correctness scoring. SplitTester uses execution feedback to generate discriminative test cases that separate indistinguishable candidate scripts for Best-of-N selection.

Result: The benchmark enables execution-based scoring without requiring evaluation layouts as agent input; SplitTester substantially improves Best-of-N selection performance in the DRC script domain.

Conclusion: Rule2DRC fills the gap in DRC script synthesis evaluation with large-scale execution-based benchmarking, and SplitTester demonstrates effective use of execution feedback for better program selection.

Abstract: Manufacturable chip layouts must satisfy thousands of geometry-based design rules, and design rule checking (DRC) enforces them by running executable DRC scripts on layouts. Translating natural language rules into correct DRC scripts is labor-intensive and requires specialized expertise, motivating LLM agents for DRC script synthesis and debugging. However, existing benchmarks have small evaluation sets and often evaluate scripts by code similarity rather than execution correctness, and prior machine learning-based methods either ignore execution feedback or require labeled test layouts as agent's input. To this end, we introduce Rule2DRC, a large-scale benchmark for DRC script coding agents with 1,000 rule-to-script tasks and 13,921 evaluation chip layouts for execution-based scoring. Rule2DRC provides an evaluation pipeline that measures functional correctness via DRC execution outcomes without requiring evaluation layouts as input to the agent. We also propose SplitTester, a tester agent for program selection that uses execution feedback to generate discriminative test cases and separate previously indistinguishable candidate scripts, substantially improving Best-of-N selection performance in this domain. We release the code at https://github.com/snu-mllab/Rule2DRC.

</details>


### [254] [Interaction-Aware Influence Functions for Group Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15675)
*Jaeseung Heo,Kyeongheung Yun,Youngbin Choi,Sehyun Hwang,Jungseul Ok,Dongwoo Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes an interaction-aware influence function that captures pairwise interactions between training examples, going beyond the standard sum of individual influences. It demonstrates improved tracking of leave-group-out retraining across multiple model architectures and beats baselines on instruction-tuning data selection for Llama-3.1-8B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard influence functions sum individual influences of group members, failing to capture how examples jointly affect the target (e.g., redundancy or complementarity). The authors aim to address this limitation.

Method: Expands the target function to second order around trained parameters, augmenting the standard sum with a pairwise interaction term that captures alignment between two examples' effects on the target.

Result: (1) On six dataset-model pairs (logistic regression, MLPs, ResNet-9), the estimator tracks leave-group-out retraining substantially better than first-order influence. (2) For Llama-3.1-8B instruction-tuning data selection, it beats prior influence-based and representation-similarity baselines on five of seven downstream tasks, while standard influence selection underperforms random selection.

Conclusion: Interaction-aware influence functions capture pairwise example interactions and outperform standard first-order influence methods, particularly in group-level influence estimation and data selection for large language models.

Abstract: Influence functions approximate how removing a training example changes a quantity of interest, called the target function, such as a held-out loss. To estimate the influence of a group of examples, the standard practice is to sum the individual influences of its members. However, this sum does not capture how examples jointly affect the target: a pair of examples may be redundant or complementary, but the sum cannot distinguish these cases. We propose an interaction-aware influence function that characterizes how interactions between examples influence the target. By expanding the target to second order around the trained parameters, we obtain an estimator that augments the standard sum with a pairwise interaction term that captures the alignment between two examples' effects on the target. We empirically evaluate our estimator in two settings. First, on six dataset-model pairs spanning logistic regression, MLPs, and ResNet-9, our estimator tracks leave-group-out retraining substantially better than first-order influence across all settings. Second, when used as a greedy selection rule for instruction-tuning data on Llama-3.1-8B, it beats prior influence-based and representation-similarity baselines on five of seven downstream tasks, in a regime where standard influence-based selection underperforms random selection.

</details>


### [255] [FRWKV+: Adaptive Periodic-Position Branch Interaction for Frequency-Space Linear Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15690)
*Qingyuan Yang,Dongyue Chen,Da Teng,Junhua Xiao,Jiaji Pan,Shizhuo Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FRWKV+ enhances frequency-space forecasting by exchanging complementary information between real/imaginary streams and using an adaptive trust mechanism to selectively incorporate periodic-position cues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two challenges in frequency-space forecasting: (1) weak information exchange between real and imaginary complex spectra streams, and (2) periodic-position cues help recurring patterns only when reliable for the dataset and prediction horizon.

Method: Proposes FRWKV+ with (1) cross-branch gates for exchanging contexts between real/imaginary frequency streams, (2) Adaptive PhaseGate mechanism to extract periodic-position context and generate signed corrections, and (3) adaptive trust mechanism to control correction strength at sample/variable/channel levels.

Result: FRWKV+ achieves largest MSE winner coverage among family variants with clear gains in selected periodic regimes; component analysis supports usefulness of periodic-position context, signed correction, and adaptive trust.

Conclusion: FRWKV+ improves frequency-space forecasting via selective periodic-position branch interaction while revealing boundary cases where simpler correction rules remain preferable.

Abstract: Long-term time series forecasting is essential for decision making in energy, finance, transportation, and healthcare systems. Recent lightweight forecasting models improve efficiency by operating in transformed or linearized spaces, but two challenges remain in frequency-space forecasting. The real and imaginary streams of complex spectra contain complementary information that is often weakly exchanged, and periodic-position cues can help recurring patterns only when they are reliable for the current dataset and prediction horizon. To address these challenges, we propose FRWKV+, an enhanced FRWKV forecasting model for selective periodic-position branch interaction. FRWKV+ first introduces cross-branch gates that exchange compact contexts between the real and imaginary frequency streams, allowing each stream to modulate the other. It then uses the Adaptive PhaseGate mechanism to extract periodic-position context and generate signed corrections to these gates. An adaptive trust mechanism controls the correction strength at the sample, variable, and channel levels, so periodic-position information is admitted as a reliable correction signal while preserving the efficiency of the FRWKV backbone. External benchmark tables report a separately labeled FRWKV-family selected system for manuscript-level comparison, while mechanism-level claims are based on strict matched-seed FRWKV-family ablations and representative component-level ablations. Under this matched protocol, FRWKV+ achieves the largest MSE winner coverage among the family variants and provides clear gains in selected periodic regimes. Component analysis further supports the usefulness of periodic-position context, signed correction, and adaptive trust in these regimes, while revealing boundary cases where simpler correction rules remain preferable.

</details>


### [256] [SEED: Targeted Data Selection by Weighted Independent Set](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15691)
*Yuan Zhang,Lifeng Guo,Junwen Pan,Chang Liu,Wenzhao Zheng,Kuan Cheng,Kurt Keutzer,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces SEED, a data selection method using Weighted Independent Set (WIS) on similarity graphs with node value calibration and local scale normalization. It outperforms SOTA on instruction tuning, visual instruction tuning, and semantic segmentation. A compact multimodal dataset Honeybee-Remake-SEED-200K is also constructed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data selection aims to find a compact yet informative subset from large-scale training corpora by balancing sample quality and diversity. Existing naive approaches suffer from gradient noise in node weights and structurally imbalanced graphs due to heterogeneous domain distributions, which bias selection toward sparse regions.

Method: The problem is formulated as Weighted Independent Set (WIS) on a similarity graph. Two refinements are proposed: (1) Node value calibration that restricts influence estimation to the bilateral salient subspace to ground importance in task-relevant signals; (2) Local scale normalization that adapts edge thresholds to local neighborhood density to mitigate cross-domain distribution shift.

Result: SEED consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on instruction tuning, visual instruction tuning, and semantic segmentation across diverse model families. A compact multimodal dataset Honeybee-Remake-SEED-200K is curated.

Conclusion: The graph-based WIS formulation with principled refinements yields a robust and scalable data selection pipeline that effectively balances quality and diversity, validated across multiple tasks and model families.

Abstract: Data selection seeks to identify a compact yet informative subset from large-scale training corpora, balancing sample quality against collection diversity. We formulate this problem as a Weighted Independent Set (WIS) on a similarity graph, where nodes represent data samples weighted by influence, and edges connect semantically redundant pairs. This formulation naturally yields subsets that are simultaneously high-quality and diverse. However, two challenges arise in practice: naive node weights fail to distinguish informative signals from gradient noise, and edge construction under heterogeneous domain distributions produces structurally imbalanced graphs that bias selection toward sparse regions. To address these issues, we introduce two principled refinements from a unified graph perspective: (1) \textit{node value calibration} that restricts influence estimation to the bilateral salient subspace to ground node importance in task-relevant signals rather than surface-level statistics; (2) \textit{local scale normalization} that adapts edge thresholds to local neighborhood density, mitigating graph imbalance induced by cross-domain distribution shifts. Together, these components yield a robust and scalable data selection pipeline dubbed SEED. We further construct \texttt{Honeybee-Remake-SEED-200K}, a compact multimodal dataset curated by SEED. Extensive experiments show that SEED consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on instruction tuning, visual instruction tuning, and semantic segmentation across diverse model families.

</details>


### [257] [Tighter Regret Bounds for Contextual Action-Set Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15692)
*Zijun Chen,Zihan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies episodic RL with episode-dependent action sets (action contexts) and extends the MVP algorithm to achieve minimax regret bounds of Õ(√(SAH³K log L)) for adversarial contexts and Õ(√(SAH³K)) for stochastic contexts, plus a gap-dependent regret bound that improves when suboptimality gaps are large.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address reinforcement learning problems where admissible actions vary per episode (action contexts), bridging the gap between fixed-action-set RL and realistic settings with changing action availability.

Method: Extends the MVP (Monotonic Value Propagation) algorithm to handle episode-dependent admissible action sets, analyzing both adversarial and stochastic context settings.

Result: Achieves minimax regret Õ(√(SAH³K log L)) for adversarial contexts, Õ(√(SAH³K)) for stochastic contexts, and a sample complexity of Õ(SAH³/ε²). Also provides a gap-dependent regret bound using the global p-trimmed positive-gap floor.

Conclusion: The MVP algorithm naturally extends to action-context RL with strong theoretical guarantees; the gap-dependent bound can substantially improve upon the minimax rate when suboptimality gaps are large.

Abstract: We study episodic reinforcement learning with fixed reward and transition functions, but with episode-dependent admissible action sets that are observed at the start of each episode. Performance is measured by cumulative regret against the episode-wise optimal value, $\sum_{k=1}^K [V^{*,M^k} - V^{π^k,M^k}]$, where $M^k$ represents the action context in the $k$-th episode. We show that the MVP algorithm naturally extends to this framework and enjoys strong theoretical guarantees. In particular, we establish a minimax regret bound of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{SAH^3K\log L})$ for adversarial contexts, where $L$ denotes the number of possible contexts. This result implies a regret bound of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{SAH^3K})$ for stochastic contexts. We further translate the stochastic regret guarantee into a sample complexity bound of $\widetilde{O}(SAH^3/ε^2)$ for a fixed context distribution.
  In addition, we derive a gap-dependent regret bound of \[ \widetilde O\left( \inf_{p\in [0,1)} \left( \frac{1}{Δ_{\min}^{p}} + pKΔ_{\min}^{p} \right)\log K \cdot \mathrm{poly}(S,A,H) \right), \] where $Δ_{\min}^{p}$ is the global $p$-trimmed positive-gap floor over suboptimal $(h,s,a)$ triples. This bound can substantially improve upon the minimax rate when the relevant suboptimality gaps are large.

</details>


### [258] [Going Beyond the Edge: Distributed Inference of Transformer Models on Ultra-Low-Power Wireless Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15694)
*Alexander Gräfe,Ding Huo,Johannes Berger,Marco Zimmerling,Sebastian Trimpe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CATS is a communication-aware distributed inference framework that enables multiple ultra-low-power IoT devices to collaboratively run transformer models up to 14x larger than a single device can handle, using pruned communication (SomeGather) and message-dropout training for robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer models are too computationally and memory-intensive for individual ultra-low-power IoT devices, so a distributed approach is needed to enable their deployment on resource-constrained wireless devices.

Method: CATS co-designs transformer partitioning, wireless communication, and training. It uses SomeGather (a pruned communication primitive that selectively broadcasts activation columns) to reduce bandwidth and RAM usage, a partitioning method leveraging this primitive for model parallelism, and message-dropout during training to mimic packet losses for robustness.

Result: Real-world experiments show CATS enables distributed transformer inference on up to 16 ultra-low-power wireless devices, executing models up to 14x larger than what a single device can run.

Conclusion: CATS successfully brings distributed transformer inference to ultra-low-power wireless devices for the first time, demonstrating feasibility through communication-aware co-design and robustness mechanisms.

Abstract: Transformer models are rapidly becoming a cornerstone of modern Internet of Things (IoT) applications, yet their computational and memory demands far exceed the capabilities of a single typical ultra-low-power IoT device. We present CATS, a framework for distributed transformer inference on ultra-low-power wireless devices, enabling multiple devices to collaboratively execute models far larger than what a single device can sustain. At its core, CATS is a communication-aware distributed transformer inference scheme co-designed across transformer partitioning, wireless communication and training. It employs SomeGather, a new pruned communication primitive that selectively broadcasts activation columns to reduce communication bandwidth and RAM usage without sacrificing model accuracy. Building on SomeGather, we design a partitioning method that exploits this primitive for efficient model parallelism. To cope with unreliable wireless communication, CATS employs message-dropout during training, which mimics packet losses and yields models that are robust to message loss during inference. In real-world experiments, we show that CATS brings distributed transformer inference to ultra-low-power wireless devices for the first time, with deployments on up to 16 devices that collaboratively execute transformer models up to 14 times larger than what a single device can run.

</details>


### [259] [AGOP-IxG: A Gradient Covariance Filter for Local Feature Attribution on Tabular Data, with a Controlled Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15700)
*Raj Kiran Gupta Katakam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes AGOP-IxG, a fast per-sample attribution method for tabular classifiers that combines gradient outer product with rank truncation. Evaluated on synthetic benchmarks where ground-truth is known, AGOP-IxG outperforms SHAP, Integrated Gradients, InputXGradient, and LIME in terms of Spearman correlation and noise mass, while being 350-1650x faster than SHAP. On real-world global faithfulness tests, all methods perform similarly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature attribution methods are often chosen by convention rather than measured fidelity because rigorous evaluation is hindered by the lack of ground-truth attributions on real data.

Method: AGOP-IxG pre-multiplies the per-sample gradient by a top-K rank-truncated Average Gradient Outer Product matrix, providing fast per-sample attributions for tabular classifiers.

Result: AGOP-IxG leads on Spearman rank correlation and noise feature mass on all three synthetic datasets, and on top-k precision on the interaction dataset. It is approximately 350x to 1650x faster than SHAP. In global faithfulness tests, all methods cluster within ~1.7% relative AUC.

Conclusion: AGOP-IxG offers a compelling speed-accuracy tradeoff for per-sample local attribution, though it is optimized for local rather than global feature ranking tasks.

Abstract: Automated machine learning pipelines increasingly produce models whose predictions must be explained to end users, auditors, and downstream decision systems. The most widely used feature attribution methods (SHAP, Integrated Gradients, LIME) are typically chosen by convention rather than measured fidelity, because rigorous evaluation is impeded by the absence of ground-truth attribution on real data. We propose AGOP-IxG, a fast per-sample attribution method for tabular classifiers that pre-multiplies the per-sample gradient by a top-$K$ rank-truncated Average Gradient Outer Product matrix, and evaluate it against four widely-used baselines on a controlled tabular benchmark designed for AutoML practitioners. In Part 1, we construct three synthetic multi-class tabular tasks (linear, sparse nonlinear, interaction-based) where ground-truth attribution per sample is analytically or numerically derivable, and compare five methods: AGOP-IxG, SHAP (DeepExplainer), Integrated Gradients, InputXGradient, and LIME. AGOP-IxG leads on Spearman rank correlation and noise feature mass on all three synthetic datasets, and on top-$k$ precision on the interaction dataset. Across all settings, AGOP-IxG is approximately $350\times$ to $1{,}650\times$ faster than SHAP. In Part 2, we evaluate global faithfulness on Adult Income and Credit Card Default using the ROAR protocol; the methods cluster within $\sim 1.7\%$ relative AUC, consistent with AGOP-IxG being optimized for per-sample local attribution rather than global feature ranking.

</details>


### [260] [Differentiable Mixture-of-Agents Incentivizes Swarm Intelligence of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15706)
*Xingjian Wu,Junkai Lu,Siyu Yan,Xiangfei Qiu,Jilin Hu,Chenjuan Guo,Bin Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DMoA is a self-evolving multi-agent framework that uses differentiable, context-aware routing to dynamically activate agents at each reasoning step, achieving SOTA on 9 benchmarks with improved efficiency and adaptability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent systems rely on pre-defined or pre-compiled communication topologies, limiting flexibility and adaptability to dynamic task requirements.

Method: DMoA employs a differentiable, context-aware routing mechanism with recurrent structures for historical context, producing sparse agent activations step-wise; predictive entropy serves as self-supervised signals for test-time adaptation.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across 9 benchmarks with strong efficiency, robustness, and ensembling capabilities.

Conclusion: DMoA demonstrates that dynamic, self-evolving agent routing outperforms static topologies in multi-agent LLM systems.

Abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have catalyzed the development of multi-agent systems (MAS) for complex reasoning tasks. However, existing MAS typically rely on pre-defined or pre-compiled communication topologies, which limits their flexibility and adaptability to dynamic task requirements. In this work, we propose Differentiable Mixture-of-Agents (DMoA), a self-evolving multi-agent framework that enables elastic and adaptive agent collaboration during inference. Instead of statically constructing workflows, DMoA dynamically routes and activates agents at each reasoning step, allowing the system to implicitly simulate diverse communication topologies and adapt to evolving demands. To achieve this, we design a differentiable, context-aware routing mechanism that leverages recurrent structures to incorporate historical and contextual information, producing sparse agent activations in a step-wise manner. Furthermore, we introduce predictive entropy as self-supervised signals to optimize the routing process, enabling efficient test-time adaptation without external annotations. Extensive experiments across 9 benchmarks demonstrate that DMoA achieves state-of-the-art performance while exhibiting strong efficiency, robustness, and ensembling capabilities.

</details>


### [261] [GOMA: Toward Structure-Driven Multimodal Alignment from a Graph Signal Smoothing Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15723)
*Xu Wang,Xunkai Li,Yinlin Zhu,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GOMA is a structure-driven post-alignment framework that refines frozen multimodal embeddings using multimodal attributed graphs, achieving state-of-the-art retrieval by decoupling message flow, propagation, and smoothing depth control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CLIP-style dual encoders learn multimodal alignment from isolated image-text pairs, ignoring relational context among entities. Multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs) offer a natural setting but face challenges from modality-specific topological barriers, over-smoothing, and semantic boundary collapse.

Method: GOMA decouples three design choices: learning modality-aware propagation operators, performing finite-step coupled smoothing without diagonal cross-modal shortcuts, and adaptively reading out node-specific smoothing trajectories. It operates under a transductive MAG retrieval protocol with no self-pair edges.

Result: On seven MAG benchmarks, GOMA achieves state-of-the-art or tied state-of-the-art retrieval performance and is substantially more stable than the strongest graph competitor.

Conclusion: MAG structure can serve as an effective post-encoder for frozen multimodal embeddings, proving that leveraging graph context improves multimodal alignment without extra training of the base encoders.

Abstract: Multimodal alignment is commonly learned from isolated image-text pairs via CLIP-style dual encoders, leaving the relational context among entities largely unused. Multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs), where nodes carry multimodal attributes and edges encode corpus structure, provide a natural setting for refining frozen vision-language embeddings. This refinement is challenging: visual, textual, and cross-modal relations often induce different neighborhood geometries, while unrestricted graph propagation can quickly over-smooth retrieval representations. Effectively leveraging graph context therefore requires simultaneously breaking modality-specific topological barriers, controlling the smoothing regime, and preserving informative smoothing before semantic boundaries collapse. We propose Graph-Optimized Multimodal Alignment (GOMA), a structure-driven post-alignment framework that views frozen multimodal embeddings as graph signals and addresses these requirements through a unified retrieval-oriented design. GOMA decouples three key design choices: where messages should flow, how multimodal evidence should propagate, and which smoothing depth should be retained. Concretely, it learns modality-aware propagation operators, performs finite-step coupled smoothing without diagonal cross-modal shortcuts, and adaptively reads out node-specific smoothing trajectories to preserve useful smoothing before collapse. All experiments follow a transductive MAG retrieval protocol where the graph serves only as unlabeled context and diagonal self-pair edges are removed. On seven MAG benchmarks, GOMA achieves state-of-the-art or tied state-of-the-art retrieval and remains substantially more stable than the strongest graph competitor, demonstrating that MAG structure can serve as an effective post-encoder for frozen multimodal embeddings.

</details>


### [262] [A Unified Perturbation Framework for Analyzing Leaderboard Stability and Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15761)
*Hosna Oyarhoseini,Jimmy Lin,Amir-Hossein Karimi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that LLM leaderboards like LMArena are surprisingly non-robust: tiny (<1%) targeted perturbations to pairwise comparison data can change top-ranked models, degrade ranking consistency, and alter confidence intervals. The authors propose an influence-based perturbation framework that also enables efficient targeted promotion/demotion of specific models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluation leaderboards (e.g., LMArena) are central to benchmarking LLMs but their robustness to small data perturbations is poorly understood, raising concerns about the reliability of model rankings.

Method: A unified influence-based perturbation framework for Bradley-Terry leaderboards analyzing three match-level perturbations (Drop, Add, Flip) and player removal, evaluated via top-k membership, Kendall's tau, and confidence-interval-based uncertainty.

Result: Modern leaderboards are non-robust: sub-1% targeted perturbations can change the top-ranked model, degrade Kendall's tau, and alter confidence intervals. The influence scores also enable efficient targeted manipulation and uncertainty reduction.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a practical tool for auditing leaderboard stability and motivates more robust evaluation protocols; normalized dataset-level robustness scores summarize overall effects.

Abstract: Evaluation leaderboards such as LMArena play a central role in benchmarking large language models by aggregating pairwise human preferences into model rankings, yet the robustness of these rankings remains poorly understood. We present a unified perturbation framework for analyzing Bradley-Terry leaderboards under structured data modifications using influence-based approximations. Our framework studies three match-level perturbations -- Drop, Add, and Flip -- together with player removal, and evaluates their effects on top-k membership, global ranking consistency via Kendall's tau, and confidence-interval-based uncertainty. Across Chatbot Arena and six additional pairwise-comparison datasets, we show that modern leaderboards are non-robust across all three objectives: sub-1% targeted perturbations can change the top-ranked model, degrade Kendall's tau, and alter confidence intervals. Beyond robustness auditing, we show that the same influence scores enable efficient targeted perturbations, promoting or demoting specific models and reducing target-model uncertainty with fewer actions than previous manipulation and active-sampling baselines. By summarizing these effects with normalized dataset-level robustness scores, our framework provides a practical and helpful tool for auditing leaderboard stability and motivating more robust evaluation protocols.

</details>


### [263] [Continual Learning of Domain-Invariant Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15775)
*Pascal Janetzky,Tobias Schlagenhauf,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes continual learning methods that learn domain-invariant representations to improve generalization to unseen domains, outperforming existing CL baselines across vision, medicine, manufacturing, and ecology datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CL methods optimize for in-domain performance and suffer from shortcut learning (overfitting to domain-specific spurious cues), which limits generalization to unseen target domains after deployment.

Method: The authors propose combining replay-based training with sequential invariance alignment to learn and preserve invariant structures (which capture causal mechanisms) across domains over time.

Result: Across six datasets spanning vision, medicine, manufacturing, and ecology, the proposed methods consistently outperform existing CL baselines in generalization to unseen target domains. Naive extensions of sequential training with existing DIRL methods provide only limited benefits.

Conclusion: This is the first work to develop domain-invariant representation methods for CL, showing they reduce overfitting to domain-specific cues and improve out-of-domain generalization.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) aims to train models sequentially over multiple domains without forgetting previously learned knowledge. However, existing CL methods optimize for in-domain performance and are therefore prone to learning spurious, domain-specific cues (``shortcut learning''), which limits generalization to unseen domains after deployment. In this paper, we address this limitation through continual learning of domain-invariant representation. We introduce a broad class of CL methods that sequentially learn representations capturing invariant structures across domains. Our methods are motivated by the observation that such invariant structures often preserve the underlying causal mechanisms, which can reduce the risk of overfitting to domain-specific cues and thus offer better out-of-domain generalization. Our proposed CL methods combine replay-based training with a tailored sequential invariance alignment to learn -- and preserve -- invariant structures over time. We evaluate our methods under a deployment-oriented protocol that measures performance on unseen target domains. Across six benchmark and real-world datasets spanning vision, medicine, manufacturing, and ecology, our methods consistently outperform existing CL baselines in terms of generalization to unseen target domains. As an ablation, we further show that naïve extensions of sequential training with existing domain-invariant representation learning (DIRL) methods provide only limited benefits. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to develop domain-invariant representation methods for CL.

</details>


### [264] [Grokking as Structural Inference: Transformers Need Bayesian Lottery Tickets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15787)
*Kai Hidajat,Solden Stoll,Joseph An*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explains delayed generalization (grokking) in Transformers by decoupling it into two conditions: MLP capacity (Goldilocks bound) and a novel Bayesian structural condition requiring attention to place sufficient mass on every informative token. The delay arises because memorization starves attention of structural gradients, requiring weight decay to erode memorization before generalization occurs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing accounts of delayed generalization in Transformers (norm minimization, feature emergence, sparse subnetworks) ignore a constraint unique to attention-based models: if attention discards an informative token, no bounded downstream computation can recover it. The authors aim to formalize this constraint and explain why Transformers wait thousands of steps before generalizing.

Method: The authors formalize attention as an implicit Bayesian posterior over the task dependency graph. They prove generalization requires two separable conditions: (1) a Goldilocks bound on MLP capacity (norm-based theory), and (2) a Bayesian structural condition requiring attention to place sufficient mass on every informative token. They derive the grokking delay as a structural waiting time and propose a KL-based structural intervention to bypass it.

Result: The decoupling explains delayed generalization as delayed structural inference: early memorization starves attention of structural gradient, requiring weight decay to erode memorization. The KL-based intervention yields an inverse-intervention-strength scaling law for grokking time, and experiments on algorithmic sequence tasks show this Bayesian ticket matches or outperforms lottery-ticket transfer.

Conclusion: Delayed generalization in Transformers is primarily a structural inference delay caused by attention's inability to recover discarded informative tokens. This can be bypassed through KL-based structural interventions, offering a new understanding and potential acceleration of grokking in attention-based models.
