TL;DR: This paper proposes an entropic inference-time scheduler for flow-based generative models under small budgets, derived from a conditional-marginal entropy-rate objective, which improves sample quality by allocating function evaluations near boundaries (U-shaped schedule) and shows gains across 2D bridges, CIFAR-10, and protein generation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under a small inference budget, sample quality depends heavily on where the sampler spends its few function evaluations, yet existing flow matching and Schrödinger bridge methods use heuristic or inherited inference grids. The authors aim to derive a principled, training-free scheduler from first principles.

Method: Derives a conditional-marginal entropy-rate objective that separates endpoint-conditioned bridge geometry from marginal flow evolution. For Gaussian Brownian bridges, the rate is closed-form and U-shaped, motivating boundary-heavy nonuniform grids. The scheduler is applied as a training-free entropic time-discretization.

Result: On 2D models, estimated profiles recover the predicted shape and improve 10-step ODE-Heun MMD by 18.1% and SDE-Heun by 22.7% over linear scheduling. On EDM/CIFAR-10, entropic discretization achieves best five-step FID (186.3 vs 200.5 linear, 238.0 cosine). On AlphaFlow protein generation, it shows advantage in low-NFE regimes on CAMEO22 and ATLAS.

Conclusion: Entropy-rate scheduling is a practical, low-budget allocation signal for high-dimensional bridge and flow samplers, consistently outperforming heuristic schedules across multiple domains and metrics.

Abstract: For a fixed flow-based generative model under a small inference budget, sample quality can depend strongly on where the sampler spends its few function evaluations. Flow matching and Schrödinger bridges define probability paths, yet their inference grids are usually heuristic or inherited from one-endpoint diffusion. We derive a conditional-marginal entropy-rate objective for bridge-aware discretization, separating endpoint-conditioned bridge geometry from marginal flow evolution, and use it to build a training-free entropic inference-time scheduler from first principles. For Gaussian Brownian bridges this rate is closed-form and U-shaped, motivating boundary-heavy nonuniform grids. On trained two-dimensional bridge/flow models, the estimated profile recovers the predicted shape and improves 10-step ODE-Heun MMD over linear by 18.1%, with a paired 22.7% SDE-Heun improvement in the same low-NFE sweep. On EDM/CIFAR-10, the entropic time-discretization gives the best tested five-step FID (186.3 \pm 4.0 versus 200.5 \pm 2.9 for linear and 238.0 \pm 5.3 for cosine). On AlphaFlow protein generation, entropic conditional-marginal (cond-marg) scheduling shows advantage in low-NFE regimes on both CAMEO22 and ATLAS benchmarks. These results support entropy-rate scheduling as a practical low-budget allocation signal for high-dimensional bridge and flow samplers.

</details>


### [293] [Navigating Potholes with Geometry-Aware Sharpness Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16134)
*Simon Dufort-Labbé,Mehrab Hamidi,Razvan Pascanu,Ioannis Mitliagkas,Damien Scieur,Aristide Baratin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLQR+SAM combines sharpness-aware minimization with a learned preconditioner from LLQR, using a two-timescale approach where the preconditioner captures smoothed loss geometry while SAM probes local curvature, leading to improved generalization across vision and sequence tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SAM treats all parameter directions uniformly, ignoring the underlying loss geometry, which limits its effectiveness in finding flat minima.

Method: LLQR+SAM integrates SAM with a learned preconditioner from LLQR (layerwise linear-quadratic regulator). The preconditioner is updated sparsely via slow exponential moving average to capture smoothed loss geometry, while SAM performs faster-timescale perturbation on top of this learned geometry.

Result: Consistent improvements over both SAM and LLQR alone across vision and sequence modeling benchmarks, demonstrating that slow learned geometry and fast sharpness correction are complementary.

Conclusion: The two-timescale structure is not just computationally convenient; the preconditioner amplifies SAM's escape signal in directions that are flat under average geometry but locally sharp (potholes), while wide flat basins remain stable.

Abstract: Sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) encourages flat minima by perturbing parameters along directions of high loss curvature, but treats all parameter directions uniformly, ignoring the underlying loss geometry. We introduce LLQR+SAM, which combines SAM with a learned preconditioner obtained from the recently proposed LLQR framework, a second-order method that recasts steepest descent as a layerwise linear-quadratic regulator problem. The preconditioner is updated sparsely and maintained as a slow exponential moving average, so it captures a smoothed, low-resolution picture of the loss landscape geometry. The SAM perturbation then operates on top of this learned geometry, probing curvature at a faster timescale. We show that this two-timescale structure is not merely a computational convenience: theoretically, the preconditioner amplifies the SAM escape signal in directions that are flat under the average geometry but locally sharp (potholes). Wide, flat basins, by contrast, remain stable. Empirically, LLQR+SAM gives consistent gains over both SAM and LLQR alone across standard vision and sequence modeling benchmarks, supporting the view that slow learned geometry and fast sharpness correction are genuinely complementary.

</details>


### [294] [Surrogate Neural Architecture Codesign Package (SNAC-Pack)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16138)
*Jason Weitz,Dmitri Demler,Benjamin Hawks,Aaron Wang,Nhan Tran,Javier Duarte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SNAC-Pack is an open-source AutoML framework for hardware-aware neural architecture codesign and FPGA deployment, using multi-objective search, surrogate modeling, and compression to automatically discover compact architectures that match or exceed baselines while reducing FPGA resource utilization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NAS methods optimize for accuracy alone or use proxy metrics like BOPs that correlate poorly with hardware cost, especially for FPGA deployment where cost involves multi-dimensional budgets (LUTs, DSPs, flip-flops, BRAM, latency).

Method: Multi-objective global search (Optuna + NSGA-II) with shared SQLite store for parallel workers; hardware surrogate model to estimate resource/latency without synthesis; local search with QAT + iterative magnitude pruning; YAML config + agentic frontend; FPGA synthesis via hls4ml.

Result: Demo on jet classification (LHC) and superconducting qubit readout; discovered compact architectures matching/exceeding baselines on task metrics while reducing FPGA resource utilization; qubit readout design space reduced from months to hours.

Conclusion: SNAC-Pack enables efficient, automated, hardware-aware neural architecture codesign for FPGA deployment, significantly reducing manual effort and design time while maintaining or improving task performance.

Abstract: Neural architecture search (NAS) is a powerful approach for automating model design, but existing methods often optimize for accuracy alone or rely on proxy metrics such as bit operations (BOPs) that correlate poorly with hardware cost. This gap is particularly large for FPGA deployment, where cost is dominated by a multi-dimensional budget of lookup tables, DSPs, flip-flops, BRAM, and latency. We present the Surrogate Neural Architecture Codesign Package (SNAC-Pack), an open-source AutoML framework for hardware-aware neural architecture codesign and end-to-end FPGA deployment. SNAC-Pack runs a multi-objective global search with Optuna and NSGA-II, loading trials to a shared SQLite store that enables parallel workers across compute nodes. A hardware surrogate model outputs per-trial resource and latency estimates, avoiding the synthesis cost that would otherwise dominate the search loop. A local search stage then applies quantization-aware training (QAT) together with iterative magnitude pruning in a combined compression loop, after which the final model is synthesized to FPGA firmware via the hls4ml Python library. A YAML configuration and an optional agentic frontend let users run the pipeline on new datasets without modifying the framework. We demonstrate SNAC-Pack on jet classification at the Large Hadron Collider and superconducting qubit readout, discovering compact architectures that match or exceed strong baselines on the task metric while reducing FPGA resource utilization and, in the qubit readout case, reducing the design space exploration process from months of manual fine-tuning to hours of automated search.

</details>


### [295] [Entropic Auto-Encoding via Implicit Free-Energy Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16164)
*Hazhir Aliahmadi,Irina Babayan,Greg van Anders*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EAEs replace explicit priors with an entropy-driven ensemble of encoders, mitigating posterior collapse and learning non-Gaussian multimodal latent representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VAEs suffer from posterior collapse where latent variables are ignored due to explicit prior imposition driving optimization toward uninformative representations.

Method: EAEs use reconstruction loss as the only explicit objective, with entropy generating latent priors implicitly through a free energy-minimizing ensemble of encoders, biasing toward high-volume near-optimal regions.

Result: EAEs mitigate posterior collapse, learn multimodal non-Gaussian distributions, capture reaction-diffusion dynamics, identify implicit categorical distinctions in MNIST, and learn hierarchical facial structure on CelebA.

Conclusion: EAEs effectively address posterior collapse by learning informative, diverse latent representations that preserve underlying data structure across multiple datasets.

Abstract: Despite their ubiquity, variational autoencoders (VAEs) inherently suffer from posterior collapse, a failure mode in which latent variables are effectively ignored. This failure arises because explicit prior imposition drives optimization toward loss landscape regions corresponding to uninformative latent representations. Here, we introduce Entropic Autoencoders (EAEs), a framework in which reconstruction loss is the only explicit objective, and entropy generates the latent variables' prior implicitly through a free energy-minimizing ensemble of encoders. This ensemble biases learning toward high-volume regions of near-optimal solutions, while decoder updates direct the search trajectories toward informative latent representations. We demonstrate that EAEs mitigate posterior collapse by learning non-Gaussian, multimodal latent distributions that yield diverse, data-consistent generations and preserve different forms of underlying structure in the data. As a proof-of-concept, we show that an EAE captures a superposition of the known low-dimensional dynamics of a reaction-diffusion process. Then, we show that an EAE identifies implicit categorical distinctions in MNIST latent representations, and displays a hierarchical understanding of facial structure on the CelebA dataset, from an "all-human" face to individual-dependent features.

</details>


### [296] [BAPR: Bayesian amnesic piecewise-robust reinforcement learning for non-stationary continuous control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16170)
*Yifan Zhang,Liang Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BAPR unifies Bayesian Online Change Detection with robust ensemble RL to handle piecewise stationary control systems, providing formal verification in Lean 4 of a sharp boundary condition for contraction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard robust RL methods face a dilemma between conservative (inefficient during stable periods) and adaptive (risks catastrophic failure during undetected regime changes) policies for piecewise stationary control systems.

Method: BAPR combines BOCD with robust ensemble RL; uses a convex combination of mode-conditional Bellman operators weighted by a frozen belief distribution; employs context-conditioning module with RMDM loss; includes formal verification in Lean 4.

Result: The BAPR operator is a γ-contraction; sharp boundary established: contraction factor becomes γ+λΔ when beliefs depend on Q-function, failing when γ+λΔ≥1; component-wise formal error budget for post-switch recovery; detection delay O(log(1/δ)); 22 machine-verified theorems across 1,145 Lean 4 lines.

Conclusion: BAPR provides a formally verified framework for piecewise stationary control with adaptive conservatism that detects change-points and relaxes as confidence grows, with no mode labels needed at deployment.

Abstract: Real-world control systems frequently operate under \emph{piecewise stationary} conditions, where dynamics remain stable for extended periods before undergoing abrupt regime changes. Standard robust RL methods face a fundamental dilemma: a globally conservative policy wastes performance during stable periods, while a locally adaptive policy risks catastrophic failure when the regime changes undetected.
  We propose \textbf{BAPR} (Bayesian Amnesic Piecewise-Robust SAC), which unifies Bayesian Online Change Detection (BOCD) with robust ensemble RL. The BAPR operator -- a convex combination of mode-conditional Bellman operators weighted by a frozen belief distribution -- is a $γ$-contraction. A complementary counterexample, machine-verified in Lean~4, establishes a \emph{sharp boundary}: when beliefs depend on the Q-function, the contraction factor becomes $γ+ λΔ$ (where $Δ$ is the mode reward gap), and contraction fails exactly when $γ+ λΔ\geq 1$. We derive a \emph{component-wise} formal error budget for the abstract operator -- every component machine-verified -- bounding post-switch recovery; the budget applies to the abstract mode-mixture operator and inherits to the implemented shared-critic algorithm only through the frozen-parameter design intuition. All results are formally verified with no \texttt{sorry} (1,145 lines across 3 Lean~4 files, 22 machine-verified theorems). BOCD drives an adaptive conservatism mechanism: the policy becomes maximally conservative after detected change-points and smoothly relaxes as confidence grows, with detection delay $O(\log(1/δ))$. A context-conditioning module trained via RMDM loss provides mode-aware representations from simulator-provided mode IDs at training time and requires no mode labels at deployment.

</details>


### [297] [Imitation learning for clinical decision support in pediatric ECMO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16175)
*Fateme Golivand,Michael Skinner,Saurabh Mathur,Ameet Soni,Phillip Reeder,Kristian Kersting,Lakshmi Raman,Sriraam Natarajan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper applies TabPFN, a transformer-based model for tabular data, to imitate clinician decision-making in pediatric ECMO care, where actions are not directly observed. TabPFN outperforms XGBoost and MLP baselines, providing a strong behavioral baseline for decision support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pediatric critical care, especially ECMO, involves high complexity and data scarcity. Modeling clinical interventions from observational data is crucial for decision support, but actions are not directly observed, making imitation learning challenging.

Method: Frames clinical decision-making as imitation learning from trajectories with unobserved actions. Evaluates TabPFN (transformer for tabular data) against XGBoost and MLPs on real-world pediatric ECMO data to learn action models.

Result: TabPFN consistently outperforms XGBoost and MLP baselines in learning action models from the observational pediatric ECMO data.

Conclusion: TabPFN serves as a strong clinician-behavior baseline for pediatric ECMO decision support, suggesting transformer-based models are promising for this high-stakes, data-scarce setting.

Abstract: Pediatric critical care is a dynamic, high-stakes process involving constant monitoring and adjustments in life-saving treatments. Modeling these interventions is crucial for effective decision support. To address the challenges of high complexity and data scarcity in pediatric Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (ECMO), we frame clinical decision-making as learning to act from trajectories, i.e., imitation learning that learns action models from observational data, with a key feature that actions are not directly observed. We consider TabPFN, a recent transformer-based approach for tabular data, and traditional baselines including XGBoost and Multi-Layer Perceptrons(MLPs) on real-world pediatric ECMO data to learn the action models. We find that the TabPFN-based approach consistently outperforms these classical baselines, supporting its use as a strong clinician-behavior baseline for pediatric ECMO decision support.

</details>


### [298] [Hypothesis-driven construction of mesoscopic dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16211)
*Zhuoyuan Li,Aiqing Zhu,Qianxiao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a new paradigm for learning mesoscopic dynamics using a mathematically constrained hypothesis class based on a generalized Onsager principle, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional scientific modeling faces challenges in complex applications like multiscale systems due to fixed instance-wise equations and equation-specific analysis; a more flexible and unified paradigm is needed.

Method: Builds upon a generalized Onsager principle to create a unified framework covering both dissipative and conservative mesoscopic dynamics, with theoretical guarantees established before learning, then uses instance-specific data to identify members of the hypothesis class.

Result: Achieves accurate, robust, and interpretable dynamical models, validated on continuum PDE data and microscopic chain models where exact meso-scale models are unknown.

Conclusion: The framework serves as both an effective dynamics learner and provides interpretable diagnostics of underlying physics.

Abstract: Traditional scientific modeling typically begins with fixed, instance-wise effective equations and then carries out equation-specific analysis and computation, a procedure that becomes exceptionally challenging in complex applications such as multiscale systems. We propose an alternative paradigm by learning mesoscopic dynamics within a mathematically constrained hypothesis class. Building upon a generalized Onsager principle, we introduce a unified framework encompassing both dissipative and conservative mesoscopic dynamics. We establish uniform and a priori theoretical guarantees, including global well-posedness, asymptotic stability, unique factorization identifiability, and discrete energy dissipation, applicable to all spatio-temporal evolution equations within this hypothesis class prior to all learning stages. Data from each problem instance is then used to guide the identification of members within our hypothesis class, giving rise to accurate, robust and interpretable dynamical models. We empirically validate this framework on both data from continuum PDE models as a check, and on data arising from microscopic chain models for which exact meso-scale models are unknown. The proposed approach not only acts as an effective dynamics learner, but also offers vital interpretable diagnostics of the underlying physics.

</details>


### [299] [The Privacy Price of Tail-Risk Learning: Effective Tail Sample Size in Differentially Private CVaR Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16219)
*El Mustapha Mansouri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes how differential privacy affects CVaR learning, showing that the effective sample size is reduced to nτ (tail records), and provides complete rate characterizations for private CVaR excess risk under pure and approximate DP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how differential privacy interacts with tail-risk (CVaR) learning, specifically how privacy constraints alter the statistical complexity and effective sample size for estimating conditional value-at-risk.

Method: The paper derives minimax rates for private CVaR learning under pure and approximate DP, covering scalar estimation, finite classes, and convex Lipschitz learning, using modular upper and lower bound reductions.

Result: Key findings include: (1) the effective private tail sample size is εnτ; (2) scalar estimation rate Θ(B min{1, (nτ)^{-1/2} + (εnτ)^{-1}}); (3) finite classes rate Θ(B min{1, √(log(2M)/(nτ)) + log(2M)/(εnτ)}); (4) convex Lipschitz case has a 1/(εnτ) privacy term.

Conclusion: Private CVaR learning reduces to ordinary private learning on Θ(nτ) informative tail records, establishing this as the canonical hard subproblem.

Abstract: Differential privacy changes the effective sample size governing CVaR learning. For tail mass $τ$, the privacy-relevant sample size is not $n$, but $nτ$; equivalently, the effective private tail sample size is $εnτ$. Private CVaR excess risk decomposes into ordinary tail-risk statistical error and a privacy price. This decomposition is complete for scalar estimation and finite classes: scalar estimation has rate $Θ(B \min\{1,(nτ)^{-1/2}+(εnτ)^{-1}\})$, and finite classes of size $M$ have rate $Θ(B \min\{1,\sqrt{\log(2M)/(nτ)}+\log(2M)/(εnτ)\})$. These complete rates hold under pure DP, and their lower bounds extend to approximate DP in the stated small-$δ$ regimes. For convex Lipschitz learning, modular upper and lower reductions show that the CVaR-specific privacy term necessarily scales as $1/(εnτ)$, with dimension dependence inherited from private stochastic convex optimization. Together, these results identify ordinary private learning on $Θ(nτ)$ informative tail records as the canonical hard subproblem inside private CVaR learning.

</details>


### [300] [Layer Equivalence Is Not a Property of Layers Alone: How You Test Redundancy Changes What You Find](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16234)
*Gabriel Garcia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that two common tests for transformer layer "equivalence" (replacement and interchange) can give very different results, affecting which layers appear safe to prune. The gap grows during training and varies across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Researchers conflate replacement and interchange tests when assessing layer equivalence for compression, but these tests can disagree significantly, leading to inconsistent pruning decisions.

Method: The authors measure both replacement (substituting one layer's map for another in place) and interchange (swapping layer positions to test commutativity) protocols across Pythia 410M/1.4B and 8B-scale models, evaluating the gap between these swap-KL probes.

Result: The replacement-interchange gap grows from initialization to convergence. In Qwen3-8B, interchange-guided removal is much safer than replacement-guided; Llama-3.1-8B shows tied pruning cost despite lower interchange KL, indicating metric gaps do not map one-to-one to removal effects.

Conclusion: Before layer removal or merging, practitioners should score both swap-KLs on the target checkpoint; the diagnostic only requires unlabeled forward passes.

Abstract: When researchers ask whether two transformer layers are "equivalent" for compression, they often conflate distinct tests. Replacement asks whether one layer's map can substitute for another's in place; interchange asks whether two layers approximately commute when their positions are swapped. Both are output-grounded swap-KL probes, but they need not agree: on pretrained transformers the protocol gap can change which layers look safe to prune by several-fold under the same evaluator, especially when replacement distances are high.
  We measure both protocols across checkpoints and architectures. On a Pythia training trajectory (410M and 1.4B), the replacement-interchange gap grows from initialization to convergence. Under one matched WikiText-2 contract at 8B scale, Qwen3-8B enters a divergent regime: interchange-guided removal is several-fold safer than replacement-guided at the same layer budgets, while Llama-3.1-8B ties the two protocols for pruning cost even though interchange KL is lower, showing metric gaps need not map one-to-one to removal. Before layer removal or merging, score both swap-KLs on the target checkpoint; the diagnostic requires only unlabeled forward passes.

</details>


### [301] [Dynamics-Level Watermarking of Flow Matching Models with Random Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.16239)
*Shuchan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a dynamics-level watermarking approach for generative models, embedding watermarks into the velocity field of flow matching models. It uses random coding over a continuous channel with key-dependent perturbations during training, allowing message recovery from black-box queries while preserving generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a watermarking method for generative models that embeds signals directly into the learned continuous dynamics (velocity field) rather than into model weights or outputs, enabling reliable message recovery without compromising generation quality.

Method: The watermark is embedded as random coding over a continuous channel: a key-dependent perturbation is added during training of the flow matching model's velocity field. At detection time, the message is recovered from black-box queries. The perturbation is designed to leave the generated distribution unchanged.

Result: Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 across different architectures confirm reliable message recovery, preserved generation quality, and chance-level decoding accuracy without the secret key.

Conclusion: Dynamics-level watermarking is a viable approach for generative models, offering robust message recovery while maintaining generation quality and security against unauthorized detection.

Abstract: We introduce a dynamics-level approach to watermarking generative models. Rather than embedding signals into model weights or outputs, we embed the watermark directly into the learned continuous dynamics -- the velocity field of a flow matching model. We formulate this as random coding over a continuous channel: a key-dependent perturbation is added during training, and the message is recovered at detection time from black-box queries. The perturbation is designed to leave the generated distribution unchanged. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 across different architectures confirm reliable message recovery, preserved generation quality, and chance-level decoding accuracy without the secret key.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [302] [PhysBrain 1.0 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15298)
*Shijie Lian,Bin Yu,Xiaopeng Lin,Changti Wu,Hang Yuan,Xiaolin Hu,Zhaolong Shen,Yuzhuo Miao,Haishan Liu,Yuxuan Tian,Yukun Shi,Cong Huang,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PhysBrain 1.0 converts large-scale human egocentric video into structured physical commonsense supervision to train VLMs, then transfers learned priors to robot policies, achieving SOTA results across QA and embodied benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot trajectories alone provide limited coverage for learning broad physical understanding; human egocentric video offers a complementary, scalable source of physical commonsense.

Method: A data engine extracts scene elements, spatial dynamics, action execution, and depth-aware relations from human video, converting them into question-answer supervision for training PhysBrain VLMs. Physical priors are then transferred to VLA policies via a capability-preserving, language-sensitive adaptation design.

Result: Achieves SOTA on ERQA, PhysBench, SimplerEnv-WidowX, LIBERO, and RoboCasa, with especially strong out-of-domain performance on SimplerEnv.

Conclusion: Scaling physical commonsense from human interaction video provides an effective bridge from multimodal understanding to robot action.

Abstract: Vision-language-action models have advanced rapidly, but robot trajectories alone provide limited coverage for learning broad physical understanding. PhysBrain 1.0 studies a complementary route: converting large-scale human egocentric video into structured physical commonsense supervision before robot adaptation. Our data engine extracts scene elements, spatial dynamics, action execution, and depth-aware relations, then turns them into question-answer supervision for training PhysBrain VLMs. The resulting physical priors are further transferred to VLA policies through a capability-preserving and language-sensitive adaptation design. Across multimodal QA benchmarks and embodied control benchmarks, including ERQA, PhysBench, SimplerEnv-WidowX, LIBERO, and RoboCasa, PhysBrain 1.0 achieves SOTA results and shows especially strong out-of-domain performance on SimplerEnv. These results suggest that scaling physical commonsense from human interaction video can provide an effective bridge from multimodal understanding to robot action.

</details>


### [303] [Where to Perch in a Tree: Vision-Guidance for Tree-Grasping Drones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15430)
*Alex Dunnett,Leonie Bottomley,Mirko Kovac,Basaran Bahadir Kocer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a vision-guided method for autonomous drones to identify optimal tree-perching locations by evaluating branch width, slope, and curvature, achieving 76% success on urban tree images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable vision-guided autonomous tree-perching drones to locate ideal perch positions by assessing branch suitability beyond simply finding the closest branch.

Method: Uses image processing, machine learning, segmentation, and binary morphology to evaluate branch width, slope, and curvature for perch suitability.

Result: Achieved 76% success on feasible targets from a dataset of 10,000+ urban tree images collected February-October in subtropical/temperate monsoon climates.

Conclusion: The successful preliminary results provide a foundation for further development, including integration of depth and attitude sensors for improved generalization.

Abstract: This study demonstrates a method to locate an ideal perch location on a tree for vision-guided autonomous tree-perching drones. Various image processing algorithms, including those used for machine learning, image segmentation and binary image morphology, are implemented to assess the shape and structure of a tree. Rather than identifying the closest available branch, this study builds on vision methods by evaluating the potential of each branch, determining its suitability for perching based on factors such as branch width, slope (angle to the horizontal) and curvature. For a given tree-perching drone and a dataset of more than 10,000 urban tree images taken from February to October in a subtropical and temperate monsoon climate, the proposed method successfully produces a result for 76% of feasible targets. A feasible target defined as a tree where the branch diameters are sufficiently thick and where the available perching space is at least equal to the width of a tendon-driven grasping claw. These successful preliminary results create a foundation from which a number of identified improvements and additional features can be developed to create a generalised method; this will involve the incorporation of supplementary data from depth perception and attitude sensors to enhance the branch assessment.

</details>


### [304] [Residual Reinforcement Learning for Robot Teleoperation under Stochastic Delays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15480)
*Kaize Deng,Zewen Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid delay-resilient RL framework combining LSTM state estimator with residual RL policy for stable teleoperation under stochastic communication delays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stochastic communication delays in teleoperation cause signal discontinuities, control instability, and performance degradation. Conventional RL methods fail due to delay-induced high-frequency chattering.

Method: Proposes a hybrid control framework integrating (1) an LSTM-based state estimator to reconstruct smooth state estimates from delayed observations, and (2) a residual RL policy that learns torque compensation balancing tracking accuracy and velocity smoothness.

Result: Experimental validation on Franka Panda robots shows the approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving robust and stable teleoperation under high-variance stochastic delays.

Conclusion: The proposed delay-resilient RL framework effectively mitigates stochastic communication delays, enabling stable teleoperation control.

Abstract: Stochastic communication delays in teleoperation introduce signal discontinuities that undermine control stability and degrade control performance. Consequently, the conventional reinforcement learning (RL) methods struggle with the delayed observations due to the delay-induced observations, leading to high-frequency chattering. To address this, we propose a hybrid control framework, delay-resilient RL, integrating a state estimator utilizing Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) with a residual RL policy, which is resilient to stochastic delays. The LSTM reconstructs smooth, continuous state estimates from delayed observations, enabling the RL agent to learn a residual torque compensation policy that balances tracking accuracy with velocity smoothness. Experimental validation on Franka Panda robots demonstrates that our approach significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines, ensuring robust and stable teleoperation even under high-variance stochastic delays.

</details>


### [305] [Hybrid LLM-based Intelligent Framework for Robot Task Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15486)
*Swayamjit Saha,Subhabrata Das,Haonan Duan,Xiao-Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces LLM-based frameworks (using GPT-4 as generator and various models as supervisor) for construction robot task scheduling, optimizing time and resource allocation via NLP interfaces and real-time adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve task scheduling for construction robots by leveraging LLMs to handle dynamic site conditions and complex resource allocation, addressing limitations of traditional scheduling methods.

Method: Dual LLM agent architecture: generator (GPT-4) creates schedules, supervisor (Gemma 3/Llama 4/Mistral 7b) validates them. NLP interface for human communication, real-time adaptation to site changes.

Result: Metric scores from a straightforward scenario demonstrate the efficacy of the LLM-based scheduling frameworks.

Conclusion: LLM implementation is crucial for improving construction operational tasks involving robots, particularly for scheduling and real-time adaptation.

Abstract: This study introduces intelligent frameworks that use Large Language Models (LLMs) to improve task scheduling for construction robots. The LLM is fed with key data about the desired task, such as agent action abilities, and the desired end goal to be achieved. A well-balanced allocation strategy is developed, optimizing both time efficiency and resource utilization. Our system utilizes a Natural Language Processing interface to streamline communication with construction professionals and adapt in real-time to unexpected site conditions. We concurrently use two LLM agents, specifically generator (GPT-4) and supervisor (Gemma 3/Llama 4/Mistral 7b) LLM agents to provide a more precise task schedule. We evaluate the proposed methodology using a straightforward scenario and provide metric scores to prove the efficacy of the frameworks. Our results highlight that the implementation of LLMs is crucial in construction operational tasks including robots.

</details>


### [306] [FLASH: Efficient Visuomotor Policy via Sparse Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15492)
*Jiaqi Bai,Jindou Jia,Yuxuan Hu,Gen Li,Xiangyu Chen,Tuo An,Kuangji Zuo,Jianfei Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FLASH Policy replaces discrete action-chunk generation with continuous Legendre polynomial trajectory representation, enabling single-step flow matching inference for real-time robotic control by initiating from history polynomial coefficients instead of Gaussian noise. It achieves state-of-the-art success rates (≥92%), 31.40ms inference time (up to 175× faster than diffusion policies), and 5-7× reduction in controller tracking error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models (diffusion, flow matching) for visuomotor policy learning suffer from high inference latency due to iterative denoising, making them incompatible with real-time robotic control requirements.

Method: 1) Replaces discrete action-chunks with continuous Legendre polynomial trajectory representation. 2) Fits expert demonstrations under sparse temporal sampling to extend action horizon. 3) Initiates flow matching from history polynomial coefficients (not Gaussian noise) to shorten transport distance and enable single-step inference. 4) Uses analytic polynomial differentiation for velocity feed-forward signals.

Result: Achieves ≥92% success rates across all tasks, 31.40ms per-episode inference (175× faster than diffusion, 18× faster than flow matching), 4× faster training convergence than ACT, and 5-7× reduction in controller tracking error.

Conclusion: FLASH Policy demonstrates that continuous polynomial trajectory representation with history-anchored flow matching significantly accelerates inference while improving success rates and tracking accuracy, making generative policies viable for real-time robotic control.

Abstract: Generative models such as diffusion and flow matching have become dominant paradigms for visuomotor policy learning, yet their reliance on iterative denoising incurs high inference latency incompatible with real-time robotic control. We present Fast Legendre-polynomial Action policy via Sparse History-anchored flow (FLASH Policy), which replaces discrete action-chunk generation with continuous Legendre polynomial trajectory representation. Specifically, by fitting expert demonstrations under sparse temporal sampling, FLASH enables a single inference to cover a significantly extended action horizon. To further accelerate generation, FLASH initiates the flow matching process from history polynomial coefficients rather than uninformative Gaussian noise, shortening the transport distance and enabling accurate single-step inference. Moreover, analytic polynomial differentiation directly provides desired velocity feed-forward signals to the torque controller without numerical approximation. Extensive experiments on five simulated and two real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate that FLASH achieves state-of-the-art success rates ($\ge 92\%$ across all tasks), a per-episode inference time of $31.40\,ms$ (up to $175\times$ faster than diffusion policies and $18\times$ faster than prior flow matching policies), up to $4\times$ faster training convergence than ACT, and $5\times$ to $7\times$ reduction in controller tracking error compared to discrete-action baselines.

</details>


### [307] [LAPS: Improving Incremental LiDAR Mapping using Active Pooling and Sampling for Neural Distance Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15496)
*Dongjae Lee,Wooseong Yang,Yifu Tao,Maurice Fallon,Ayoung Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LAPS is a replay management framework for incremental neural mapping using LiDAR that combines active pooling (retaining reliable historical samples) and active sampling (focusing on under-constrained regions) to combat catastrophic forgetting, achieving better reconstruction completeness and accuracy than prior methods like PIN-SLAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural distance fields suffer from catastrophic forgetting during incremental LiDAR mapping, where new observations degrade previously reconstructed geometry. Existing replay methods use passive buffers and uniform sampling, wasting memory on redundant data and under-training poorly constrained regions.

Method: LAPS combines two components: (1) reliability-based active pooling to retain reliable historical samples under limited memory, and (2) uncertainty-guided active sampling to focus optimization on under-constrained regions during online updates.

Result: On the Oxford Spires dataset (Blenheim Palace 05 sequence), LAPS improves recall by 4.66 pp and F1-score by 3.79 pp over PIN-SLAM. It consistently improves reconstruction completeness while maintaining geometric accuracy across synthetic and real-world benchmarks.

Conclusion: Active replay management (active pooling + active sampling) effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting in incremental neural mapping, outperforming passive replay strategies. Open-source implementation is released.

Abstract: Neural distance fields offer a compact and continuous representation of 3D geometry, making them attractive for incremental LiDAR mapping. However, their online optimization is vulnerable to catastrophic forgetting, where new observations can degrade previously reconstructed geometry. Replay-based training is commonly used to address this issue, but existing methods typically rely on passive replay buffers and uniform sampling, which can waste memory on redundant observations and under-train poorly constrained regions. We propose LAPS, a replay management framework for incremental neural mapping that improves both replay retention and replay allocation during online updates. LAPS combines reliability-based active pooling to retain reliable historical samples under limited memory with uncertainty-guided active sampling to focus optimization on under-constrained regions. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show that LAPS consistently improves reconstruction completeness while maintaining competitive geometric accuracy. On Oxford Spires, it improves recall by 4.66 pp and F1-score by 3.79 pp over PIN-SLAM on the Blenheim Palace 05 sequence. We release our open source implementation at: https://github.com/dongjae0107/LAPS.

</details>


### [308] [A QUBO Formulation Framework for Kinematic Structure-Based Robot Design Optimization: A Robotic Hand Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15510)
*HyoJae Kang,Yeong Jae Park,Jeongdo Ahn,Dongil Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a QUBO-based framework for robot design optimization, using a robotic hand case study with 27 variables, validated via simulated annealing and quantum annealing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a generalized framework that transforms kinematic structure-based robot design optimization into combinatorial optimization problems compatible with quantum annealing.

Method: A QUBO formulation integrating individual design rewards, overlap workspace interactions, one-hot constraints, and structural dependency penalties. Simulated annealing as classical baseline, then quantum annealing execution.

Result: Feasible design combinations satisfying both one-hot and pairwise constraints were obtained; objective-value range narrowed with increased reads.

Conclusion: The proposed framework generalizes robot design problems into combinatorial optimization solvable via quantum annealing.

Abstract: This paper presents a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization-based formulation framework for robot design optimization using kinematic structure-level evaluation metrics. In the proposed framework, classical computation is used to evaluate design-dependent metrics while the resulting combinatorial selection problem is formulated in a structure compatible with quantum annealing-based optimization. A robotic hand is adopted as a representative case study, as its performance is determined by both the individual kinematic characteristics of each finger and interaction terms. The proposed formulation incorporates individual design rewards, overlap workspace interactions, one-hot constraint, and structural dependency penalties into a unified quadratic model. A 27-variable robotic hand design problem is constructed, and simulated annealing is used as a classical baseline to verify the feasibility of the formulation. Quantum annealing is further performed to examine the applicability of the proposed formulation to annealing-based hardware execution. The results show that feasible design combinations satisfying both one-hot selection and pairwise constraints can be obtained, with the observed objective-value range becoming narrower as the number of reads increases. In addition, the formulation process is discussed for other robotic systems. The proposed framework provides a generalized approach for transforming kinematic structure-based robot design problems into combinatorial optimization problems.

</details>


### [309] [Terrain Consistent Reference-Guided RL for Humanoid Navigation Autonomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15517)
*William D. Compton,Zachary Olkin,Aaron D. Ames*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A method for training humanoid locomotion policies that modulates reference trajectories to match terrain geometry, enabling SE(2)-controllable policies deployable with standard navigation planners, demonstrated on real hardware (Unitree G1) for long-horizon outdoor navigation over rough terrain and stairs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable humanoid robots to navigate complex, unstructured outdoor environments autonomously by bridging the gap between reference-guided RL training and real-world terrain variability.

Method: Reference trajectories are modulated during RL training to be consistent with terrain geometry by projecting footsteps onto valid footholds and adjusting swing-foot/CoM trajectories inside the training loop. The policy exposes an SE(2) velocity interface compatible with standard navigation planners.

Result: Environmentally-conditioned references significantly improve tracking performance over agnostic ones in simulation. On hardware, the policy enables >70m closed-loop autonomous navigation on Unitree G1 over rough terrain and stairs with onboard sensing and computation.

Conclusion: Terrain-modulated reference trajectories within RL training produce robust locomotion policies that can be integrated with existing navigation planners (MPC+CBF) for real-world autonomous humanoid navigation.

Abstract: We present a method for training reference-guided, perceptive reinforcement learning locomotion policies for humanoid robots in which reference trajectories are modulated in training to be consistent with terrain geometry. Aiming to deploy our method with standard navigation autonomy infrastructure, we synthesize SE(2)-controllable reference trajectories inside the RL training loop, projecting desired footsteps onto valid footholds and adjusting swing-foot and center-of-mass trajectories to match the terrain. The resulting policy exposes a clean SE(2) velocity interface compatible with standard navigation planners. In simulation, environmentally-conditioned references significantly improve reference tracking performance compared to environment agnostic references. On hardware, we integrate the policy with an MPC + control barrier function planner and demonstrate long-horizon (>70m) closed-loop autonomous navigation on the Unitree G1 through outdoor environments containing rough terrain and consecutive flights of stairs, with all sensing and computation onboard.

</details>


### [310] [Task-Semantic Graph-Driven Distributed Agent Networking for Underwater Target Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15528)
*Shengchao Zhu,Guangjie Han,Chuan Lin,Yu He*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces an open-source MARL-AUV platform integrating DI-engine with a 6-DOF underwater target-tracking simulator, and proposes STG-MAPPO, a semantic task graph-enhanced MARL algorithm for distributed AUV swarm target tracking under acoustic constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MARL studies for AUV swarms lack a unified open-source platform that evaluates different algorithms under realistic 6-DOF AUV dynamics, and policies trained on raw geometric states struggle to represent task phases, observation reliability, link quality, and local cooperation roles.

Method: The authors built an open-source MARL-AUV platform connecting DI-engine with a physical 6-DOF underwater simulator, and proposed STG-MAPPO which uses semantic policy inputs (tracking diagnostics, task phases, observation confidence, link quality, etc.), a compact semantic task graph linking communication-constrained states to decentralized actions, and velocity-level action abstraction for executable 6-DOF control.

Result: The platform is the first open platform connecting a public MARL training framework with physically modeled AUV swarm tasks, providing unified experimental protocols for fair comparison of RL/MARL algorithms; STG-MAPPO enhances policy representation through semantic inputs.

Conclusion: The proposed platform and STG-MAPPO address the gap in standardized MARL evaluation for AUV swarms, and the semantic task graph approach improves policy performance in communication-constrained underwater tracking scenarios.

Abstract: Autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) swarms are emerging as intelligent underwater networks, where each node must sense, communicate, process local data, and make decisions under severe acoustic constraints. Persistent underwater target tracking is a typical task with moving targets, changing communication topology, intermittent acoustic links, and limited observation for each AUV. Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) is a natural candidate for distributed tracking, yet existing studies still lack a unified open-source platform for evaluating different MARL algorithms under six-degree-of-freedom AUV dynamics. In addition, policies trained with raw geometric states and low-level force actions often struggle to represent task phases, observation reliability, link quality, and local cooperation roles. This paper addresses these issues by developing an open-source MARL-AUV platform that integrates DI-engine with a six-degree-of-freedom underwater AUV target-tracking simulator. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first open platform that connects a public MARL training framework with physically modeled AUV swarm-based tasks, and provides a unified experimental protocol for fair training, testing, and comparison of representative RL and MARL algorithms. Based on this platform, we propose STG-MAPPO, a Semantic Task Graph-enhanced variant of Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization. STG-MAPPO builds semantic policy inputs from tracking diagnostics, task phases, observation confidence, link availability, neighbor tracking quality, and local role advantage. A compact semantic task graph links communication-constrained network states to decentralized actor decisions, and a velocity-level action abstraction maps high-level cooperative decisions to executable six-degree-offreedom AUV control inputs.The code is available at https://github.com/dasjsaj/MARL-AUV.

</details>


### [311] [SkiP: When to Skip and When to Refine for Efficient Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15536)
*Mingtong Dai,Guanqi Peng,Yongjie Bai,Feng Yan,Chunjie Chen,Lingbo Liu,Liang Lin,Xinyu Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SkiP proposes an action relabeling mechanism that allows policies to skip redundant steps in manipulation tasks, automatically partitioning demonstrations into key/skip segments via Motion Spectrum Keying, achieving 15-40% execution reduction with maintained or improved success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing imitation learning policies treat all control steps uniformly, wasting computation on free-space motions while lacking resolution on critical contact-rich steps. The goal is to dynamically skip redundant steps and focus on key moments.

Method: (1) Action relabeling: replace behavior cloning target in skip segments with the action at the entrance of the next key segment. (2) Skip Policy (SkiP): a unified network that leaps over skip segments and refines actions in key segments. (3) Motion Spectrum Keying (MSK): automatic, task-agnostic detection of local motion complexity to partition demonstrations.

Result: Across 72 simulated and 3 real-robot tasks, SkiP reduces executed steps by 15-40% while matching or improving success rates across various policy backbones.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively eliminates wasteful prediction steps in manipulation without needing explicit skip planners or hierarchical structures, achieving efficiency gains without performance loss.

Abstract: Previous imitation learning policies predict future actions at every control step, whether in smooth motion phases or precise, contact-rich operation phases. This uniform treatment is wasteful: most steps in a manipulation trajectory traverse free space and carry little task-relevant information, while a small fraction of \emph{key} steps around contacts, grasps, and alignment demand dense, high-resolution prediction. We propose a novel \emph{action relabeling} mechanism: at each timestep in a skip segment, we replace the behavior cloning target with the action at the entrance of the next key segment, enabling the policy to leap over redundant steps in a single decision. The resulting \textbf{Skip Policy (SkiP)} dynamically leaps over skip segments and intensively refines actions in key segments, within a single unified network requiring no learned skip planner or hierarchical structure. To automatically partition demonstrations into key and skip segments without manual annotation, we introduce \emph{Motion Spectrum Keying} (MSK), a fast, task-agnostic procedure that detects local motion complexity from action signals. Extensive experiments across 72 simulated manipulation tasks and three real-robot tasks show that SkiP reduces executed steps by $15$--$40\%$ while matching or improving success rates across various policy backbones. Project page: \texttt{https://pgq18.github.io/SkiP-page/}.

</details>


### [312] [KaRMA: A Kinematic Metric for Fine Manipulation Ability in Robotic Hands](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15548)
*Martin Peticco,Pulkit Agrawal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KaRMA is a new kinematic metric that measures a robotic hand's ability to continuously translate and rotate a grasped object in-hand via rolling motions, evaluated on 16 hands, revealing tradeoffs invisible to static metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robotic hand metrics (workspace, manipulability, grasp stability) are static and do not capture dexterity as defined in manipulation: the ability to continuously change an object's pose within the hand while maintaining contact.

Method: KaRMA quantifies reachable in-hand translation and reorientation of a spherical object in a two-finger precision pinch, using a breadth-first search over translation/rotation primitives while enforcing joint limits, collisions, rolling contact, and antipodal force feasibility. Outputs three scores: KaRMA-T (translational coverage), KaRMA-R (rotational coverage), KaRMA-S (initial grasp sensitivity).

Result: Evaluated on 16 robotic hands; KaRMA separates hands that rank identically under static proxies, reveals translation-rotation tradeoffs invisible to existing baselines, and aligns with published task benchmarks where Jacobian-based metrics are misleading.

Conclusion: KaRMA provides a more informative, kinematic-only evaluation of in-hand dexterity than static metrics, highlighting performance tradeoffs and better matching empirical task performance.

Abstract: Traditional robotic hand metrics focus on static properties such as workspace, manipulability, and grasp stability. However, these metrics do not directly measure dexterity under the standard definition in robotic manipulation: the ability to continuously change an object's pose within the hand while maintaining contact from an initial grasp. We introduce Kinematic Rolling Manipulation Ability (KaRMA), a kinematic-only metric for fine manipulation that quantifies reachable in-hand translation and reorientation of a spherical test object within a two-finger precision pinch through feasible rolling motions. KaRMA enforces joint limits, collision constraints, rolling contact, and antipodal force feasibility, then investigates reachable in-hand object poses via breadth-first search over translation and rotation primitives. KaRMA reports three scores: translational coverage (KaRMA-T), rotational coverage (KaRMA-R), and sensitivity to the initial grasp (KaRMA-S). We evaluate KaRMA on 16 widely used robotic hands and compare against static baselines, showing that KaRMA separates hands that rank identically under static proxies, reveals translation-rotation tradeoffs invisible to existing baselines, and is qualitatively consistent with selected published task benchmarks where Jacobian-based metrics can be misleading.

</details>


### [313] [NavRL++: A System-Level Framework for Improving Sim-to-Real Transfer in Reinforcement Learning-Based Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15559)
*Zhefan Xu,Hanyu Jin,Kenji Shimada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a systematic study of sim-to-real transfer for RL-based navigation, introduces perturbation-aware fine-tuning and a Transformer-based temporal reasoning policy, and demonstrates zero-shot transfer on aerial and legged robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL navigation research focuses on framework design but lacks analysis of sim-to-real transfer factors and how training strategies affect real-world deployment.

Method: (1) Systematic empirical study isolating key sim-to-real factors (sensor noise, perception failures, latency, control response). (2) Perturbation-aware fine-tuning for post-training adaptation. (3) Transformer-based temporal reasoning policy using short-horizon observations.

Result: Outperforms learning-based baselines in static/dynamic environments; comparable to optimization-based planners in static settings; validated zero-shot on aerial and legged robots for exploration and inspection.

Conclusion: Understanding and explicitly modeling domain discrepancies (via perturbation-aware fine-tuning and temporal reasoning) significantly improves sim-to-real transfer robustness in RL navigation.

Abstract: Recent years have witnessed significant progress in autonomous navigation using reinforcement learning. However, existing approaches largely emphasize reinforcement learning framework design, such as input representations, action spaces, and reward functions, while providing limited analysis of sim-to-real transfer and insufficient insight into how training strategies affect real-world deployment performance. To bridge this gap, we not only introduce an effective RL framework but also present a complete training and deployment pipeline, along with a systematic empirical study that disentangles the key factors affecting sim-to-real transfer in reinforcement learning-based navigation, including sensor noise, perception failures, system latency, and control response. Building on insights from this analysis, we introduce perturbation-aware fine-tuning, a post-training adaptation strategy that improves transfer robustness by explicitly accounting for empirically identified domain discrepancies. To further mitigate perception degradation and enhance control smoothness in real-world deployment, we propose a Transformer-based temporal reasoning policy that leverages short-horizon observation for navigation control. We quantitatively evaluate how individual sim-to-real perturbations and training design choices impact navigation performance across environments. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed training strategy and policy architecture outperform learning-based baselines in both static and dynamic environments, while achieving performance comparable to optimization-based planners in static settings. We validate our approach through real-world deployment on multiple robotic platforms, including aerial and legged robots, across navigation-centric tasks such as exploration and inspection, demonstrating zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.

</details>


### [314] [Wind-Aware Optimal Trajectory Planning for Efficient Gliding of Fixed-Wing Aerial Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15619)
*Luca Morando,Nishanth Bobbili,Giuseppe Loianno*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a nonlinear trajectory planner for small fixed-wing UAV gliders that uses Bernstein polynomials, differential flatness, and online re-planning to achieve energy-efficient gliding under wind disturbances and obstacle constraints, validated through CFD simulations and real-world flight tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gliding offers extended endurance and silent operation for small UAVs but requires accurate energy management under wind disturbances and obstacles; traditional TECS-based controllers reactively regulate potential/kinetic energy trade-offs and require fine-tuning.

Method: A nonlinear multi-cost trajectory planner generating C^3 continuous Bernstein polynomial trajectories, mapped to control via differential flatness, re-planned online using sink polar curves. A simulated netto variometer estimates air mass motion; Dubins path-based cruising segments connect gliding phases for hybrid missions.

Result: Validated in CFD simulations and real-world experiments, showing reliable stabilization of sink rate, airspeed, and glide ratio under wind gusts and obstacle constraints.

Conclusion: Shifting energy management from reactive control to planning level improves gliding performance under disturbances and obstacles, enabling robust hybrid powered/unpowered UAV missions.

Abstract: Gliding offers small fixed-wing UAVs extended endurance and silent operation but requires accurate energy management, especially under wind disturbances and obstacle constraints. Traditional Total Energy Control Systems based controllers regulate the trade between potential and kinetic energy reactively, often requiring fine-tuning and trim-conditions knowledge. In this work, we shift the regulation to the planning level and present a nonlinear, multi-cost trajectory planner for small UAV gliders. The method generates $\mathcal{C}^3$ continuous trajectories based on Bernstein polynomials, mapped into control commands through differential flatness, and re-planned online to match experimentally derived sink polar curves. A simulated netto variometer is integrated into the optimization to estimate air mass motion, constraining the glide to energy-balanced states. Consecutive gliding trajectories are linked by cruising segments computed through trajectories initialized on Dubins path-based waypoints, enabling hybrid missions that combine powered and unpowered flight. The approach is validated in CFD simulations and real-world experiments with a fixed-wing platform, showing reliable stabilization of sink rate, airspeed, and glide ratio under wind gusts and in presence of obstacles.

</details>


### [315] [Propagating Unsafe Actions in LLM Controlled Multi-Robot Collaboration via Single Robot Compromise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15641)
*Zhen Huang,Zhihuang Liu,Weishang Wu,Zhiping Cai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes InfectBot, a novel attack paradigm for multi-robot systems where a single compromised LLM-controlled robot propagates malicious instructions via inter-robot communication, achieving rapid system-wide compromise (obedience 1.00, infectiousness 0.90) with high efficiency (3 rounds, stealth 0.81).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work studied security threats in single-robot LLM planners, but security risks in LLM-controlled multi-robot collaboration (especially those propagated through inter-robot communication) remain largely unexplored.

Method: The authors propose an attack paradigm where the adversary interacts with only a single entry robot, which then propagates malicious intent through peer communication to coordinate unsafe actions across the entire multi-robot system.

Result: Obedience reaches 1.00 in the strongest cases, infectiousness rises to 0.90, attacks require as few as 3.0 rounds to compromise all robots, and stealthiness score is 0.81. Risks amplify when robots resolve trade-offs in critical situations (emergencies or rights conflicts).

Conclusion: There is a persistent safety alignment gap in multi-robot LLM planners. The coordination mechanism can unintentionally allow adversarial instructions to override safety requirements, especially in critical scenarios.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as general planners in embodied intelligence, enabling high level coordination and low level task planning for both single robot and multi-robot collaboration. This increasing reliance on embodied LLM planners also raises critical security concerns, since misaligned or manipulated instructions can be translated into physical actions. Prior work has studied such threats in single robot settings, while security risks in LLM controlled multi-robot collaboration, especially those propagated through inter robot communication, remain largely unexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel attack paradigm for multi-robot system in which the adversary interacts with only a single entry robot. The compromised robot then propagates malicious intent through peer communication, leading to coordinated unsafe actions across the system. Our evaluation, covering high risk dimensions of dereliction of duty, privacy compromise, and public safety hazards, reveals a persistent safety alignment gap in multi-robot planners. We quantify this process with three metrics, obedience, infectiousness, and stealthiness. Experiments demonstrate both persistent attacker control and rapid propagation: obedience reaches 1.00 in the strongest cases, and infectiousness rises to 0.90. Notably, the attack is highly efficient, requiring as few as 3.0 rounds to compromise all the robots while maintaining a stealthiness score of 0.81. Such risks are amplified when robots must resolve trade offs in critical situations, such as emergencies or conflicts of rights, because the coordination mechanism can unintentionally allow adversarial instructions to override safety requirements. The code is available at https://github.com/TheFatInsect/InfectBot.

</details>


### [316] [MyoChallenge 2025: A New Benchmark for Human Athletic Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15650)
*Cheryl Wang,Chun Kwang Tan,Balint K. Hodossy,Eric Lyu,Jun Guo,Wentao Zhao,Huaping Liu,Chengkun Li,Merkourios Simos,Bianca Ziliotto,Alexander Mathis,Siyuan Liu,Jiahao Chen,Shanlin Zhong,Bo Jiang,Ci Song,Yaoye Zhu,Chenhui Zuo,Yanan Sui,Mohamed Irfan Refai,Massimo Sartori,Guillaume Durandau,Vikash Kumar,Vittorio Caggiano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MyoChallenge at NeurIPS 2025 is a benchmark competition for motor control intelligence in sports, using high-fidelity musculoskeletal models in physics simulation. It features two tracks (table tennis and soccer) and attracted nearly 70 teams and 560+ submissions, advancing state-of-the-art control algorithms bridging machine learning, biomechanics, sports science, and neuroscience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Athletic performance in AI/robotics is hard to replicate, and understanding biological muscle coordination is limited by in vivo measurement constraints. MyoChallenge aims to bridge this gap by providing a reproducible, physiologically realistic simulated benchmark.

Method: The competition uses high-fidelity musculoskeletal models within physics simulation (MyoSuite framework) across two tracks: (1) upper limb control for table tennis rally, (2) lower limb control for soccer penalty kick. It leverages machine learning techniques like physics-based motion planners, on-policy behavior cloning, hierarchical planning, and muscle synergies.

Result: Nearly 70 teams and over 560 submissions globally participated, uniting physicians, neuroscientists, and ML experts. Several state-of-the-art control algorithms for musculoskeletal sports agility were developed.

Conclusion: MyoChallenge'25 serves as a reproducible, reusable testbed accelerating interdisciplinary research across ML, biomechanics, sports science, and neuroscience, integrating standardized tasks and physiologically realistic models into the open-source MyoSuite framework.

Abstract: Athletic performance represents the pinnacle of human motor intelligence, demanding rapid choices, precise control, agility, and coordinated physical execution. Replicating this seamless combination of capabilities remains elusive in current artificial intelligence and robotic systems. Concurrently, understanding the biological mastery of these movements is hindered because complex muscle coordination is rarely measured in vivo due to the limitations of physical equipment. To bridge this fundamental gap in understanding, MyoChallenge at NeurIPS 2025 established a pioneering benchmark for motor control intelligence in sports, leveraging high-fidelity musculoskeletal models within physics simulation combined with machine learning-driven algorithms. The competition introduces two distinct tracks emphasizing either upper or lower limbs control: a table tennis rally task utilizing a biomechanic upper limb composed of an arm with a hand and a trunk; and a soccer penalty kick using a biomechanic model of legs and a trunk. Marking the fourth iteration of the MyoChallenge series, this event attracted almost 70 teams and over 560 submissions globally, uniting a diverse community ranging from physicians and neuroscientists to machine learning experts. The competition facilitated the development of several state-of-the-art control algorithms for a musculoskeletal system capable of sports agility, leveraging techniques such as physics-based motion planners, on-policy behaviour cloning, hierarchical planning, and muscle synergies. By integrating standardized tasks and physiologically realistic models into the open-source framework of MyoSuite, MyoChallenge'25 serves as a reproducible and reusable testbed to accelerate interdisciplinary research across machine learning, biomechanics, sports science, and neuroscience. Project page: https://www.myosuite.org//myochallenge/myochallenge-2025.

</details>


### [317] [PCASim: Promptable Closed-loop Adversarial Simulation for Urban Traffic Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15654)
*Chuancheng Zhang,Zhenhao Wang,Kaizheng Li,Yaran Lin,Qiang Guo,Bin Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a framework integrating adversarial scenario generation with safety agent training using LLMs and reinforcement learning, achieving improved scenario diversity and safety performance in autonomous driving testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing studies lack integration of adversarial scenario generation with safety agent training in closed-loop testing, preventing efficient co-evolution and mutual enhancement of both components for real-world autonomous driving safety.

Method: Constructs an adversarial behavior knowledge repository via rule-based filtering on open-source data, uses LLMs to integrate knowledge-, data-, and adversarial-driven approaches, and employs reinforcement learning to train diverse vehicle behaviors for scenario generation.

Result: Domain-specific language generation accuracy improved by 12%, scenario transformation success rate increased by 8%, and obstacle-avoidance capability enhanced by 30%.

Conclusion: The framework effectively co-evolves adversarial scenario generation and safety agent training, enhancing both scenario diversity and safety performance in autonomous driving testing.

Abstract: Real-world autonomous driving, particularly in urban environments with numerous corner cases, requires rigorous testing to ensure product safety and robustness. However, few studies have explored integrating adversarial scenario generation with the training of safety agents in closed-loop testing, enabling efficient co-evolution and mutual enhancement of both. To address this challenge, an adversarial behavior knowledge repository is constructed by applying rule-based filtering to an open-source dataset, combined with knowledge retrieval modules tailored for simulation environments. A large language model (LLM) is employed to integrate knowledge-, data-, and adversarial-driven approaches, generating safety-critical traffic scenarios customized to user needs. Additionally, while evaluating the generated scenarios, we employ reinforcement learning models to train the behaviors of different types of vehicles, thereby enriching scenario diversity beyond existing datasets while preserving realism. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework improves the accuracy of domain-specific language generation by 12\%. Moreover, the success rate of newly generated scenario transformations increases by 8\%, while obstacle-avoidance capability is enhanced by 30\%. For the complete manuscript, please refer to: https://zhenhaooo.github.io/PCASim.github.io/

</details>


### [318] [Feedback World Model Enables Precise Guidance of Diffusion Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15705)
*Tuo An,Jindou Jia,Gen Li,Jingliang Li,Chuhao Zhou,Pengfei Liu,Bofan Lyu,Jiaqi Bai,Xinying Guo,Geng Li,Jianfei Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a feedback world model that corrects predictions online using real-time observations at inference time, reducing prediction error by up to 76.4% and improving out-of-distribution success rate by 30% in robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World models for robotic decision making become unreliable under distribution shift at deployment because they are used as static open-loop predictors. The key insight is that execution provides a natural signal (the true next state after each action) that is underutilized for correcting predictions.

Method: The feedback world model maintains a lightweight feedback state that is updated online at inference time to iteratively correct future predictions using real-time observations. It acts as a latent-space observer with convergence guarantees under mild conditions. Action-aware guidance is also introduced to emphasize action-controllable components while suppressing irrelevant variations.

Result: On LIBERO-Plus, Robomimic, and real-world manipulation tasks: reduces world model prediction error by up to 76.4%, improves OOD success rate by 30%.

Conclusion: Incorporating real-time feedback at inference time provides a simple yet powerful alternative to static world modeling, offering substantial improvements in both prediction accuracy and policy performance under distribution shift without requiring additional training data or parameter updates.

Abstract: World models aim to improve robotic decision making by predicting the consequences of actions. However, in practice, their predictions often become unreliable once the robot encounters states outside the training distribution, limiting their effectiveness at deployment. We observe that execution itself provides a natural but underutilized signal: after each action, the robot directly observes the true next state, revealing the mismatch between predicted and actual outcomes. Building on this insight, we propose feedback world model, a new paradigm that closes the loop between prediction and observation at inference time. Instead of treating the world model as a static open-loop predictor, our method maintains a lightweight feedback state that is updated online to iteratively correct future predictions, compensating for model errors using real-time observations without additional training data or parameter updates. We show that this process can be interpreted as a latent-space observer and admits convergence guarantees under mild conditions. We further introduce action-aware guidance to better translate corrected predictions into control by emphasizing action-controllable components while suppressing irrelevant variations. Experiments on LIBERO-Plus, Robomimic, and real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate that our method substantially improves both prediction accuracy and policy performance under distribution shift. In particular, it reduces world model prediction error by up to 76.4% and improves out-of-distribution (OOD) success rate by 30%. These results show that incorporating real-time feedback at inference time provides a simple yet powerful alternative to static world modeling.

</details>


### [319] [Learning Dynamic Pick-and-Place for a Legged Manipulator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15713)
*Moonkyu Jung,Jiseong Lee,Zhengmao He,Donghoon Youm,Juhyeok Mun,HyeongJun Kim,Hyunsik Oh,Donghyuk Choi,Jungwoo Hur,Jie Song,Jemin Hwangbo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical RL framework for quadrupedal mobile manipulators enables dynamic pick-and-place with heavier payloads (up to 2.3 kg in simulation, 1.3 kg in real-world) and extended workspaces (ground to 1.1m), achieving 86.05% simulation and 73.3% real-world success rates through adaptive whole-body control with explicit mass estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Legged manipulators face the challenge of achieving precise manipulation while maintaining coordinated locomotion, and prior works are limited to handling lightweight objects with slow, piecewise motions.

Method: A hierarchical reinforcement learning framework with an explicit mass estimation module for adaptive whole-body control, enabling concurrent locomotion and manipulation for dynamic continuous execution.

Result: 86.05% success rate in simulation (2.3 kg payloads); 73.3% success rate in real-world (1.3 kg payloads) across six scenarios with varying object properties and task heights, average execution time of 4.06 s.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates the potential of quadrupedal mobile manipulators for adaptive, whole-body pick-and-place with heavier payloads and extended workspaces, outperforming prior slow piecewise methods.

Abstract: Legged manipulators extend robotic capabilities beyond static manipulation by integrating agile locomotion with versatile arm control. However, achieving precise manipulation while maintaining coordinated locomotion remains a major challenge. This work presents a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework for dynamic pick-and-place tasks using a quadruped equipped with a 6-DOF robotic arm. The framework incorporates an explicit mass estimation module enabling adaptive whole-body control for objects with varying weights. In simulation, the system achieves an 86.05% success rate with payloads up to 2.3 kg. The approach is further validated through real-world experiments across six representative scenarios with controlled variations in object physical properties (size and mass) and task heights.
